message.content: read ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/agentrail-rs to see how to set this project up to use that tool.  Create a saga from reading docs/research.txt.  Note that the Pascal compiler is a separate project.   The scope of this project is to create a VM for COR24 ISA using ~/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs cor24-bin as the assembler and emulator.  To test this VM, this project needs to implement an Assembler for the target p-code (not to be confused with the cor24 isa .s assembler).  This assembler can be writte in cor24 assembler.  This assembler is used to create tests for the VM.  come up with docs/{architecture,prd,design} and use agentrail to define the steps in the saga.  Questions?  Look at ~/github/sw-cli-tools/tf24a for how to build using cor24 assembler.

1→
     2→
     3→
     4→
     5→How would I implement Pascal from scratch for a custom 24-bit ISA?  I have a working C compiler and Assembler (and emulator and FPGA hardware).  Was Pascal bootstapped in assembler or C and then written in Pascal?  Are there open source implementations with liberal licenses (BSD, MIT) that I can look at?  I do not want to port from a 16-bit or 32-bit system.  I prefer to write-from-scratch, clean-room implementation, possibly using already-written Pascal souce examples for testing my compiler.  I also want a debugger.  How should I start?  
     6→
     7→
     8→Thought for 36s
     9→Given your constraints, I would not start by trying to build a self-hosting Pascal compiler immediately.
    10→
    11→You already have the right base:
    12→
    13→working assembler
    14→
    15→working C compiler
    16→
    17→emulator + FPGA target
    18→
    19→desire for a clean-room implementation
    20→
    21→debugger requirement from day one
    22→
    23→That points to a practical architecture:
    24→
    25→Recommended architecture
    26→Build Pascal in three stages:
    27→
    28→Pascal front end in C
    29→
    30→lexer
    31→
    32→parser
    33→
    34→AST
    35→
    36→symbol tables
    37→
    38→type checker
    39→
    40→simple error recovery
    41→
    42→Portable intermediate form
    43→
    44→either a small stack VM / p-code
    45→
    46→or a very small typed IR of your own
    47→
    48→Back ends
    49→
    50→first: interpreter for the IR/p-code on the host
    51→
    52→second: native code generator for your 24-bit ISA
    53→
    54→later: optional self-hosting compiler in Pascal
    55→
    56→That is the lowest-risk path because it lets you validate semantics before you fight register allocation, calling conventions, and codegen bugs.
    57→
    58→Historically, how Pascal was bootstrapped
    59→The earliest Pascal compiler was not originally bootstrapped in assembler in the way many people assume. Niklaus Wirth’s first FORTRAN-based attempt was unsuccessful; the successful implementation was done in a C-like language called Scallop and then translated by hand into Pascal for bootstrapping. Many later Pascal compilers became self-hosting. A famous exception is GNU Pascal, which was written in C. Turbo Pascal, much later, was famously written in assembly for speed and size. 
    60→
    61→So the historical pattern is roughly:
    62→
    63→prototype in another language
    64→
    65→get a working compiler
    66→
    67→rewrite or re-express the compiler in Pascal
    68→
    69→use the previous compiler to compile the new compiler
    70→
    71→That is exactly the path I would recommend for your ISA as well.
    72→
    73→What to target first: native code or p-code?
    74→For your situation, I would start with a Pascal-S / Pascal-P style subset and a p-code-like intermediate machine.
    75→
    76→Why:
    77→
    78→Pascal semantics are nontrivial enough already: nested procedures, static scoping, sets, records, packed/unpacked data, file types, pass-by-reference, variant records, etc.
    79→
    80→your ISA is 24-bit, which means you will be making many nonstandard representation decisions anyway
    81→
    82→a p-code interpreter gives you a correctness oracle before native codegen
    83→
    84→Historically, Pascal-P was created specifically as a compiler porting kit: Pascal source compiled to p-code for a virtual stack machine, then interpreted on each target. That was done to make Pascal portable. 
    85→
    86→For a custom ISA, that strategy is still excellent.
    87→
    88→What subset should you implement first
    89→Do not begin with full ISO Pascal.
    90→
    91→Begin with a clean educational subset:
    92→
    93→Phase 0: expression language
    94→integers only
    95→
    96→constants
    97→
    98→variables
    99→
   100→assignment
   101→
   102→arithmetic
   103→
   104→comparisons
   105→
   106→if, while
   107→
   108→begin ... end
   109→
   110→writeln-like builtin for debugging
   111→
   112→Phase 1: real Pascal block structure
   113→program
   114→
   115→declarations
   116→
   117→scalar types: integer, boolean, char
   118→
   119→arrays with fixed bounds
   120→
   121→records
   122→
   123→procedures/functions
   124→
   125→lexical nesting
   126→
   127→local variables
   128→
   129→pass-by-value and var parameters
   130→
   131→Phase 2: features that force runtime design
   132→strings or packed char arrays
   133→
   134→sets
   135→
   136→pointers
   137→
   138→heap
   139→
   140→files
   141→
   142→case statements
   143→
   144→standard procedures/functions
   145→
   146→Phase 3: optional/full language
   147→ISO-ish cleanup
   148→
   149→conformance tests
   150→
   151→self-hosting subset
   152→
   153→For your first compiler, avoid:
   154→
   155→packed types
   156→
   157→files
   158→
   159→variant records
   160→
   161→conformant arrays
   162→
   163→procedural parameters
   164→
   165→goto
   166→
   167→real numbers
   168→
   169→Those features create a disproportionate amount of compiler/runtime complexity.
   170→
   171→Clean-room strategy
   172→Your instinct is good: use existing Pascal systems for ideas and tests, not for porting.
   173→
   174→A good clean-room posture is:
   175→
   176→read language reports and public docs
   177→
   178→study high-level architecture of older Pascal compilers
   179→
   180→do not copy implementation code
   181→
   182→write your own grammar, AST, type system, runtime, and codegen
   183→
   184→use existing Pascal example programs only as black-box test inputs
   185→
   186→That gives you educational value and keeps the implementation genuinely yours.
   187→
   188→Best implementation strategy for your toolchain
   189→Since you already have C and assembler, I would do this:
   190→
   191→Stage A — host-side compiler in C
   192→Run on your current host machine.
   193→
   194→Outputs either:
   195→
   196→textual p-code / bytecode
   197→
   198→or textual assembly for your 24-bit ISA
   199→
   200→This lets you iterate quickly.
   201→
   202→Stage B — target runtime
   203→On your ISA:
   204→
   205→tiny startup
   206→
   207→memory map
   208→
   209→stack/heap model
   210→
   211→runtime support calls
   212→
   213→optional p-code interpreter
   214→
   215→Stage C — native backend
   216→Replace or complement the p-code interpreter with native code generation.
   217→
   218→Stage D — self-hosting subset
   219→Once the compiler is stable enough, rewrite the front end in Pascal itself and compile it with your earlier compiler.
   220→
   221→IR choice: stack machine vs register IR
   222→For Pascal, a stack IR is the easiest starting point.
   223→
   224→Why:
   225→
   226→expressions map naturally
   227→
   228→nested scopes can be handled without solving global register allocation early
   229→
   230→procedure calls are simpler
   231→
   232→p-code history gives you many good precedents
   233→
   234→A tiny IR might look like:
   235→
   236→LDC k load constant
   237→
   238→LD level, offset
   239→
   240→ST level, offset
   241→
   242→ADD SUB MUL DIV
   243→
   244→EQ NE LT LE GT GE
   245→
   246→JMP label
   247→
   248→JZ label
   249→
   250→CALL proc_id
   251→
   252→RET
   253→
   254→ENTER locals_size
   255→
   256→LEAVE
   257→
   258→LDI STI for indirect access
   259→
   260→IDX for array indexing
   261→
   262→FIELD offset
   263→
   264→Then later map those to your 24-bit ISA.
   265→
   266→The hardest design decisions you need early
   267→These are the places where Pascal compilers get real:
   268→
   269→1. Lexical nesting and static links
   270→Pascal’s nested procedures are one of the biggest differences from C.
   271→
   272→You need a decision for nonlocal access:
   273→
   274→static link per frame
   275→
   276→or display
   277→
   278→or closure-like environment objects
   279→
   280→For a first implementation, use static links. They are simpler and faithful to classic Pascal.
   281→
   282→Each frame should contain:
   283→
   284→return address
   285→
   286→dynamic link
   287→
   288→static link
   289→
   290→locals
   291→
   292→temporaries / spills if needed
   293→
   294→2. Data layout on a 24-bit ISA
   295→Decide up front:
   296→
   297→size of integer
   298→
   299→size of pointer
   300→
   301→alignment rules
   302→
   303→representation of boolean
   304→
   305→char width
   306→
   307→array bound representation
   308→
   309→set representation
   310→
   311→If your ISA is naturally 24-bit, I would strongly consider:
   312→
   313→integer = signed 24-bit
   314→
   315→pointer = 24-bit
   316→
   317→boolean = 24-bit 0/1
   318→
   319→char = 8-bit, but stored with clear load/store conventions
   320→
   321→align aggregates to 24-bit word boundaries unless you have compelling byte-density reasons
   322→
   323→Packed representations can wait.
   324→
   325→3. Calling convention
   326→Define this before codegen:
   327→
   328→who pushes params
   329→
   330→who cleans stack
   331→
   332→how function result is returned
   333→
   334→where static link goes
   335→
   336→which registers are caller/callee saved
   337→
   338→how large structs/records are passed
   339→
   340→For simplicity:
   341→
   342→caller pushes arguments left-to-right or right-to-left, but pick one and freeze it
   343→
   344→caller also pushes static link
   345→
   346→callee builds frame
   347→
   348→return value in a dedicated register if scalar, hidden pointer if aggregate
   349→
   350→4. Runtime model
   351→You need a tiny Pascal runtime for:
   352→
   353→startup
   354→
   355→bounds-check failure
   356→
   357→nil deref failure
   358→
   359→division by zero
   360→
   361→heap alloc/free if pointers are supported
   362→
   363→I/O stubs
   364→
   365→exit / halt
   366→
   367→Keep it tiny and callable from generated code.
   368→
   369→Debugger: how to design it from the start
   370→You said you also want a debugger. Good. Add debug support immediately, not later.
   371→
   372→I would design the compiler around a symbol-rich listing/debug model.
   373→
   374→Emit these artifacts for every compile
   375→token dump
   376→
   377→AST dump
   378→
   379→symbol table dump
   380→
   381→frame layout report
   382→
   383→IR listing
   384→
   385→native assembly listing
   386→
   387→source line map
   388→
   389→procedure metadata
   390→
   391→Runtime debugger features
   392→At minimum:
   393→
   394→single-step by source line
   395→
   396→single-step by IR instruction
   397→
   398→breakpoints by source line
   399→
   400→show call stack
   401→
   402→show current procedure
   403→
   404→show locals/parameters
   405→
   406→inspect static chain
   407→
   408→inspect arbitrary memory
   409→
   410→inspect globals
   411→
   412→inspect heap blocks
   413→
   414→show array bounds and current indices
   415→
   416→trap on runtime errors
   417→
   418→Best UI split
   419→You already have Rust/Yew/WASM skills, so I would split it this way:
   420→
   421→compiler/debug metadata generator in C
   422→
   423→emulator instrumentation hooks in Rust or current emulator code
   424→
   425→browser debugger UI in Yew/WASM
   426→
   427→Views worth having:
   428→
   429→source pane
   430→
   431→current AST / scope pane
   432→
   433→call stack pane
   434→
   435→frame layout pane
   436→
   437→registers pane
   438→
   439→memory pane
   440→
   441→static-chain visualization
   442→
   443→disassembly pane
   444→
   445→IR pane
   446→
   447→For Pascal specifically, one killer feature is a scope-aware variable inspector:
   448→show locals, parameters, globals, and nonlocals via static-link traversal.
   449→
   450→Recommended project plan
   451→Milestone 1 — define the language subset
   452→Write these documents first:
   453→
   454→language.md
   455→
   456→runtime.md
   457→
   458→abi.md
   459→
   460→ir.md
   461→
   462→debug-format.md
   463→
   464→test-plan.md
   465→
   466→This matters more than code at the start.
   467→
   468→Milestone 2 — host interpreter for Pascal AST
   469→Before codegen, you can interpret the AST directly for a tiny subset.
   470→
   471→Pros:
   472→
   473→fastest way to validate parser + semantics
   474→
   475→easiest way to debug language rules
   476→
   477→Cons:
   478→
   479→not reusable for target execution
   480→
   481→Still, it is often worth doing for the first 2–3 milestones.
   482→
   483→Milestone 3 — p-code generator + host p-code VM
   484→This becomes your semantic reference implementation.
   485→
   486→Milestone 4 — target p-code VM on your 24-bit ISA
   487→Now real Pascal programs run on your emulator/FPGA without needing native codegen yet.
   488→
   489→Milestone 5 — native code generator
   490→Target your 24-bit ISA directly.
   491→
   492→Milestone 6 — debugger integration
   493→Hook source lines, symbols, frames, and memory inspection into the emulator UI.
   494→
   495→Milestone 7 — self-hosting subset
   496→Rewrite enough of the compiler in Pascal to compile itself.
   497→
   498→Open-source implementations worth studying
   499→You asked specifically about liberal licenses.
   500→
   501→Good for ideas / architecture
   502→Pascal-P / Pascal-P5 is historically important and directly relevant because it is a portable p-code Pascal family. The Pascal-P5 project is presented as the fifth version of the Zurich Pascal-P compiler and emphasizes ISO 7185 compliance. 
   503→
   504→Free Pascal is real, mature, and valuable to study conceptually, but its compiler itself is not BSD/MIT. Its FAQ says applications produced with its RTL are allowed to be proprietary because the RTL uses a modified LGPL exception, but that does not make the compiler source permissively licensed. 
   505→
   506→Stanford Pascal is interesting because it still uses p-code plus an interpreter, and the repo says the compiler files are Pascal/P-Code while the interpreter is in C. I did not find an explicit permissive license on the GitHub page I checked, so I would treat it as “study carefully, license unclear until verified.” 
   507→
   508→Small permissive educational repos
   509→These are useful for architecture ideas, not for production-grade reuse:
   510→
   511→JayPascal — MIT, Pascal compiler in C#. 
   512→
   513→XDPC — MIT-0, small educational Pascal compiler with native x86 codegen. Very relevant as a “small compiler that directly emits native code” example, even though it targets DOS/x86. 
   514→
   515→Qwerasdzxc/Pascal-Compiler — Apache-2.0, modular educational compiler with interpreter and C code generator. Architecturally useful because it separates lexer/parser/symbolizer/generator/runner. 
   516→
   517→One caution: many permissively licensed Pascal repos on GitHub are student or educational compilers, not robust reference implementations. They are good for structure, testing ideas, and scope control, but not for blindly trusting semantics.
   518→
   519→What I would personally avoid
   520→For your project, I would avoid:
   521→
   522→porting Turbo Pascal ideas too literally
   523→
   524→implementing full ISO Pascal before subset stability
   525→
   526→designing codegen before you define frame layout and static-scope access
   527→
   528→adding real numbers early
   529→
   530→building an optimizing compiler first
   531→
   532→trying to self-host too soon
   533→
   534→Suggested first deliverable
   535→Your first usable milestone should be:
   536→
   537→“Compile a small Pascal subset on the host into p-code, run it in a p-code VM on the emulator, and debug it with source-line stepping.”
   538→
   539→That gives you:
   540→
   541→proof of parsing
   542→
   543→proof of semantic analysis
   544→
   545→proof of runtime model
   546→
   547→proof of debugger architecture
   548→
   549→proof of target execution
   550→
   551→before native code generation complexity arrives.
   552→
   553→A concrete starting subset
   554→I would start with exactly this grammar surface:
   555→
   556→program
   557→
   558→const
   559→
   560→var
   561→
   562→scalar integer/boolean
   563→
   564→fixed arrays
   565→
   566→procedures with parameters
   567→
   568→function returning integer
   569→
   570→begin/end
   571→
   572→assignment
   573→
   574→if/then/else
   575→
   576→while/do
   577→
   578→relational operators
   579→
   580→arithmetic operators
   581→
   582→writeln builtin
   583→
   584→one-dimensional arrays only
   585→
   586→That is enough to validate:
   587→
   588→scopes
   589→
   590→frames
   591→
   592→calls
   593→
   594→control flow
   595→
   596→memory layout
   597→
   598→debugger variable display
   599→
   600→My recommendation in one sentence
   601→Start with a clean-room Pascal-S/Pascal-P style subset in C, targeting a small p-code VM first, build the debugger metadata and frame model from day one, and only then add a native 24-bit code generator and eventual self-hosting.
   602→
   603→If you want, I can turn this into a concrete architecture.md + milestone plan + test matrix + debugger metadata format tailored to your 24-bit ISA.
   604→
   605→
   606→So I want to split this project into layers.  A low-level p-code interpreter and possibly run-time.  How is memory typically managed in Pascal, malloc/free or gc or other?  Pascal would be the higher-level tooling to compile to the p-code.  Other languaged that compile to p-code would follow.
   607→
   608→
   609→Thought for 8s
   610→You want the project split into layers, with a low-level p-code machine/runtime first, and then Pascal as one front end targeting that machine, with room for other languages later. That is a good direction.
   611→
   612→It gives you:
   613→
   614→a stable execution substrate,
   615→
   616→a semantic reference model before native codegen,
   617→
   618→a reusable toolchain for multiple languages.
   619→
   620→The key design question now is the runtime model: stack, heap, globals, code, metadata, and how dynamic memory works.
   621→
   622→Short answer on Pascal memory management
   623→Classic Pascal was not generally garbage-collected. The usual model was:
   624→
   625→stack allocation for locals, parameters, temporaries
   626→
   627→static/global storage for globals
   628→
   629→heap allocation for dynamically created structures via new
   630→
   631→explicit reclamation, usually via dispose, or sometimes effectively “free everything at program end”
   632→
   633→That is still the cleanest model for your first implementation. Standard Pascal and mainstream descendants historically center on explicit heap management rather than GC. Free Pascal’s docs and wiki still document New/Dispose and GetMem/FreeMem style management, and its heap debugging support tracks allocation/deallocation rather than automatic tracing GC. 
   634→
   635→So for your first p-code runtime, I would recommend:
   636→
   637→no GC initially
   638→
   639→explicit heap operations
   640→
   641→optional region/arena reset at process end
   642→
   643→later, optional reference counting or tracing GC as a runtime extension for other languages
   644→
   645→Best layering for your project
   646→I would split the system like this:
   647→
   648→Layer 0 — target substrate
   649→Your existing pieces:
   650→
   651→assembler
   652→
   653→emulator
   654→
   655→FPGA hardware
   656→
   657→low-level monitor/debug hooks
   658→
   659→native calling convention for your 24-bit ISA
   660→
   661→This layer should know nothing about Pascal.
   662→
   663→Layer 1 — p-code VM / abstract machine
   664→This becomes the real foundation.
   665→
   666→It should define:
   667→
   668→instruction set
   669→
   670→value representation
   671→
   672→activation record/frame layout
   673→
   674→globals model
   675→
   676→heap model
   677→
   678→runtime traps
   679→
   680→debug metadata hooks
   681→
   682→Think of this as your language-neutral machine.
   683→
   684→Historically, Pascal p-code systems such as UCSD Pascal used a stack machine model, with a shared stack for expression evaluation and procedure frames. That is exactly the right family of design here. 
   685→
   686→Layer 2 — base runtime services
   687→This is not “Pascal runtime” yet. It is the minimal substrate any front end can rely on:
   688→
   689→heap allocator
   690→
   691→string/array block helpers
   692→
   693→bounds-check failure handler
   694→
   695→nil-pointer failure handler
   696→
   697→divide-by-zero handler
   698→
   699→assertion/trap support
   700→
   701→I/O hooks
   702→
   703→debug symbol and source mapping support
   704→
   705→Layer 3 — Pascal-specific runtime
   706→Only put Pascal-specific semantics here:
   707→
   708→new, dispose
   709→
   710→read, write, writeln
   711→
   712→Pascal file model, if you add it later
   713→
   714→set operations, if not in the VM
   715→
   716→conformant-array helpers, if you ever add them
   717→
   718→Pascal string helpers, if you extend beyond packed char arrays
   719→
   720→Layer 4 — front ends
   721→Start with:
   722→
   723→Pascal compiler → p-code
   724→
   725→Later:
   726→
   727→tiny BASIC → p-code
   728→
   729→structured Forth-like front end → p-code
   730→
   731→tiny Lisp → p-code
   732→
   733→maybe a C subset → p-code
   734→
   735→That is a strong architecture because the VM becomes your shared semantic platform.
   736→
   737→How memory is usually organized in a Pascal system
   738→A practical memory map for your p-code machine would be:
   739→
   740→+--------------------+ high memory
   741→| debugger scratch   |
   742→| VM bookkeeping     |
   743→+--------------------+
   744→| heap               |  grows upward
   745→|                    |
   746→+--------------------+
   747→| free space         |
   748→+--------------------+
   749→| stack              |  grows downward
   750→| call frames        |
   751→| eval stack         |
   752→+--------------------+
   753→| globals/statics    |
   754→+--------------------+
   755→| constants/literals |
   756→+--------------------+
   757→| p-code / bytecode  |
   758→+--------------------+ low memory
   759→That is a very natural fit for an emulator and FPGA target.
   760→
   761→Separate or unified stack?
   762→You have two choices:
   763→
   764→Option A — one unified stack
   765→Used for:
   766→
   767→procedure frames
   768→
   769→expression temporaries
   770→
   771→This matches classic p-code machine design well. UCSD p-Machine is described as using one shared stack for both procedure stack frames and instruction operands. 
   772→
   773→Pros
   774→
   775→simple VM
   776→
   777→fewer pointers/registers
   778→
   779→historically faithful
   780→
   781→Cons
   782→
   783→debugger is slightly harder
   784→
   785→expression stack corruption can damage frames if your VM is buggy
   786→
   787→less convenient for multiple languages with different evaluation models
   788→
   789→Option B — call stack + eval stack
   790→Separate:
   791→
   792→call stack for frames
   793→
   794→eval stack for p-code operands
   795→
   796→Pros
   797→
   798→cleaner debugger
   799→
   800→better invariants
   801→
   802→easier to support multiple front ends later
   803→
   804→easier to reason about stack depth separately from frame layout
   805→
   806→Cons
   807→
   808→slightly more VM state
   809→
   810→a bit less “classic”
   811→
   812→For your project, I would choose separate stacks unless you specifically want historical UCSD-like fidelity. It makes debugging much easier.
   813→
   814→Recommended runtime memory model
   815→For a first clean-room system, I would use four storage classes.
   816→
   817→1. Globals/statics
   818→Allocated once when program loads.
   819→
   820→Use for:
   821→
   822→global variables
   823→
   824→typed constants that need storage
   825→
   826→runtime state
   827→
   828→2. Stack
   829→Used for:
   830→
   831→parameters
   832→
   833→locals
   834→
   835→temporaries
   836→
   837→return address / dynamic link / static link
   838→
   839→This is where Pascal naturally lives for ordinary code.
   840→
   841→3. Heap
   842→Used for:
   843→
   844→pointers created by new
   845→
   846→dynamic records
   847→
   848→dynamic nodes for trees/lists
   849→
   850→later, strings or managed arrays if you choose
   851→
   852→4. Code/metadata space
   853→Used for:
   854→
   855→bytecode / p-code
   856→
   857→literal pools
   858→
   859→source line tables
   860→
   861→symbol tables for debugger
   862→
   863→What should heap allocation look like?
   864→For Pascal first version: explicit allocation
   865→I recommend these VM/runtime operations:
   866→
   867→HEAP_ALLOC size -> ptr
   868→
   869→HEAP_FREE ptr
   870→
   871→HEAP_ZERO ptr, size
   872→
   873→optional HEAP_REALLOC only if another language later wants it
   874→
   875→Pascal front end then maps:
   876→
   877→new(p) → allocate size of pointed-to type, assign pointer
   878→
   879→dispose(p) → free and optionally nil the pointer
   880→
   881→maybe mark/release as a nonstandard extension later
   882→
   883→That gives you a small, understandable runtime.
   884→
   885→Should the heap be malloc/free style?
   886→Yes, for the first version.
   887→
   888→Even if the source language says new/dispose, under the hood that is basically a typed wrapper around an allocator API equivalent to malloc/free. Free Pascal’s documentation explicitly distinguishes Pascal-level New/Dispose from lower-level GetMem/FreeMem, which supports this layered interpretation. 
   889→
   890→So the layering can be:
   891→
   892→VM allocator: raw blocks
   893→
   894→Pascal runtime: typed heap ops on top
   895→
   896→That is clean.
   897→
   898→Should Pascal use GC?
   899→Not initially.
   900→
   901→Reasons:
   902→
   903→not historically typical for classic Pascal
   904→
   905→adds runtime and debugger complexity
   906→
   907→muddies semantics while you are still validating the language
   908→
   909→not needed to get real Pascal programs working
   910→
   911→You can add GC later if another language benefits from it more.
   912→
   913→Good heap strategies for your VM
   914→For your hardware and emulator, here are the realistic options.
   915→
   916→Option 1 — bump allocator only
   917→Allocate by moving a heap pointer upward.
   918→
   919→No free except “reset all”.
   920→
   921→Pros
   922→
   923→tiny
   924→
   925→perfect for bring-up
   926→
   927→very easy to debug
   928→
   929→Cons
   930→
   931→cannot support true dispose
   932→
   933→leaks until program end
   934→
   935→Good for:
   936→
   937→compiler bring-up
   938→
   939→tests
   940→
   941→short-running tools
   942→
   943→Option 2 — free-list allocator
   944→Maintain block headers and a free list.
   945→
   946→Pros
   947→
   948→simple enough to implement
   949→
   950→supports dispose
   951→
   952→realistic for Pascal
   953→
   954→Cons
   955→
   956→fragmentation
   957→
   958→coalescing logic
   959→
   960→debugger must understand headers
   961→
   962→This is the best first “real” allocator.
   963→
   964→Option 3 — segregated size classes / slab style
   965→Useful later for managed runtimes or object-heavy languages.
   966→
   967→Pros
   968→
   969→fast
   970→
   971→predictable
   972→
   973→Cons
   974→
   975→more complexity
   976→
   977→overkill initially
   978→
   979→Option 4 — tracing GC
   980→Mark-sweep, copying, or generational.
   981→
   982→Pros
   983→
   984→future-proof for Lisp-like languages
   985→
   986→fewer leaks in managed languages
   987→
   988→Cons
   989→
   990→root scanning complexity
   991→
   992→interaction with stack maps
   993→
   994→debugger complexity
   995→
   996→moving GC complicates raw pointer semantics
   997→
   998→Not for version 1.
   999→
  1000→My recommendation for your first allocator
  1001→Implement two modes:
  1002→
  1003→Bring-up mode
  1004→bump allocator
  1005→
  1006→no free
  1007→
  1008→reset heap between program runs
  1009→
  1010→Real mode
  1011→free-list allocator with headers
  1012→
  1013→block size
  1014→
  1015→free/used bit
  1016→
  1017→maybe next/prev free links
  1018→
  1019→optional coalescing on free
  1020→
  1021→This gives you:
  1022→
  1023→fastest path to first success
  1024→
  1025→smooth transition to a usable Pascal runtime
  1026→
  1027→Pascal-specific semantic model for memory
  1028→Pascal usually feels cleaner than C because most ordinary objects are not heap allocated.
  1029→
  1030→Typical pattern:
  1031→
  1032→local records/arrays: stack
  1033→
  1034→globals: static
  1035→
  1036→only pointer-based structures: heap
  1037→
  1038→That means your front end should aggressively prefer stack/static placement and use heap only when the source says so.
  1039→
  1040→Suggested mapping
  1041→Variables
  1042→local scalar/record/array → frame offset
  1043→
  1044→global scalar/record/array → global segment offset
  1045→
  1046→Parameters
  1047→by value → copied into frame or parameter area
  1048→
  1049→var parameter → address passed in
  1050→
  1051→Functions/procedures
  1052→frame contains locals and bookkeeping
  1053→
  1054→static link for nested scopes
  1055→
  1056→Pointers
  1057→24-bit pointer to heap object or global/static object
  1058→
  1059→nil = 0 is simplest, unless address 0 is valid in your architecture
  1060→
  1061→Suggested p-code machine operations for memory
  1062→I would define VM instructions along these lines.
  1063→
  1064→Addressing/storage
  1065→LDG offset — load global
  1066→
  1067→STG offset — store global
  1068→
  1069→LDL offset — load local
  1070→
  1071→STL offset — store local
  1072→
  1073→LDA_LOCAL offset — push address of local
  1074→
  1075→LDA_GLOBAL offset — push address of global
  1076→
  1077→LOAD_IND size
  1078→
  1079→STORE_IND size
  1080→
  1081→Frame/nonlocal access
  1082→FOLLOW_STATIC depth
  1083→
  1084→LDN depth, offset
  1085→
  1086→STN depth, offset
  1087→
  1088→LDA_NONLOCAL depth, offset
  1089→
  1090→Heap
  1091→ALLOC size
  1092→
  1093→FREE
  1094→
  1095→MEMZERO size
  1096→
  1097→MEMCOPY size
  1098→
  1099→Checking
  1100→CHK_BOUNDS lo, hi
  1101→
  1102→CHK_NIL
  1103→
  1104→CHK_TAG if you later add tagged objects
  1105→
  1106→TRAP code
  1107→
  1108→Aggregate helpers
  1109→INDEX elem_size, low_bound
  1110→
  1111→FIELD offset
  1112→
  1113→This instruction set can support Pascal cleanly and still be reusable for other languages.
  1114→
  1115→Should the runtime be Pascal-specific or generic?
  1116→You said other languages may follow. So keep the lower runtime generic.
  1117→
  1118→Generic VM/runtime should own
  1119→raw allocation
  1120→
  1121→raw memory movement
  1122→
  1123→stack/frame model
  1124→
  1125→trap handling
  1126→
  1127→debug hooks
  1128→
  1129→primitive I/O abstraction
  1130→
  1131→Pascal front end/runtime should own
  1132→static typing rules
  1133→
  1134→new / dispose
  1135→
  1136→var parameter semantics
  1137→
  1138→nested procedure semantics
  1139→
  1140→Pascal built-ins
  1141→
  1142→Pascal standard type semantics
  1143→
  1144→That split lets you later add languages with different memory models.
  1145→
  1146→For example:
  1147→
  1148→Pascal → explicit allocation
  1149→
  1150→Lisp → GC heap
  1151→
  1152→BASIC → dynamic strings + simple reference counting
  1153→
  1154→Forth-like language → manual dictionary arena
  1155→
  1156→Same VM, different front-end/runtime layers.
  1157→
  1158→If other languages will follow, what memory model should the VM expose?
  1159→A good neutral model is:
  1160→
  1161→VM object kinds
  1162→Not source-language-visible, just runtime categories:
  1163→
  1164→raw block
  1165→
  1166→string block
  1167→
  1168→array block
  1169→
  1170→record/object block
  1171→
  1172→closure/env block
  1173→
  1174→runtime descriptor block
  1175→
  1176→You do not need this all up front, but it is helpful to reserve the concept.
  1177→
  1178→Minimal neutral API
  1179→At the VM/runtime boundary:
  1180→
  1181→alloc raw block
  1182→
  1183→free raw block
  1184→
  1185→read/write primitive
  1186→
  1187→copy/fill
  1188→
  1189→query size
  1190→
  1191→optional type tag in header
  1192→
  1193→optional finalizer hook later
  1194→
  1195→That is enough to host both manual-memory and managed-memory languages.
  1196→
  1197→Debugger implications
  1198→If memory matters, design the allocator and frames so the debugger can introspect them.
  1199→
  1200→Heap block header suggestion
  1201→Each allocated block could have:
  1202→
  1203→struct HeapHeader {
  1204→    u24 size_words;
  1205→    u24 flags;
  1206→    u24 type_id;      // optional
  1207→    u24 prev_or_next; // depending on allocator design
  1208→}
  1209→Useful flags:
  1210→
  1211→allocated/free
  1212→
  1213→Pascal object
  1214→
  1215→array
  1216→
  1217→string
  1218→
  1219→record
  1220→
  1221→internal runtime block
  1222→
  1223→maybe “poisoned/freed”
  1224→
  1225→Then your debugger can show:
  1226→
  1227→live heap blocks
  1228→
  1229→free blocks
  1230→
  1231→leaks on exit
  1232→
  1233→block ownership/type
  1234→
  1235→double free attempts
  1236→
  1237→use-after-free if you poison blocks in debug mode
  1238→
  1239→Frame record suggestion
  1240→Each call frame should expose:
  1241→
  1242→return PC
  1243→
  1244→previous frame pointer / dynamic link
  1245→
  1246→static link
  1247→
  1248→procedure id
  1249→
  1250→locals base
  1251→
  1252→eval stack base snapshot
  1253→
  1254→That makes nested Pascal debugging much easier.
  1255→
  1256→A practical staged plan
  1257→Phase 1 — define the VM first
  1258→Write documents for:
  1259→
  1260→vm-isa.md
  1261→
  1262→vm-memory.md
  1263→
  1264→vm-abi.md
  1265→
  1266→vm-debug.md
  1267→
  1268→Do this before writing much code.
  1269→
  1270→Phase 2 — build a tiny VM with no Pascal yet
  1271→Support:
  1272→
  1273→constants
  1274→
  1275→arithmetic
  1276→
  1277→branches
  1278→
  1279→calls
  1280→
  1281→locals/globals
  1282→
  1283→heap alloc/free
  1284→
  1285→traps
  1286→
  1287→line stepping
  1288→
  1289→Then hand-write small p-code tests.
  1290→
  1291→Phase 3 — add Pascal subset front end
  1292→Compile a tiny subset to p-code:
  1293→
  1294→integers
  1295→
  1296→booleans
  1297→
  1298→assignment
  1299→
  1300→if
  1301→
  1302→while
  1303→
  1304→procedures
  1305→
  1306→arrays
  1307→
  1308→records
  1309→
  1310→new / dispose
  1311→
  1312→Phase 4 — add debugger
  1313→Debugger should show:
  1314→
  1315→p-code instruction stream
  1316→
  1317→current line
  1318→
  1319→frame chain
  1320→
  1321→static chain
  1322→
  1323→locals
  1324→
  1325→globals
  1326→
  1327→heap blocks
  1328→
  1329→Phase 5 — let a second language target the same VM
  1330→This will force you to keep the VM generic rather than accidentally baking Pascal into it.
  1331→
  1332→Concrete recommendation on memory policy
  1333→If I were designing this for your 24-bit platform, I would do this:
  1334→
  1335→VM v1
  1336→separate call stack and eval stack
  1337→
  1338→globals segment
  1339→
  1340→heap with bump allocator in bring-up mode
  1341→
  1342→free-list allocator in normal mode
  1343→
  1344→no GC
  1345→
  1346→explicit traps
  1347→
  1348→pointer is 24-bit
  1349→
  1350→debug headers on every heap block in debug builds
  1351→
  1352→Pascal v1
  1353→no GC
  1354→
  1355→new / dispose
  1356→
  1357→stack for locals
  1358→
  1359→globals for statics
  1360→
  1361→heap only for pointers
  1362→
  1363→optional leak report at process exit
  1364→
  1365→runtime bounds checks enabled by default
  1366→
  1367→Later multi-language extension
  1368→Add optional alternate heap managers:
  1369→
  1370→manual heap
  1371→
  1372→reference-counted heap
  1373→
  1374→tracing GC heap
  1375→
  1376→and let each front end choose one.
  1377→
  1378→That avoids forcing Pascal into a GC design it does not need, while still leaving room for Lisp-like or scripting languages later.
  1379→
  1380→Bottom line
  1381→For your layered architecture, I would treat Pascal as a typed front end over a generic p-code VM, and I would use explicit heap management, not GC, for the first runtime.
  1382→
  1383→That means:
  1384→
  1385→stack for locals and temporaries
  1386→
  1387→globals for static storage
  1388→
  1389→heap for pointer-based dynamic objects
  1390→
  1391→new / dispose at the Pascal level
  1392→
  1393→allocator API underneath that is basically malloc/free in spirit
  1394→
  1395→GC only later, as an optional runtime mode for other languages
  1396→
  1397→That is historically aligned with Pascal, technically simpler, and a better base for a debugger and for additional languages. 
  1398→

  1400→
  1401→
  1402→Okay, in order to write tests, I'd like to introduce a p-code-assembler. Does tha make sense?  It would be a subset of Pascal not sharing Pascal idioms, but enough to create Hello World, toggle LEDs, read/write UART.   It would have a simple calling convention, not support typing per-sed, or complex data structures.  The target ISA is integer only, so no floating point.  Does this make sense?
  1403→
  1404→Yes — that makes a lot of sense.
  1405→
  1406→In fact, for your project I think a p-code assembler is one of the best early moves, because it lets you separate three problems that otherwise get tangled together:
  1407→
  1408→VM design
  1409→
  1410→runtime / calling convention design
  1411→
  1412→Pascal front-end correctness
  1413→
  1414→If you skip the assembler, every early failure is ambiguous:
  1415→
  1416→is the VM wrong?
  1417→
  1418→is the runtime wrong?
  1419→
  1420→is the code generator wrong?
  1421→
  1422→is the parser wrong?
  1423→
  1424→is the Pascal semantic analysis wrong?
  1425→
  1426→A p-code assembler gives you a way to test the VM and runtime directly, with tiny hand-written programs.
  1427→
  1428→Restating the architecture
  1429→You are proposing a layered system like this:
  1430→
  1431→Layer A — p-code VM
  1432→Executes a small instruction set.
  1433→
  1434→Layer B — p-code assembler
  1435→Human-writable source language for tests, demos, low-level runtime bring-up.
  1436→
  1437→Layer C — runtime library / monitor bindings
  1438→UART, LED, memory, traps, maybe stack/heap helpers.
  1439→
  1440→Layer D — Pascal compiler
  1441→Compiles Pascal into p-code.
  1442→
  1443→Layer E — later front ends
  1444→Other languages also target the same p-code.
  1445→
  1446→That is a very good architecture.
  1447→
  1448→The p-code assembler becomes for your VM what assembly is for a real ISA:
  1449→
  1450→a precise test tool
  1451→
  1452→a bootstrap tool
  1453→
  1454→a debugger input language
  1455→
  1456→a way to write tiny runtime pieces before Pascal is ready
  1457→
  1458→Why this is a strong idea
  1459→1. It gives you direct VM tests
  1460→You will want tests like:
  1461→
  1462→push constants, add, print result
  1463→
  1464→branch on zero
  1465→
  1466→call/return
  1467→
  1468→access locals/globals
  1469→
  1470→follow static/dynamic links if you have them
  1471→
  1472→allocate/free heap block
  1473→
  1474→write UART
  1475→
  1476→toggle LED
  1477→
  1478→bounds trap
  1479→
  1480→nil trap
  1481→
  1482→These are much easier to express in a tiny assembly-like language than in Pascal.
  1483→
  1484→2. It lets you bootstrap the runtime before Pascal exists
  1485→Before the Pascal compiler works, you still need:
  1486→
  1487→startup
  1488→
  1489→I/O shims
  1490→
  1491→debug/trap handlers
  1492→
  1493→maybe memory helpers
  1494→
  1495→A p-code assembler lets you write these as VM-level programs or modules.
  1496→
  1497→3. It clarifies the calling convention early
  1498→You said “simple calling convention.” Good.
  1499→
  1500→The assembler forces you to define:
  1501→
  1502→what a call frame looks like
  1503→
  1504→argument passing
  1505→
  1506→return values
  1507→
  1508→local storage reservation
  1509→
  1510→stack cleanup rules
  1511→
  1512→That discipline helps both the VM and Pascal backend.
  1513→
  1514→4. It gives you reproducible debugger cases
  1515→Debugger bring-up is hard if every test comes from a buggy compiler.
  1516→
  1517→With p-asm, you can hand-write:
  1518→
  1519→a three-function call chain
  1520→
  1521→a recursive countdown
  1522→
  1523→a loop with locals
  1524→
  1525→a UART output routine
  1526→
  1527→a deliberate trap
  1528→
  1529→Then verify stepping, breakpoints, stack display, and memory inspection.
  1530→
  1531→My recommendation: make it explicitly not Pascal
  1532→I agree with your instinct that it should not share Pascal idioms.
  1533→
  1534→Do not try to make it “tiny Pascal.”
  1535→
  1536→Make it look like a machine-oriented textual form.
  1537→
  1538→That keeps the layers clean:
  1539→
  1540→Pascal is a source language
  1541→
  1542→p-asm is a target-language authoring tool
  1543→
  1544→That distinction is healthy.
  1545→
  1546→What the p-code assembler should be
  1547→Think of it as:
  1548→
  1549→untyped or lightly typed
  1550→
  1551→integer-only
  1552→

  1554→
  1555→explicit loads/stores
  1556→
  1557→explicit call frames
  1558→
  1559→explicit memory operations

  1565→It should feel closer to:

  1572→
  1573→than to Pascal or C.

  1579→

  1581→

  1583→
  1584→simple loops

  1590→That suggests a very small feature set.

  1627→
  1628→A good design principle
  1629→The assembler should expose the VM’s semantics almost 1:1.
  1630→
  1631→So if the VM has an instruction like:
  1632→
  1633→PUSH 42
  1634→
  1635→LOADG uart_status
  1636→
  1637→JZ loop
  1638→
  1639→CALL putc
  1640→
  1641→RET
  1642→
  1643→then the assembler should map directly to that.
  1644→
  1645→Avoid “convenient” high-level sugar too early.
  1646→
  1647→You want this tool mainly for:
  1648→
  1649→clarity
  1650→
  1651→testing
  1652→
  1653→determinism
  1654→
  1655→not productivity.
  1656→
  1657→One key decision: stack machine vs explicit locals model
  1658→Since this is p-code, the natural design is a stack-oriented assembler.
  1659→
  1660→Example style:
  1661→
  1662→proc main
  1663→    push 72
  1664→    call putc
  1665→    push 105
  1666→    call putc
  1667→    push 10
  1668→    call putc
  1669→    halt
  1670→endproc
  1671→But for debugging and maintainability, I recommend you also support named local slots or explicit frame offsets.
  1672→
  1673→For example:
  1674→
  1675→proc blink locals 1
  1676→loop:
  1677→    push 1
  1678→    out led
  1679→    call delay
  1680→    push 0
  1681→    out led
  1682→    call delay
  1683→    jmp loop
  1684→endproc
  1685→or, more machine-like:
  1686→
  1687→proc blink 1
  1688→loop:
  1689→    push 1
  1690→    storeg LED_PORT
  1691→    call delay
  1692→    push 0
  1693→    storeg LED_PORT
  1694→    call delay
  1695→    jmp loop
  1696→endproc
  1697→That is enough.
  1698→
  1699→Suggested minimum p-code VM model
  1700→For your custom integer-only machine, I would keep the VM itself integer-only too.
  1701→
  1702→Core VM state
  1703→pc — p-code instruction pointer
  1704→
  1705→fp — frame pointer
  1706→
  1707→sp — eval stack pointer
  1708→
  1709→gp — globals base, or implicit globals segment
  1710→
  1711→maybe hp — heap pointer if heap exists now
  1712→
  1713→Core data model
  1714→one scalar word type: signed/unsigned 24-bit integer value
  1715→
  1716→addresses are also word-sized
  1717→
  1718→byte loads/stores handled explicitly if needed for UART and strings
  1719→
  1720→That is enough for quite a while.
  1721→
  1722→Suggested instruction categories
  1723→Stack / constants
  1724→push <imm>
  1725→
  1726→dup
  1727→
  1728→drop
  1729→
  1730→swap
  1731→
  1732→over
  1733→

  1767→
  1768→These can push 0/1.
  1769→

  1771→jmp <label>
  1772→
  1773→jz <label>
  1774→
  1775→jnz <label>
  1776→
  1777→call <proc>
  1778→
  1779→ret
  1780→
  1781→halt
  1782→
  1783→trap <code>
  1784→
  1785→Frame/local/global access
  1786→enter <nlocals>
  1787→
  1788→leave
  1789→
  1790→loadl <offset>
  1791→
  1792→storel <offset>
  1793→
  1794→loadg <symbol>
  1795→
  1796→storeg <symbol>
  1797→
  1798→addrg <symbol>
  1799→
  1800→addrl <offset>
  1801→
  1802→Indirect memory
  1803→load
  1804→
  1805→store
  1806→
  1807→loadb
  1808→
  1809→storeb
  1810→
  1811→Optional runtime
  1812→alloc <size>
  1813→
  1814→free
  1815→
  1816→sys <id> or hostcall <id>
  1817→
  1818→You may not need heap yet, but having the slot reserved is useful.
  1819→
  1820→Calling convention recommendation
  1821→Since this is for bring-up and testing, make the convention brutally simple.
  1822→
  1823→Suggested convention
  1824→arguments are pushed on the eval stack
  1825→
  1826→call foo
  1827→
  1828→callee uses enter nlocals
  1829→
  1830→arguments become addressable relative to frame pointer
  1831→
  1832→return value is pushed on the eval stack before ret
  1833→
  1834→caller removes args if needed, or callee does — pick one and stick to it
  1835→
  1836→For simplicity, I would choose:
  1837→
  1838→Better v1 convention
  1839→caller pushes args
  1840→
  1841→call foo
  1842→
  1843→callee reads args at known negative offsets from fp
  1844→
  1845→callee leaves one return value on the eval stack, or none for procedures
  1846→
  1847→callee pops its frame only
  1848→
  1849→arguments are cleaned by callee or encoded in ret n
  1850→
  1851→A nice compact form is:
  1852→
  1853→ret 0 for no args
  1854→
  1855→ret 2 meaning pop 2 args and return
  1856→
  1857→or retv 2 meaning return value + pop 2 args
  1858→
  1859→But that may complicate the VM slightly.
  1860→
  1861→A simpler alternative:
  1862→
  1863→keep call frames separate from eval stack
  1864→
  1865→copy args into frame slots on call
  1866→
  1867→That is cleaner but a bit more machinery.
  1868→
  1869→For early work, either is fine. If you expect lots of debugging, a separate frame area is easier to reason about.
  1870→
  1871→For your use case, I would recommend two abstractions for I/O
  1872→Since you want UART and LED access, decide whether p-code talks to:
  1873→
  1874→Option A — VM-level abstract devices
  1875→Example:
  1876→
  1877→sys PUTC
  1878→
  1879→sys GETC
  1880→
  1881→sys LED_WRITE
  1882→
  1883→Pros
  1884→
  1885→portable across host interpreter and real hardware
  1886→
  1887→easy to test
  1888→
  1889→easier debugger integration
  1890→
  1891→Cons
  1892→
  1893→less “metal”
  1894→
  1895→Option B — memory-mapped addresses
  1896→Example:
  1897→
  1898→push UART_DATA
  1899→
  1900→push 65
  1901→
  1902→storeb
  1903→
  1904→Pros
  1905→
  1906→closer to the hardware
  1907→
  1908→fewer abstractions
  1909→
  1910→Cons
  1911→
  1912→less portable
  1913→
  1914→awkward for hosted tests
  1915→
  1916→slightly harder to evolve
  1917→
  1918→Best answer: support both
  1919→Use memory-mapped addresses in the runtime if you want, but also provide small VM/syscall abstractions for common tasks.
  1920→
  1921→For example:
  1922→
  1923→putc implemented as runtime p-asm
  1924→
  1925→inside it, either call sys PUTC on host, or do memory-mapped UART on target
  1926→
  1927→That keeps the assembler small and the runtime portable.
  1928→
  1929→Hello World / LED / UART are perfect early test cases
  1930→These are exactly the right kinds of tests.

  1969→A concrete source-language sketch
  1970→Something like this would work well:
  1971→
  1972→.const UART_DATA   0xFF0100
  1973→.const UART_STATUS 0xFF0101
  1974→.const LED_PORT    0xFF0200
  1975→
  1976→.proc putc
  1977→    ; arg0 at fp-1
  1978→wait:
  1979→    push UART_STATUS
  1980→    loadb
  1981→    push 1
  1982→    and
  1983→    jz wait
  1984→
  1985→    push UART_DATA
  1986→    loadarg 0
  1987→    storeb
  1988→    ret 1
  1989→.end
  1990→
  1991→.proc puts
  1992→loop:
  1993→    loadarg 0
  1994→    loadb
  1995→    dup
  1996→    jz done
  1997→    call putc
  1998→    loadarg 0
  1999→    push 1
  2000→    add
  2001→    storearg 0
  2002→    jmp loop
  2003→done:
  2004→    drop
  2005→    ret 1
  2006→.end
  2007→
  2008→.data msg "Hello, world!\n", 0
  2009→
  2010→.proc main
  2011→    push msg
  2012→    call puts
  2013→    halt
  2014→.end
  2015→I am not saying this exact syntax is best, only that this level of abstraction is appropriate.
  2016→
  2017→One subtle point: typed vs untyped
  2018→You said “not support typing per se.”
  2019→
  2020→That is right for the assembler source language. But the VM still needs operational widths.
  2021→
  2022→Even in an “untyped” p-code assembler, you will still need distinctions like:
  2023→
  2024→word load/store
  2025→
  2026→byte load/store
  2027→
  2028→signed vs unsigned compare, maybe
  2029→
  2030→address vs integer conventions, even if both are same-sized
  2031→
  2032→So the source language can be untyped, but the instruction set should still be explicit enough to avoid ambiguity.
  2033→
  2034→For example:
  2035→
  2036→load = word
  2037→
  2038→loadb = byte
  2039→
  2040→lt = signed compare
  2041→
  2042→ltu = unsigned compare if needed later
  2043→
  2044→This will matter for UART, chars, string traversal, and array/string runtime work.
  2045→
  2046→Should the assembler include macros?
  2047→Not initially.
  2048→
  2049→For v1, I would keep it extremely small:
  2050→
  2051→labels
  2052→
  2053→constants
  2054→
  2055→procedures
  2056→
  2057→data/string directives
  2058→
  2059→instructions
  2060→
  2061→Then after the VM is stable, add:
  2062→
  2063→.macro
  2064→
  2065→.include
  2066→
  2067→conditional assembly
  2068→
  2069→symbolic frame slot aliases
  2070→
  2071→Macros are helpful later, but they can hide VM design problems early.
  2072→
  2073→What to test first
  2074→I would define a staged test suite.
  2075→
  2076→Stage 1 — pure VM instruction tests
  2077→push/add/sub
  2078→
  2079→dup/drop/swap
  2080→
  2081→compare/branch
  2082→
  2083→simple halt
  2084→
  2085→Stage 2 — procedure tests
  2086→one call
  2087→
  2088→nested calls
  2089→
  2090→recursion
  2091→
  2092→return value
  2093→
  2094→locals
  2095→
  2096→Stage 3 — memory tests
  2097→globals
  2098→
  2099→indirect load/store
  2100→
  2101→byte load/store
  2102→
  2103→string walk
  2104→
  2105→Stage 4 — device tests
  2106→UART output
  2107→
  2108→UART polling input
  2109→
  2110→LED on/off
  2111→
  2112→blink loop
  2113→
  2114→Stage 5 — error tests
  2115→bad opcode
  2116→
  2117→stack underflow
  2118→
  2119→invalid memory access
  2120→
  2121→explicit trap
  2122→
  2123→These tests should exist before Pascal codegen starts producing real programs.
  2124→
  2125→This also helps your future debugger design
  2126→A p-code assembler pairs naturally with a debugger.
  2127→
  2128→You can support debug info at two levels:
  2129→
  2130→Level 1 — instruction/label level
  2131→current pc
  2132→
  2133→current proc
  2134→
  2135→label names
  2136→
  2137→stack contents
  2138→
  2139→frame contents
  2140→
  2141→Level 2 — source mapping
  2142→p-asm source line to p-code address
  2143→
  2144→later Pascal source line to p-code address
  2145→
  2146→That means your debugger can be useful before Pascal exists.
  2147→
  2148→This is a big win.
  2149→
  2150→One thing I would avoid
  2151→I would avoid making the p-code assembler too much like a “portable assembly language for the target ISA.”
  2152→
  2153→It should target the VM, not become a second real ISA project.
  2154→
  2155→Otherwise you risk re-creating a complicated lower-level compiler target and losing the simplicity benefit.
  2156→
  2157→So keep it:
  2158→
  2159→stack-based
  2160→
  2161→compact
  2162→
  2163→procedural
  2164→
  2165→explicit
  2166→
  2167→test-friendly
  2168→
  2169→My recommendation
  2170→Yes, introduce a p-code assembler now.
  2171→
  2172→Make it:
  2173→
  2174→machine-like, not Pascal-like
  2175→
  2176→integer-only
  2177→
  2178→stack-oriented
  2179→
  2180→simple calling convention
  2181→
  2182→labels + directives + instructions
  2183→
  2184→enough for:
  2185→
  2186→Hello World
  2187→
  2188→LED toggle
  2189→
  2190→UART I/O
  2191→
  2192→loops
  2193→

  2195→
  2196→globals

1→# AgentRail
     2→
     3→A Rust workflow CLI for keeping AI agents on track across fresh contexts.
     4→
     5→AgentRail solves a specific engineering pain point: **AI coding agents lose operational knowledge between contexts.** The agent knows the goal but forgets procedural details -- which API to call, which flags to pass, which files to reference, and how to validate the output. AgentRail provides structured handoffs, deterministic step execution, and in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) so agents succeed on the first attempt instead of improvising and failing 25% of the time.
     6→
     7→## The Problem
     8→
     9→When working with AI coding agents on multi-step projects:
    10→
    11→- **Context truncation** causes agents to lose procedural details mid-session
    12→- **Tool ambiguity** leads agents to pick the wrong approach for known tasks
    13→- **Execution drift** means agents creatively rewrite procedures that should be deterministic
    14→- **Session boundaries** force you to re-explain everything when a new context starts
    15→
    16→The result: you intervene manually on roughly 1 in 4 steps, re-explaining what the agent already figured out in a previous session.
    17→
    18→## How AgentRail Fixes This
    19→
    20→AgentRail maintains a **saga** -- a persistent, append-only record of a multi-step workflow. Each saga tracks steps with typed roles, enabling an orchestration loop:
    21→
    22→1. **Meta steps** prepare structured handoff packets with objectives, procedures, success patterns, and failure modes
    23→2. **Production steps** execute semantic work guided by those packets
    24→3. **Deterministic steps** run fully-specified jobs (TTS, ffmpeg, video concat) without agent involvement
    25→4. **Validation steps** check outputs against acceptance contracts
    26→
    27→Every step records its trajectory (state, action, result, reward). On the next run of a similar task, AgentRail retrieves successful trajectories and injects them into the agent's context -- **in-context reinforcement learning**. The agent learns from its own past successes without any weight updates.
    28→
    29→## Current Status
    30→
    31→**Phases 0-5 implemented.** The full ICRL loop is functional:
    32→
    33→- 13 CLI commands: `setup`, `init`, `add`, `status`, `next`, `begin`, `complete`, `plan`, `history`, `distill`, `run-loop`, `abort`, `help`
    34→- Maintenance mode: `agentrail add` for ad-hoc tasks, agents create steps from user messages
    35→- XSkill dual memory: skills (strategic workflow docs) + trajectories (per-run records)
    36→- ICRL injection: `next` shows skill procedures, failure modes, and past successful trajectories
    37→- Trajectory recording via `complete --reward --actions --failure-mode`
    38→- Skill distillation: `distill` generates skills from accumulated trajectories
    39→- Domain repos: register external skill/executor/validator repos
    40→- Shell executors and validators for deterministic step auto-execution
    41→- Orchestrator loop: `run-loop` auto-executes deterministic steps, pauses at agent steps
    42→- 41 integration tests, all passing
    43→
    44→## Quick Start
    45→
    46→```bash
    47→# Build and install
    48→cargo build --release
    49→sw-install -p .
    50→
    51→# Bootstrap a project (creates saga + CLAUDE.md + domain registration)
    52→cd ~/my-project
    53→agentrail setup --name my-project \
    54→  --plan "Build a web app with user auth" \
    55→  --domain /path/to/agentrail-domain-coding
    56→
    57→# Create the first step
    58→agentrail complete --summary "Initialized" \
    59→  --next-slug scaffold \
    60→  --next-prompt "Set up project structure" \
    61→  --next-role production \
    62→  --planned "implement: Build core logic" \
    63→  --planned "test: Write tests"
    64→
    65→# Start Claude Code
    66→claude "go"
    67→```
    68→
    69→Or use the interactive wizard:
    70→
    71→```bash
    72→bash /path/to/agentrail-rs/scripts/agentrail-wizard.sh
    73→```
    74→
    75→For maintenance mode (ongoing bug fixes, ad-hoc tasks):
    76→
    77→```bash
    78→# Just tell Claude what to do -- no pre-planning needed
    79→claude "fix the login timeout bug in auth.rs"
    80→
    81→# Or pre-load tasks and process them
    82→agentrail add --slug fix-123 --prompt "Fix login timeout"
    83→agentrail add --slug fix-124 --prompt "Fix mobile layout"
    84→claude "go"   # does fix-123, stops
    85→claude "go"   # does fix-124, stops
    86→```
    87→
    88→See [Getting Started](docs/getting-started.md) for the full walkthrough,
    89→and [Maintenance Mode](docs/maintenance-mode.md) for ongoing task workflows.
    90→
    91→## Agent Workflow
    92→
    93→The intended workflow for an AI agent starting a fresh context:
    94→
    95→1. Run `agentrail next` -- outputs the plan, step list, current step prompt, context files, and instructions
    96→2. Read the prompt and any context files listed
    97→3. Run `agentrail begin` to mark the step as in-progress
    98→4. Do the work
    99→5. Run `agentrail complete --summary "..." --next-slug ... --next-prompt ...` to record what was done and set up the next step
   100→
   101→This ensures every fresh context starts with full awareness of what happened, what's next, and how to do it.
   102→
   103→## Architecture
   104→
   105→Cargo workspace with five crates:
   106→
   107→```
   108→crates/
   109→  agentrail-core/      Domain types: saga, step, trajectory, handoff packet, job spec, errors
   110→  agentrail-store/     File-based persistence against .agentrail/ directory
   111→  agentrail-cli/       CLI binary (agentrail) with clap derive
   112→  agentrail-exec/      Deterministic step executors (stub)
   113→  agentrail-validate/  Output validators and acceptance contracts (stub)
   114→```
   115→
   116→Dependency flow: `cli -> store, exec, validate -> core`
   117→
   118→All runtime data lives in `.agentrail/` (append-only):
   119→
   120→```
   121→.agentrail/
   122→  saga.toml                         Saga metadata
   123→  plan.md                           Evolving plan
   124→  steps/NNN-slug/
   125→    step.toml                       Step config (role, status, job spec, packet file)
   126→    prompt.md                       Instructions for this step
   127→    summary.md                      What was accomplished
   128→  trajectories/{task_type}/
   129→    run_NNN.json                    Individual ICRL trajectory records
   130→  sessions/{session-id}.jsonl       Claude Code conversation snapshots
   131→```
   132→
   133→## Key Concepts
   134→
   135→**Step Roles** define what kind of work a step involves:
   136→
   137→| Role | Description |
   138→|------|-------------|
   139→| Meta | Prepares ICRL packets and handoffs for the next production step |
   140→| Production | Executes semantic work using a prepared packet |
   141→| Deterministic | Fully specified, no agent needed (TTS, ffmpeg, etc.) |
   142→| Validation | Checks outputs against acceptance contracts |
   143→| Legacy | Untyped step (backward compatibility) |
   144→
   145→**ICRL Trajectories** are state/action/reward records stored per task type. When a task type recurs, successful trajectories are retrieved and injected into the agent's context so it can replicate what worked before.
   146→
   147→**Handoff Packets** are structured briefings from a meta agent to a production agent containing: objective, inputs, success patterns, common failures, procedure, and output contract.
   148→
   149→**Step Transitions** enforce a valid lifecycle: Pending -> InProgress -> Completed|Blocked.
   150→
   151→## Roadmap
   152→
   153→| Phase | Description |
   154→|-------|-------------|
   155→| 0 | Walking skeleton: working CLI with tests (done) |
   156→| 1 | Deterministic step execution (GenerateTts, ffmpeg, probe, concat) |
   157→| 2 | Meta handoff packet generation and rendering |
   158→| 3 | Trajectory retrieval and ICRL injection into prompts |
   159→| 4 | Hybrid orchestrator loop with auto-advance and escalation |
   160→
   161→## Lineage
   162→
   163→Evolved from [avoid-compaction](https://github.com/softwarewrighter/avoid-compaction), a saga-based context checkpoint tool. Core patterns (saga/step model, session snapshots, TOML persistence) carried forward; extended with ICRL trajectory storage, step role typing, deterministic job specs, and handoff packets.
   164→
   165→## Development
   166→
   167→```bash
   168→cargo test --workspace                    # run all tests
   169→cargo test -p agentrail-store             # run tests for one crate
   170→cargo test -p agentrail-store saga        # run tests matching a pattern
   171→cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings   # lint
   172→cargo fmt --check                         # format check
   173→```
   174→
   175→Pre-commit gate (all must pass):
   176→
   177→```bash
   178→cargo test && cargo clippy -- -D warnings && cargo fmt --check
   179→```
   180→
   181→TDD workflow: write failing test first, implement minimum logic, refactor.
   182→
