   631→1. Target shape of the system
   632→Requirements
   633→Your early Forth needs to support:
   634→
   635→: TWO 2 ;
   636→: SQUARE DUP * ;
   637→5 SQUARE .
   638→That implies:
   639→
   640→the dictionary can hold headers and bodies
   641→
   642→colon definitions compile a sequence of cells
   643→
   644→executing a colon word runs those compiled cells in order
   645→
   646→; ends the definition by compiling EXIT
   647→
   648→numeric literals in compile mode become LIT <value>
   649→
   650→2. Recommended execution model
   651→Use a tiny inner interpreter with these moving parts:
   652→
   653→data stack: your normal Forth stack
   654→
   655→return stack: used for nesting colon definitions
   656→
   657→IP: instruction pointer into a colon definition body
   658→
   659→W: current word / execution token being run
   660→
   661→A colon definition’s body is a stream of 24-bit cells:
   662→
   663→xt of another word
   664→
   665→xt of LIT
   666→
   667→literal data cell after LIT
   668→
   669→xt of EXIT
   670→
   671→Example:
   672→
   673→: SQUARE DUP * ;
   674→compiles conceptually to:
   675→
   676→[DUP] [*] [EXIT]
   677→and
   678→
   679→: TWO 2 ;
   680→compiles to:
   681→
   682→[LIT] [2] [EXIT]
   683→3. Minimal memory model
   684→Since COR24 is naturally 24-bit, I strongly recommend:
   685→
   686→dictionary body cells are 24-bit
   687→
   688→data stack cells are 24-bit
   689→
   690→return stack cells are 24-bit
   691→
   692→HERE always advances by 3 bytes
   693→
   694→That removes a lot of complexity.
   695→
   696→4. Dictionary structure
   697→You do not need a perfect ANS/Forth header layout yet. Keep it simple.
   698→
   699→A reasonable early header:
   700→
   701→+0  link        (24-bit pointer to previous word)
   702→+3  flags_len   (1 byte flags, 1 byte len, or packed however you like)
   703→+?  name bytes
   704→+?  code field  (24-bit pointer)
   705→+?  body        (24-bit cells...)
   706→Conceptually each word has:
   707→
   708→link
   709→
   710→flags
   711→
   712→name
   713→
   714→code field address (CFA)
   715→
   716→parameter/body field (PFA)
   717→
   718→Suggested flags
   719→Use at least:
   720→
   721→F_IMMED = immediate word
   722→
   723→maybe later F_HIDDEN during compilation
   724→
   725→5. Two kinds of words
   726→Primitive word
   727→A primitive word’s code field points directly to machine code.
   728→
   729→Examples:
   730→
   731→DUP
   732→
   733→DROP
   734→
   735→+
   736→
   737→.
   738→
   739→EMIT
   740→
   741→EXIT
   742→
   743→LIT
   744→
   745→Colon word
   746→A colon word’s code field points to the common runtime DOCOL.
   747→
   748→Its body is the threaded code.
   749→
   750→So for:
   751→
   752→: SQUARE DUP * ;
   753→the word header says:
   754→
   755→name = "SQUARE"
   756→
   757→code field = DOCOL
   758→
   759→body = [xt(DUP), xt(*), xt(EXIT)]
   760→
   761→6. Inner interpreter model
   762→Here is the core runtime idea.
   763→
   764→Global VM-like registers/state
   765→Conceptually:
   766→
   767→IP = pointer into currently executing colon body
   768→
   769→W = current xt / current word
   770→
   771→return stack stores previous IP
   772→
   773→When a word is executed:
   774→
   775→fetch its code field
   776→
   777→jump/call to that runtime
   778→
   779→For a primitive, that runtime does the work and returns.
   780→
   781→For a colon word, DOCOL sets up IP to walk its body.
   782→
   783→7. Pseudo-code for NEXT
   784→You may implement a central dispatch loop, or inline pieces. Conceptually:
   785→
   786→NEXT:
   787→    W = *IP
   788→    IP = IP + CELL
   789→    goto CODEFIELD(W)
   790→Where CODEFIELD(W) means “run the runtime for this word.”
   791→
   792→Depending on how you represent xt, xt may be:
   793→
   794→pointer to header
   795→
   796→pointer to code field
   797→
   798→pointer directly to executable/runtime slot
   799→
   800→For bootstrap simplicity, choose one consistent convention and stick to it.
   801→
   802→A very workable choice:
   803→
   804→xt = address of header
   805→
   806→helper routines derive CFA/PFA from header layout
   807→
   808→Or:
   809→
   810→xt = address of code field
   811→
   812→body is just after it for colon words
   813→
   814→For tiny systems, xt = CFA address is often simpler at runtime.
   815→
   816→8. Recommended representation for xt
   817→I recommend:
   818→
   819→xt = CFA address
   820→
   821→for primitive words, CFA contains pointer to machine routine
   822→
   823→for colon words, CFA contains pointer to DOCOL
   824→
   825→PFA/body begins at xt + CELL
   826→
   827→So:
   828→
   829→xt ----> [ code pointer ]
   830→         [ body cell 0   ]
   831→         [ body cell 1   ]
   832→         ...
   833→Then:
   834→
   835→executing a word = load code pointer at xt, jump there
   836→
   837→colon body starts at xt + CELL
   838→
   839→That makes DOCOL very easy.
   840→
   841→9. Pseudo-code for DOCOL
   842→Behavior
   843→When a colon word is executed:
   844→
   845→save current IP on return stack
   846→
   847→set IP to this word’s body
   848→
   849→start executing from there
   850→
   851→Pseudo-code
   852→DOCOL(W):
   853→    RPUSH(IP)
   854→    IP = W + CELL     ; skip CFA, point at first body cell
   855→    NEXT
   856→That is the classic essence.
   857→
   858→10. Pseudo-code for EXIT
   859→Behavior
   860→When EXIT runs inside a colon definition:
   861→
   862→restore the previous IP from return stack
   863→
   864→continue execution there
   865→
   866→Pseudo-code
   867→EXIT:
   868→    IP = RPOP()
   869→    NEXT
   870→That is all.
   871→
   872→11. Pseudo-code for LIT
   873→Behavior
   874→LIT is compiled into a colon definition, followed by one cell of data.
   875→
   876→At runtime:
   877→
   878→fetch next cell from IP
   879→
   880→push it on data stack
   881→
   882→advance IP
   883→
   884→Pseudo-code
   885→LIT:
   886→    value = *IP
   887→    IP = IP + CELL
   888→    PUSH(value)
   889→    NEXT
   890→That is what makes this work:
   891→
   892→: TWO 2 ;
   893→compiled as:
   894→
   895→[LIT] [2] [EXIT]
   896→12. Compile helpers you will need
   897→You need some tiny compile-time helpers.
   898→
   899→, (comma)
   900→Append one cell at HERE, then advance HERE.
   901→
   902→COMMA(x):
   903→    MEM24[HERE] = x
   904→    HERE = HERE + CELL
   905→On COR24, CELL = 3.
   906→
   907→C,
   908→Append one byte.
   909→
   910→Useful for headers/names.
   911→
   912→header creation helper
   913→Something like:
   914→
   915→CREATE_HEADER(name, flags)
   916→which writes:
   917→
   918→link
   919→
   920→flags/len
   921→
   922→name
   923→
   924→aligns HERE if needed
   925→
   926→returns xt/cfa location
   927→
   928→13. Pseudo-code for :
   929→: is executed by the interpreter, not compiled as a normal word in your first version.
   930→
   931→Behavior
   932→When : is encountered:
   933→
   934→parse next token as name
   935→
   936→create new dictionary entry
   937→
   938→set its CFA to DOCOL
   939→
   940→make its body empty for now
   941→
   942→set STATE = 1
   943→
   944→remember this new definition as the current one being compiled
   945→
   946→Pseudo-code
   947→COLON:
   948→    name = PARSE_TOKEN()
   949→    if name empty:
   950→        ERROR("missing name after :")
   951→        return
   952→
   953→    xt = CREATE_WORD(name, flags=0)
   954→    STORE_CODEPTR(xt, &DOCOL)
   955→
   956→    CURRENT = xt
   957→    STATE = 1
   958→    return
   959→If you later add “hidden/smudged until complete,” then:
   960→
   961→create with hidden flag set
   962→
   963→clear hidden at ;
   964→
   965→For first cut, you can skip hidden, though hidden is nicer.
   966→
   967→14. Pseudo-code for ;
   968→; must be IMMEDIATE.
   969→
   970→That means when encountered during compile mode, it executes right away instead of being compiled.
   971→
   972→Behavior
   973→compile EXIT
   974→
   975→set STATE = 0
   976→
   977→finalize the definition
   978→
   979→clear CURRENT
   980→
   981→Pseudo-code
   982→SEMICOLON:
   983→    if STATE == 0:
   984→        ERROR("; outside compile mode")
   985→        return
   986→
   987→    COMMA(xt_of_EXIT)
   988→    STATE = 0
   989→    CURRENT = 0
   990→    return
   991→Mark this word immediate in the dictionary.
   992→
   993→15. Pseudo-code for INTERPRET
   994→This is the most important part.
   995→
   996→Behavior
   997→For each token:
   998→
   999→try FIND
  1000→
  1001→if found:
  1002→
  1003→interpret mode: execute it
  1004→
  1005→compile mode:
  1006→
  1007→if immediate: execute it now
  1008→
  1009→else compile its xt
  1010→
  1011→if not found:
  1012→
  1013→try number conversion
  1014→
  1015→interpret mode: push number
  1016→
  1017→compile mode: compile LIT, then value
  1018→
  1019→else error
  1020→
  1021→Pseudo-code
  1022→INTERPRET:
  1023→loop:
  1024→    token = PARSE_TOKEN()
  1025→    if token is empty:
  1026→        return
  1027→
  1028→    xt = FIND(token)
  1029→
  1030→    if xt != 0:
  1031→        if STATE == 0:
  1032→            EXECUTE(xt)
  1033→        else:
  1034→            if IS_IMMEDIATE(xt):
  1035→                EXECUTE(xt)
  1036→            else:
  1037→                COMMA(xt)
  1038→        goto loop
  1039→
  1040→    ok, n = NUMBER(token, BASE)
  1041→    if ok:
  1042→        if STATE == 0:
  1043→            PUSH(n)
  1044→        else:
  1045→            COMMA(xt_of_LIT)
  1046→            COMMA(n)
  1047→        goto loop
  1048→
  1049→    ERROR("unknown word")
  1050→    STATE = 0      ; maybe
  1051→    return
  1052→That is the whole essence of interpret/compile mode.
  1053→
  1054→16. Pseudo-code for EXECUTE
  1055→Assuming xt = CFA address:
  1056→
  1057→EXECUTE(xt):
  1058→    W = xt
  1059→    codeptr = MEM24[W]
  1060→    jump to codeptr
  1061→If your assembly prefers call/return style:
  1062→
  1063→put W in a designated register
  1064→
  1065→jump/call to codeptr
  1066→
  1067→Primitive words return back to the interpreter or dispatcher.
  1068→Colon words never “return normally”; they drive NEXT.
  1069→
  1070→17. Example compiled layout
  1071→Let’s make it concrete.
  1072→
  1073→: TWO 2 ;
  1074→Header:
  1075→
  1076→link -> previous
  1077→name = "TWO"
  1078→CFA  = &DOCOL
  1079→PFA0 = xt(LIT)
  1080→PFA1 = 2
  1081→PFA2 = xt(EXIT)
  1082→: SQUARE DUP * ;
  1083→Header:
  1084→
  1085→link -> previous
  1086→name = "SQUARE"
  1087→CFA  = &DOCOL
  1088→PFA0 = xt(DUP)
  1089→PFA1 = xt(*)
  1090→PFA2 = xt(EXIT)
  1091→18. Example runtime trace
  1092→Running 5 SQUARE .
  1093→Interpret phase:
  1094→
  1095→5 => number => push 5
  1096→
  1097→SQUARE => found, STATE=0 => EXECUTE(xt_square)
  1098→
  1099→Execution of SQUARE:
  1100→
  1101→DOCOL:
  1102→
  1103→push old IP to return stack
  1104→
  1105→set IP = body_of_square
  1106→
  1107→Then threaded execution:
  1108→
  1109→fetch xt(DUP), execute DUP
  1110→
  1111→fetch xt(*), execute *
  1112→
  1113→fetch xt(EXIT), execute EXIT
  1114→
  1115→EXIT restores old IP
  1116→
  1117→interpreter continues
  1118→
  1119→next token . prints result
  1120→
  1121→19. COR24-specific register suggestion
  1122→You only have a few truly general-purpose registers, so keep the runtime conventions very small.
  1123→
  1124→A possible convention:
  1125→
  1126→r0 = TOS cache or temp
  1127→
  1128→r1 = IP
  1129→
  1130→r2 = W or temp
  1131→
  1132→fp/sp keep their normal machine meaning
  1133→
  1134→return stack might be separate from machine call stack, or you may reserve a memory region and manage it manually
  1135→
  1136→Given Forth’s nature, I would strongly consider:
  1137→
  1138→data stack = machine stack if convenient
  1139→
  1140→return stack = separate Forth-managed stack in memory
  1141→
  1142→or the opposite, depending on your existing emulator/runtime.
  1143→
  1144→Important point
  1145→Do not confuse:
  1146→
  1147→machine subroutine return addresses
  1148→
  1149→Forth return stack entries (IP values for colon nesting)
  1150→
  1151→You can implement primitives as machine subroutines, but colon control flow should still use a Forth return stack.
  1152→
  1153→20. Input/source model for .fth
  1154→Traditional .fth support does not require a filesystem inside COR24 yet.
  1155→
  1156→You just need this conceptual API:
  1157→
  1158→REFILL / KEY / PARSE_TOKEN
  1159→Early on, KEY can read from:
  1160→
  1161→UART
  1162→
  1163→a host-provided input buffer
  1164→
  1165→Then .fth support is simply:
  1166→
  1167→host sends file contents into the same input stream
  1168→
  1169→So compile mode does not care whether the source came from:
  1170→
  1171→keyboard
  1172→
  1173→UART
  1174→
  1175→redirected file text
  1176→
  1177→That is exactly the right design.
  1178→
  1179→21. First-pass simplifications I recommend
  1180→To keep the agent from getting lost, I would explicitly allow these constraints:
  1181→
  1182→Allowed simplifications
  1183→no recursion yet
  1184→
  1185→no RECURSE
  1186→
  1187→no hidden/smudged headers yet
  1188→
  1189→no [COMPILE], POSTPONE, LITERAL
  1190→
  1191→no control flow words yet
  1192→
  1193→no CREATE ... DOES> yet
  1194→
  1195→no unload / forget / marker yet
  1196→
  1197→Must work
  1198→: name ... ;
  1199→
  1200→literals in compiled code
  1201→
  1202→calling primitive words from colon defs
  1203→
  1204→calling colon words from colon defs
  1205→
  1206→nested colon calls
  1207→
  1208→.fth text fed through interpreter
  1209→
  1210→22. Very useful next words after this works
  1211→Once the above works, next best additions are:
  1212→
  1213→[ immediate: STATE = 0
  1214→
  1215→]: STATE = 1
  1216→
  1217→IMMEDIATE
  1218→
  1219→CONSTANT
  1220→
  1221→VARIABLE
  1222→
  1223→But I would not let the agent do those until : ; LIT EXIT DOCOL are solid.
  1224→
  1225→23. Suggested black-box test script
  1226→Here is a minimal progression.
  1227→
  1228→test 1
  1229→1 2 3 .S
  1230→test 2
  1231→: TWO 2 ;
  1232→TWO .
  1233→test 3
  1234→: SQUARE DUP * ;
  1235→5 SQUARE .
  1236→test 4
  1237→: TWO 2 ;
  1238→: FOUR TWO TWO + ;
  1239→FOUR .
  1240→test 5
  1241→: A 10 ;
  1242→: B A A + ;
  1243→B .
  1244→test 6
  1245→: ON 1 LED! ;
  1246→: OFF 0 LED! ;
  1247→ON
  1248→OFF
  1249→If tests 2–5 work, your compile infrastructure is basically alive.
  1250→
  1251→24. Common failure modes to warn the agent about
  1252→; not immediate
  1253→Then ; gets compiled into the word instead of ending it.
  1254→
  1255→literals compiled incorrectly
  1256→Then:
  1257→
  1258→: TWO 2 ;
  1259→won’t push 2 at runtime.
  1260→
  1261→no EXIT compiled
  1262→Then execution falls into garbage.
  1263→
  1264→bad body start address
  1265→If DOCOL points IP at the wrong cell, first instruction fetch is nonsense.
  1266→
  1267→return stack misuse
  1268→Nested colon definitions will fail or corrupt state.
  1269→
  1270→HERE misalignment
  1271→Since cells are 3 bytes, this is especially easy to get wrong.
  1272→
  1273→25. A compact implementation sketch for the agent
  1274→You can hand this to the coding agent nearly verbatim.
  1275→
  1276→Core model:
  1277→- xt = CFA address
  1278→- CFA cell contains runtime code pointer
  1279→- PFA/body begins at xt + 3
  1280→- colon word CFA points to DOCOL
  1281→- primitive word CFA points to machine routine
  1282→
  1283→Runtime:
  1284→DOCOL(W):
  1285→    RPUSH(IP)
  1286→    IP = W + 3
  1287→    NEXT
  1288→
  1289→EXIT:
  1290→    IP = RPOP()
  1291→    NEXT
  1292→
  1293→LIT:
  1294→    PUSH(MEM24[IP])
  1295→    IP += 3
  1296→    NEXT
  1297→
  1298→Compile-time:
  1299→COMMA(x):
  1300→    MEM24[HERE] = x
  1301→    HERE += 3
  1302→
  1303→COLON (:):
  1304→    name = PARSE_TOKEN()
  1305→    xt = CREATE_WORD(name)
  1306→    MEM24[xt] = &DOCOL
  1307→    CURRENT = xt
  1308→    STATE = 1
  1309→
  1310→SEMICOLON (;, immediate):
  1311→    COMMA(xt_EXIT)
  1312→    STATE = 0
  1313→    CURRENT = 0
  1314→
  1315→INTERPRET token loop:
  1316→    xt = FIND(token)
  1317→    if found:
  1318→        if STATE == 0:
  1319→            EXECUTE(xt)
  1320→        else if IMMEDIATE(xt):
  1321→            EXECUTE(xt)
  1322→        else:
  1323→            COMMA(xt)
  1324→    else if NUMBER(token):
  1325→        if STATE == 0:
  1326→            PUSH(n)
  1327→        else:
  1328→            COMMA(xt_LIT)
  1329→            COMMA(n)
  1330→    else:
  1331→        ERROR
  1332→26. My strongest recommendation
  1333→For this phase, tell the agent to avoid cleverness.
  1334→
  1335→Do not:
  1336→
  1337→optimize primitives inline
  1338→
  1339→compile machine branches yet
  1340→
  1341→unify all stacks too early
  1342→
  1343→add control words yet
  1344→
  1345→invent a special .fth compiler
  1346→
  1347→Instead, keep it faithful:
  1348→
  1349→.fth is just source text
  1350→
  1351→same interpreter handles REPL and file-fed input
  1352→
  1353→compile mode only changes what happens to recognized words and numbers
  1354→
  1355→That gets you to the point where Forth starts building itself.
  1356→
  1357→If you want, next I can give you a more concrete memory diagram for a sample word like : SQUARE DUP * ;, including header bytes/cells and how HERE, LATEST, and FIND should interact.
  1358→
  1359→
  1360→
  1361→
  1362→

130→
   131→; Near DOCOL: CFA is "bra do_docol; .byte 0" (3 bytes), r0 = CFA from NEXT
   132→do_docol:
   133→    add r1, -3
   134→    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; push IP to return stack
   135→    mov r2, r0           ; r2 = CFA (from NEXT's jmp)
   136→    add r2, 3            ; r2 = PFA = CFA + 3
   137→    ; NEXT
   138→    lw r0, 0(r2)
   139→    add r2, 3
   140→    jmp (r0)
   141→
   142→; Far DOCOL: CFA is "push r0; la r0, do_docol_far; jmp (r0)" (6 bytes)
   143→; CFA address was pushed to data stack by "push r0" in the CFA
   144→do_docol_far:
   145→    add r1, -3
   146→    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; push IP to return stack
   147→    pop r2               ; r2 = CFA (from data stack)
   148→    add r2, 6            ; r2 = PFA = CFA + 6
   149→    ; NEXT
   150→    lw r0, 0(r2)
   151→    add r2, 3
   152→    jmp (r0)

   154→; ============================================================
   155→; Primitives with Dictionary Headers
   156→; ============================================================
   157→; Chain: entry_emit(link=0) → entry_key → ... → entry_immediate(LATEST)
   158→
   159→; ------------------------------------------------------------
   160→; EMIT ( c -- ) : Write character to UART with TX busy-wait
   161→; ------------------------------------------------------------
   162→entry_emit:
   163→    .word 0
   164→    .byte 4
   165→    .byte 69, 77, 73, 84
   166→do_emit:
   167→    pop r0              ; r0 = character
   168→    add r1, -3
   169→    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; save IP on return stack
   170→    add r1, -3
   171→    sw r0, 0(r1)        ; save byte on return stack
   172→    la r2, -65280       ; r2 = UART base
   173→emit_poll:
   174→    lb r0, 1(r2)        ; status (sign-extended; bit 7 → negative)
   175→    cls r0, z           ; C = (status < 0) = TX busy
   176→    brt emit_poll
   177→    lw r0, 0(r1)        ; restore byte
   178→    add r1, 3
   179→    sb r0, 0(r2)        ; write byte to UART TX
   180→    lw r2, 0(r1)        ; restore IP
   181→    add r1, 3
   182→    ; NEXT
   183→    lw r0, 0(r2)
   184→    add r2, 3
   185→    jmp (r0)
   186→
   187→; ------------------------------------------------------------
   188→; KEY ( -- c ) : Read character from UART with RX busy-wait
   189→; ------------------------------------------------------------
   190→entry_key:
   191→    .word entry_emit
   192→    .byte 3
   193→    .byte 75, 69, 89
   194→do_key:
   195→    add r1, -3
   196→    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; save IP on return stack
   197→key_poll:
   198→    la r0, -65280       ; UART base
   199→    lbu r0, 1(r0)       ; status byte (zero-extended)
   200→    lcu r2, 1           ; bit 0 mask
   201→    and r0, r2          ; isolate RX ready bit
   202→    ceq r0, z           ; C = (not ready)
   203→    brt key_poll
   204→    la r0, -65280       ; reload UART base
   205→    lbu r0, 0(r0)       ; read byte
   206→    push r0
   207→    lw r2, 0(r1)        ; restore IP
   208→    add r1, 3
   209→    ; NEXT
   210→    lw r0, 0(r2)
   211→    add r2, 3
   212→    jmp (r0)
   213→
   214→; ------------------------------------------------------------
   215→; EXIT ( -- ) : End colon definition, pop IP from return stack
   216→; ------------------------------------------------------------
   217→entry_exit:
   218→    .word entry_key
   219→    .byte 4
   220→    .byte 69, 88, 73, 84
   221→do_exit:
   222→    lw r2, 0(r1)        ; restore IP from return stack
   223→    add r1, 3
   224→    ; NEXT
   225→    lw r0, 0(r2)
   226→    add r2, 3
   227→    jmp (r0)
   228→
   229→; ------------------------------------------------------------

1300→    sw r0, 0(r2)        ; LATEST = new_entry
  1301→    lw r2, 0(r1)        ; restore r2
  1302→    add r1, 3           ; RS: [IP]
  1303→
  1304→    ; Restore IP and NEXT
  1305→    lw r2, 0(r1)
  1306→    add r1, 3
  1307→    lw r0, 0(r2)
  1308→    add r2, 3
  1309→    jmp (r0)
  1310→
  1311→; ------------------------------------------------------------
  1312→; : ( -- ) : Start colon definition
  1313→; Calls CREATE, writes 6-byte far CFA, enters compile mode
  1314→; ------------------------------------------------------------
  1315→entry_colon:
  1316→    .word entry_create
  1317→    .byte 1
  1318→    .byte 58
  1319→do_colon:
  1320→    add r1, -3
  1321→    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; save IP. RS: [IP]
  1322→
  1323→    ; --- Inline CREATE logic (read word, build header) ---
  1324→    ; This duplicates CREATE's word-reading and header-building.
  1325→    ; For code size, we CALL CREATE by jumping to it with a return trick.
  1326→    ; Push a return address on return stack, set IP to a thread that calls
  1327→    ; CREATE then returns. Actually, simpler: just copy CREATE's body.
  1328→    ;
  1329→    ; For now, use a colon-definition approach:
  1330→    ; We'll define do_colon as a hand-assembled colon word.
  1331→    ; This requires do_docol to be nearby... or use do_docol_far.
  1332→    ; Since we're IN a primitive, let's manually call CREATE.
  1333→
  1334→    ; Save return info and call CREATE by setting up a mini thread
  1335→    ; Actually, the simplest approach: CREATE reads from UART and builds header.
  1336→    ; COLON does the same PLUS writes CFA + sets STATE.
  1337→    ; Rather than duplicating, let's store a return address and jump.
  1338→
  1339→    ; Use data stack for return: push address of colon_after_create, jmp do_create
  1340→    ; But do_create uses NEXT to return, so it would follow IP, not our return addr.
  1341→    ;
  1342→    ; Alternative: set IP to point to a mini thread: [do_create, colon_continue]
  1343→    ; and let NEXT drive it.
  1344→    la r0, colon_thread
  1345→    lw r2, 0(r1)        ; restore original IP... wait, I already saved it.
  1346→    ; Actually, I want to save original IP, replace IP with colon_thread, do NEXT.
  1347→    ; Original IP is already on RS from the first sw.
  1348→    ; Set IP to colon_thread:
  1349→    la r2, colon_thread
  1350→    ; NEXT
  1351→    lw r0, 0(r2)
  1352→    add r2, 3
  1353→    jmp (r0)
  1354→
  1355→; Helper thread for COLON: CREATE, then colon_write_cfa
  1356→colon_thread:
  1357→    .word do_create
  1358→    .word do_colon_cfa
  1359→    .word do_rbrac       ; enter compile mode
  1360→    .word do_exit        ; return to original IP (saved on RS by do_colon)
  1361→
  1362→; Helper: write 6-byte CFA at HERE
  1363→; Writes: push r0 (0x7D), la r0 opcode (0x29), do_docol_far addr (3B), jmp r0 (0x26)
  1364→do_colon_cfa:
  1365→    add r1, -3
  1366→    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; save IP
  1367→    ; Load HERE
  1368→    la r0, var_here_val
  1369→    lw r2, 0(r0)        ; r2 = HERE (dest)
  1370→    ; Copy 6 bytes from cfa_template
  1371→    la r0, cfa_template
  1372→    push r0              ; save template addr
  1373→    lw r0, 0(r0)        ; first 3 bytes
  1374→    sw r0, 0(r2)        ; store at HERE
  1375→    add r2, 3
  1376→    pop r0               ; template addr
  1377→    add r0, 3
  1378→    lw r0, 0(r0)        ; next 3 bytes
  1379→    sw r0, 0(r2)        ; store at HERE+3
  1380→    add r2, 3
  1381→    ; Update HERE (+6)
  1382→    la r0, var_here_val
  1383→    sw r2, 0(r0)
  1384→    ; Restore IP and NEXT
  1385→    lw r2, 0(r1)
  1386→    add r1, 3
  1387→    lw r0, 0(r2)
  1388→    add r2, 3
  1389→    jmp (r0)
  1390→
  1391→; Template for far colon CFA (6 bytes):
  1392→; push r0 (0x7D), la r0 (0x29), addr_lo, addr_mid, addr_hi, jmp(r0) (0x26)
  1393→cfa_template:
  1394→    .byte 125            ; push r0
  1395→    .byte 41             ; la r0 opcode
  1396→    .word do_docol_far   ; 3-byte address of do_docol_far
  1397→    .byte 38             ; jmp (r0)
  1398→
  1399→; ------------------------------------------------------------
  1400→; ; ( -- ) : End colon definition [IMMEDIATE]
  1401→; Compiles EXIT, enters interpret mode
  1402→; ------------------------------------------------------------
  1403→entry_semi:
  1404→    .word entry_colon
  1405→    .byte 129
  1406→    .byte 59
  1407→do_semi:
  1408→    add r1, -3
  1409→    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; save IP
  1410→    ; Compile EXIT at HERE
  1411→    la r0, var_here_val
  1412→    lw r2, 0(r0)        ; r2 = HERE
  1413→    la r0, do_exit
  1414→    sw r0, 0(r2)        ; mem[HERE] = do_exit
  1415→    add r2, 3
  1416→    la r0, var_here_val
  1417→    sw r2, 0(r0)        ; update HERE
  1418→    ; STATE = 0 (interpreting)
  1419→    la r0, var_state_val
  1420→    lc r2, 0
  1421→    sw r2, 0(r0)
  1422→    lw r2, 0(r1)
  1423→    add r1, 3
  1424→    ; NEXT
  1425→    lw r0, 0(r2)
  1426→    add r2, 3
  1427→    jmp (r0)
  1428→
  1429→; ------------------------------------------------------------
  1430→; IMMEDIATE ( -- ) : Toggle IMMEDIATE flag on most recent word
  1431→; ------------------------------------------------------------
  1432→entry_immediate:
  1433→    .word entry_semi
  1434→    .byte 9
  1435→    .byte 73, 77, 77, 69, 68, 73, 65, 84, 69
  1436→do_immediate:
  1437→    add r1, -3
  1438→    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; save IP
  1439→    la r0, var_latest_val
  1440→    lw r0, 0(r0)        ; r0 = latest entry
  1441→    add r0, 3           ; r0 = address of flags_len
  1442→    lbu r2, 0(r0)       ; r2 = flags_len
  1443→    push r0              ; save flags address
  1444→    lcu r0, 128
  1445→    xor r2, r0           ; toggle bit 7
  1446→    pop r0               ; r0 = flags address
  1447→    sb r2, 0(r0)        ; store updated flags
  1448→    lw r2, 0(r1)
  1449→    add r1, 3

837→entry_find:
   838→    .word entry_rbrac
   839→    .byte 4
   840→    .byte 70, 73, 78, 68
   841→do_find:
   842→    add r1, -3
   843→    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; save IP          RS: [IP]
   844→
   845→    pop r0               ; r0 = c-addr
   846→    add r1, -3
   847→    sw r0, 0(r1)        ; save c-addr      RS: [c-addr, IP]
   848→
   849→    lbu r2, 0(r0)       ; r2 = search length
   850→    add r1, -3
   851→    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; save search_len  RS: [search_len, c-addr, IP]
   852→
   853→    add r0, 1           ; r0 = search name start
   854→    add r1, -3
   855→    sw r0, 0(r1)        ; save search_start RS: [ss, sl, ca, IP]
   856→
   857→    ; Load LATEST and push on DS for find_loop
   858→    la r0, var_latest_val
   859→    lw r0, 0(r0)
   860→    push r0              ; DS: [entry]
   861→
   862→find_loop:
   863→    ; Entry pointer is on data stack
   864→    pop r0               ; r0 = entry (0 = end of chain)
   865→    ceq r0, z
   866→    brf find_have_entry
   867→
   868→    ; === Not found (inline handler) ===
   869→    lw r0, 6(r1)        ; c-addr (RS offset 6)
   870→    add r1, 9           ; pop ss, sl, ca. RS: [IP]
   871→    push r0              ; DS: [c-addr]
   872→    lc r0, 0
   873→    push r0              ; DS: [0, c-addr]
   874→    lw r2, 0(r1)
   875→    add r1, 3
   876→    lw r0, 0(r2)
   877→    add r2, 3
   878→    jmp (r0)
   879→
   880→find_have_entry:
   881→    ; r0 = entry pointer
   882→    ; Save entry on RS
   883→    add r1, -3
   884→    sw r0, 0(r1)        ; RS: [entry, ss, sl, ca, IP]
   885→
   886→    ; Load flags_len byte
   887→    lbu r2, 3(r0)       ; r2 = flags_len
   888→    add r1, -3
   889→    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; RS: [fl, entry, ss, sl, ca, IP]
   890→
   891→    ; Check HIDDEN (bit 6): if hidden, skip via la+jmp
   892→    lcu r0, 64
   893→    and r0, r2
   894→    ceq r0, z
   895→    brt find_not_hidden
   896→    la r0, find_skip_entry
   897→    jmp (r0)
   898→find_not_hidden:
   899→
   900→    ; Extract name_len = flags_len & 0x3F
   901→    lw r0, 0(r1)        ; r0 = flags_len
   902→    lcu r2, 63
   903→    and r0, r2           ; r0 = name_len
   904→
   905→    ; Compare with search_len
   906→    lw r2, 9(r1)        ; r2 = search_len (RS offset 9)
   907→    ceq r0, r2
   908→    brt find_len_match
   909→    la r0, find_skip_entry
   910→    jmp (r0)
   911→find_len_match:
   912→
   913→    ; === Lengths match — compare characters ===
   914→    ; r0 = name_len = counter
   915→
   916→    ; Save counter
   917→    add r1, -3
   918→    sw r0, 0(r1)        ; RS: [ctr, fl, entry, ss, sl, ca, IP]
   919→
   920→    ; ename_ptr = entry + 4
   921→    lw r0, 6(r1)        ; entry (RS offset 6)
   922→    add r0, 4
   923→    add r1, -3
   924→    sw r0, 0(r1)        ; RS: [ep, ctr, fl, entry, ss, sl, ca, IP]
   925→
   926→    ; sname_ptr = search_start
   927→    lw r0, 12(r1)       ; search_start (RS offset 12)
   928→    add r1, -3
   929→    sw r0, 0(r1)        ; RS: [sp, ep, ctr, fl, entry, ss, sl, ca, IP]
   930→
   931→find_cmp_loop:
   932→    lw r0, 6(r1)        ; counter (RS offset 6)
   933→    ceq r0, z
   934→    brt find_matched
   935→
   936→    ; Load entry char
   937→    lw r0, 3(r1)        ; ename_ptr (RS offset 3)
   938→    lbu r2, 0(r0)       ; r2 = entry char
   939→
   940→    ; Load search char
   941→    lw r0, 0(r1)        ; sname_ptr (RS offset 0)
   942→    lbu r0, 0(r0)       ; r0 = search char
   943→
   944→    ceq r0, r2
   945→    brf find_char_fail
   946→
   947→    ; Advance ename_ptr
   948→    lw r0, 3(r1)
   949→    add r0, 1
   950→    sw r0, 3(r1)
   951→    ; Advance sname_ptr
   952→    lw r0, 0(r1)
   953→    add r0, 1
   954→    sw r0, 0(r1)
   955→    ; Decrement counter
   956→    lw r0, 6(r1)

1829→entry_interpret:
  1830→    .word entry_number
  1831→    .byte 9
  1832→    .byte 73, 78, 84, 69, 82, 80, 82, 69, 84
  1833→
  1834→do_interpret:
  1835→    add r1, -3
  1836→    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; RS: [caller_IP]
  1837→    ; Start: call WORD
  1838→    la r2, i_word_thread
  1839→    lw r0, 0(r2)
  1840→    add r2, 3
  1841→    jmp (r0)
  1842→
  1843→i_word_thread:
  1844→    .word do_word
  1845→    .word do_i_after_word
  1846→
  1847→; After WORD: DS has [c-addr]. Check if empty.
  1848→do_i_after_word:
  1849→    ; IP points past this in i_word_thread — we ignore it.
  1850→    ; RS: [caller_IP] (WORD saved/restored its own IP on RS)
  1851→    pop r0               ; c-addr
  1852→    lbu r2, 0(r0)       ; length
  1853→    ceq r2, z
  1854→    brf i_have_token
  1855→    ; Empty token → end of input, return to caller
  1856→    lw r2, 0(r1)
  1857→    add r1, 3
  1858→    lw r0, 0(r2)
  1859→    add r2, 3
  1860→    jmp (r0)
  1861→
  1862→i_have_token:
  1863→    ; r0 = c-addr, push for FIND
  1864→    push r0
  1865→    la r2, i_find_thread
  1866→    lw r0, 0(r2)
  1867→    add r2, 3
  1868→    jmp (r0)
  1869→
  1870→i_find_thread:
  1871→    .word do_find
  1872→    .word do_i_after_find
  1873→
  1874→; After FIND: DS has (c-addr 0) or (cfa flag)
  1875→do_i_after_find:
  1876→    ; RS: [caller_IP]
  1877→    pop r0               ; flag (0=not found, 1=IMMEDIATE, -1=normal)
  1878→    ceq r0, z
  1879→    brt i_not_found
  1880→
  1881→    ; Found: DS has [cfa], r0 = flag
  1882→    ; Check STATE
  1883→    add r1, -3
  1884→    sw r0, 0(r1)        ; save flag. RS: [flag, caller_IP]
  1885→    la r0, var_state_val
  1886→    lw r0, 0(r0)        ; r0 = STATE
  1887→    ceq r0, z
  1888→    brt i_found_exec_interp ; STATE=0 → interpreting → always execute
  1889→
  1890→    ; Compiling (STATE != 0): check IMMEDIATE flag
  1891→    lw r0, 0(r1)        ; flag
  1892→    add r1, 3           ; pop flag. RS: [caller_IP]
  1893→    lcu r2, 1
  1894→    ceq r0, r2           ; C = (flag == 1 = IMMEDIATE)
  1895→    brt i_found_exec     ; IMMEDIATE → execute even in compile mode
  1896→
  1897→    ; Normal word + compile mode → COMMA the CFA
  1898→    ; DS: [cfa]
  1899→    la r2, i_comma_continue
  1900→    lw r0, 0(r2)
  1901→    add r2, 3
  1902→    jmp (r0)
  1903→
  1904→i_comma_continue:
  1905→    .word do_comma       ; compile the CFA
  1906→    .word do_word        ; get next token
  1907→    .word do_i_after_word
  1908→
  1909→i_found_exec:
  1910→    ; Execute the word. DS: [cfa]
  1911→    ; Clean RS if flag is still there (STATE=0 path)
  1912→    ; STATE=0 path: RS = [flag, caller_IP], need to pop flag
  1913→    ; IMMEDIATE path: RS = [caller_IP], flag already popped
  1914→    ; To unify: check RS. Actually, let me use separate labels.
  1915→    ; The STATE=0 branch jumps here with RS: [flag, caller_IP]
  1916→    ; The IMMEDIATE branch jumps here with RS: [caller_IP]
  1917→    ; I need to know which path. Use a flag or separate labels.
  1918→    bra i_do_exec        ; IMMEDIATE path (flag already popped)
  1919→
  1920→i_found_exec_interp:
  1921→    ; STATE=0 path: RS: [flag, caller_IP]
  1922→    add r1, 3           ; pop flag. RS: [caller_IP]
  1923→
  1924→i_do_exec:
  1925→    la r2, i_continue
  1926→    pop r0               ; cfa
  1927→    jmp (r0)             ; execute — NEXT will use IP=i_continue
  1928→
  1929→i_continue:
  1930→    .word do_word
  1931→    .word do_i_after_word
  1932→
  1933→i_not_found:
  1934→    ; DS: [c-addr] (FIND returned it unchanged)
  1935→    ; Try NUMBER. Dup for error reporting.
  1936→    pop r0
  1937→    push r0
  1938→    push r0              ; DS: [c-addr, c-addr]
  1939→    la r2, i_num_thread
  1940→    lw r0, 0(r2)
  1941→    add r2, 3
  1942→    jmp (r0)
  1943→
  1944→i_num_thread:
  1945→    .word do_number
  1946→    .word do_i_after_number
  1947→
  1948→; After NUMBER: DS has [flag, n, c-addr]
  1949→do_i_after_number:
  1950→    ; RS: [caller_IP]
  1951→    pop r0               ; flag (0=ok)
  1952→    ceq r0, z
  1953→    brt i_num_ok
  1954→    ; Failed: print "? ", discard n and c-addr
  1955→    pop r0               ; discard n
  1956→    pop r0               ; discard c-addr
  1957→    ; Print "? "
  1958→    lc r0, 63
  1959→    push r0
  1960→    la r2, -65280
  1961→i_err1:
  1962→    lb r0, 1(r2)
  1963→    cls r0, z
  1964→    brt i_err1
  1965→    pop r0
  1966→    sb r0, 0(r2)
  1967→    lc r0, 32
  1968→    push r0
  1969→i_err2:
  1970→    lb r0, 1(r2)
  1971→    cls r0, z
  1972→    brt i_err2
  1973→    pop r0
  1974→    sb r0, 0(r2)
  1975→    ; Continue loop
  1976→    la r2, i_continue
  1977→    lw r0, 0(r2)
  1978→    add r2, 3
  1979→    jmp (r0)
  1980→
  1981→i_num_ok:
  1982→    ; DS: [n, c-addr]. Keep n, discard c-addr
  1983→    pop r2               ; n
  1984→    pop r0               ; discard c-addr
  1985→    push r2              ; DS: [n]
  1986→
  1987→    ; Check STATE
  1988→    la r0, var_state_val
  1989→    lw r0, 0(r0)
  1990→    ceq r0, z
  1991→    brt i_num_interp     ; STATE=0 → leave on stack, continue
  1992→
  1993→    ; Compiling: compile LIT, n
  1994→    ; DS: [n]. Need to compile do_lit then n.
  1995→    ; First push do_lit address, comma it, then push n, comma it.
  1996→    pop r0               ; n
  1997→    add r1, -3
  1998→    sw r0, 0(r1)        ; save n on RS. RS: [n, caller_IP]
  1999→    la r0, do_lit
  2000→    push r0              ; DS: [do_lit_addr]
  2001→    la r2, i_compile_lit_thread
  2002→    lw r0, 0(r2)
  2003→    add r2, 3
  2004→    jmp (r0)
  2005→
  2006→i_compile_lit_thread:
  2007→    .word do_comma       ; compile do_lit address
  2008→    .word do_i_compile_n
  2009→
  2010→do_i_compile_n:
  2011→    ; RS: [n, caller_IP]
  2012→    lw r0, 0(r1)        ; n
  2013→    add r1, 3           ; pop n. RS: [caller_IP]
  2014→    push r0              ; DS: [n] for comma
  2015→    ; Comma n, then continue loop
  2016→    la r2, i_comma_continue
  2017→    lw r0, 0(r2)
  2018→    add r2, 3
  2019→    jmp (r0)
  2020→
  2021→i_num_interp:
  2022→    ; Interpreting: n already on stack, continue loop
  2023→    la r2, i_continue
  2024→    lw r0, 0(r2)
  2025→    add r2, 3
  2026→    jmp (r0)
  2027→
  2028→; ------------------------------------------------------------

     2→# demo.sh — Run tf24a Forth demos
     3→# Usage:
     4→#   ./demo.sh           Run automated demo (non-interactive)
     5→#   ./demo.sh repl      Start interactive REPL
     6→#   ./demo.sh test      Run test suite and check results

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a complete understanding. Let me compile my findings.

## Comprehensive Exploration of tf24a: Tiny Forth for COR24

I've thoroughly explored the **tf24a** project at `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/tf24a/`. This is **not** a Rust project with cor24-rs dependencies—it's a **clean-room Direct Threaded Code (DTC) Forth implementation written entirely in COR24 assembly language**.

---

### 1. Project Overview

**tf24a** is a minimal, self-extending Forth kernel targeting the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA, implemented as a single assembly file (`forth.s`, 2,630 lines). Key characteristics:

- **Language**: COR24 assembly (not Rust)
- **Size**: Single monolithic `.s` file assembled and run via `cor24-run`
- **Architecture**: Direct Threaded Code (DTC) with a Forth text interpreter
- **Development Stage**: Phases 1-4 complete—bootstrap, threading, dictionary, compile mode, and UART-based REPL

---

### 2. How It Builds & Runs (cor24-run assembler)

The project does **not** use the cor24-rs library as a Rust dependency. Instead:

- **Assembler used**: `cor24-run --run forth.s [options]`
- **Invocation patterns** (from CLAUDE.md):
  ```bash
  cor24-run --run forth.s [opts]              # assemble and execute
  cor24-run --run forth.s --dump --speed 0    # dump machine state
  cor24-run --run forth.s --terminal --echo   # interactive REPL
  cor24-run --run forth.s -u 'input' --speed 0  # feed UART input
  ```
- **Testing**: Uses `reg-rs` regression framework for comparing UART output against golden baselines

---

### 3. Assembler API & COR24 ISA Constraints (from CLAUDE.md)

The assembly strictly follows COR24's **24-bit instruction set limitations**:

**Load destinations** (r0, r1, r2 only—NOT fp, sp):
```assembly
lc r0, imm8        ; load 8-bit constant
lcu r0, imm8       ; load 8-bit unsigned
la r0, imm24       ; load 24-bit address
lw r0, off(base)   ; load word
lb r0, off(base)   ; load byte (sign-extended)
lbu r0, off(base)  ; load byte (zero-extended)
```

**ALU destinations** (r0, r1, r2 only):
```assembly
add r0, r1         ; add register
add r0, imm8       ; add immediate (works for r0,r1,r2,sp)
sub, and, or, xor  ; similar constraints
```

**Unique fp/sp rules**:
- `fp` is **only** usable as a base register for indexing data stack memory
- `sp` cannot receive load results; use `mov fp, sp; push fp; pop r0` to read it
- **Cell size**: 3 bytes (24-bit), so `add r2, 3` advances by one cell
- **Branch offset**: ±127 bytes (signed 8-bit); far jumps use `la r0, label; jmp (r0)`

---

### 4. Assembler Syntax Rules

**COR24 Assembly specifics** (from CLAUDE.md):
- Labels on own line: `label:` (never inline `label: instr`)
- Comments: `;` only (not `#`)
- **Decimals only**: `la r0, -65280` not hex `0xFF0100`
- `.word label` emits 24-bit address (one label per directive)
- `.byte 72, 101, 108` for raw bytes (no string literals)
- No `.align` directive; manually pad with `.byte 0`

---

### 5. Architecture & Register Allocation (Frozen)

From `forth.s` header and CLAUDE.md:

```
r0 = W (work register / scratch)
r1 = RSP (return stack pointer, SRAM ~0x0F0000 growing down)
r2 = IP (instruction pointer for threaded code)
sp = DSP (data stack, hardware push/pop in EBR)
fp = scratch (limited: pop/push/add-as-source only; used as base register for memory ops)
```

**Return stack**: Separate from hardware call stack, grows down from 0x0F0000 in 3-byte cells.

---

### 6. How It Uses the Assembler API (DTC Inner Interpreter)

The core execution model is **Direct Threaded Code**. Every word execution follows this pattern:

**NEXT (inlined at tail of every primitive, 5 bytes)**:
```assembly
lw r0, 0(r2)    ; W = mem[IP] — fetch code address from thread
add r2, 3       ; IP += cell
jmp (r0)        ; execute code at CFA
```

**Two kinds of word entry points**:

1. **Near DOCOL** (for colon words close to do_docol, ~127B range):
   ```assembly
   do_docol:
       add r1, -3
       sw r2, 0(r1)        ; save IP on return stack
       mov r2, r0           ; r2 = CFA (from previous jmp)
       add r2, 3            ; r2 = PFA (skip 3-byte CFA)
       lw r0, 0(r2)
       add r2, 3
       jmp (r0)             ; NEXT
   ```

2. **Far DOCOL** (for distant definitions):
   ```assembly
   do_docol_far:
       add r1, -3
       sw r2, 0(r1)        ; save IP on return stack
       pop r2               ; r2 = CFA (pushed by 6-byte far CFA code)
       add r2, 6            ; r2 = PFA (CFA is 6 bytes for far calls)
       lw r0, 0(r2)
       add r2, 3
       jmp (r0)             ; NEXT
   ```

**Colon word CFA format decision**:
- **Near**: 3 bytes total: `bra do_docol; .byte 0` (if within 127B)
- **Far**: 6 bytes: `push r0; la r0, do_docol_far; jmp (r0)` (CFA saved on data stack)

**EXIT** (restore IP and continue):
```assembly
do_exit:
    lw r2, 0(r1)        ; restore IP from return stack
    add r1, 3
    lw r0, 0(r2)        ; NEXT
    add r2, 3
    jmp (r0)
```

---

### 7. Assembler API Usage in forth.s Source Files

Key implementation primitives with CFA+PFA structure:

**Primitive word entry** (e.g., EMIT):
```assembly
entry_emit:
    .word 0              ; link to previous (0 = chain end)
    .byte 4              ; flags + name length (lower 6 bits = len)
    .byte 69, 77, 73, 84 ; "EMIT"
do_emit:
    pop r0               ; r0 = character from data stack
    add r1, -3
    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; save IP on return stack
    ; ... UART TX polling logic ...
    lw r2, 0(r1)        ; restore IP
    add r1, 3
    lw r0, 0(r2)        ; NEXT
    add r2, 3
    jmp (r0)
```

**Colon word CFA template** (far format):
```assembly
cfa_template:
    .byte 125            ; push r0
    .byte 41             ; la r0 opcode
    .word do_docol_far   ; 3-byte address
    .byte 38             ; jmp (r0)
```

Then the body is a sequence of 24-bit cells pointing to other words:
```assembly
: DOUBLE DUP + ;
double_word:
    push r0
    la r0, do_docol_far
    jmp (r0)
    .word do_dup          ; call DUP primitive
    .word do_plus         ; call + primitive
    .word do_exit         ; return
```

---

### 8. Dictionary Structure

**Header layout** (4 + name_len bytes):
```
Offset  Content
+0      .word link         (3 bytes) — link to previous entry (0 = end)
+3      .byte flags_len    (1 byte) — bit7=IMMEDIATE, bit6=HIDDEN, bits0-5=name_len
+4      .byte c1..cN       (N bytes) — name characters
+4+N    (CFA follows immediately)
```

**Dictionary chain**: All words linked via LATEST pointer, searchable with FIND.

---

### 9. Key Primitives (Assembler-Level)

From `forth.s`, lines 162–485+:

- **EMIT** (entry_emit): Write char to UART (-65280) with TX busy-wait
- **KEY** (entry_key): Read char from UART with RX busy-wait
- **EXIT** (entry_exit): Pop IP from return stack
- **LIT** (entry_lit): Push inline literal from instruction stream
- **BRANCH** (entry_branch): Unconditional branch by signed offset
- **0BRANCH** (entry_zbranch): Branch if TOS is zero
- **+, *, -, AND, OR, XOR, =, <, 0=** (arithmetic/logic primitives)
- **DUP, DROP, SWAP, OVER, >R, R>, R@** (stack manipulators)
- **@, !, C@, C!** (memory load/store)
- **FIND, COMMA, CREATE, :, ;** (compile-time primitives)
- **INTERPRET, WORD, NUMBER** (text interpreter)
- **EXECUTE** (execute word at address)
- **HEX, DECIMAL, BASE** (number base control)
- **.S, DEPTH, WORDS, LED!, .** (debug/I/O)

All primitives follow the same structure: header → machine code with inlined NEXT.

---

### 10. Compile Mode (Phase 4c)

The interpreter (`do_interpret`) switches between two modes:

**Interpret mode** (STATE = 0):
- Words execute immediately
- Numbers push onto data stack

**Compile mode** (STATE ≠ 0, triggered by `:` word):
- Words compile their CFA (except IMMEDIATE words, which execute)
- Numbers compile as `LIT <value>`
- `;` compiles EXIT and returns to interpret mode

**Key compile words**:
- **:**: Calls CREATE (read word name, build header), writes 6-byte far CFA, sets STATE=1
- **;**: Marks IMMEDIATE; compiles EXIT, clears STATE, returns from definition
- **LIT**: Runtime helper; pushes next cell from instruction stream
- **COMMA** (`,`): Append cell at HERE, advance HERE by 3
- **C,**: Append byte

**do_interpret state machine** (from forth.s, line 1834+):
1. Call WORD → get counted string from UART
2. Call FIND → lookup in dictionary
3. If found: execute (STATE=0) or compile (STATE≠0, unless IMMEDIATE)
4. If not found: try NUMBER → push (STATE=0) or compile LIT (STATE≠0)
5. Loop until empty token

---

### 11. Project Structure

```
tf24a/
├── forth.s              (2,630 lines: single monolithic assembly)
├── examples/
│   ├── 00-smoke.fth     (basic interpret: 1 2 3 .S, HEX FF . DECIMAL)
│   ├── 01-colon.fth     (colon definitions: : TWO 2 ;)
│   ├── 02-led.fth       (LED control: : ON 1 LED! ;)
│   ├── 03-math.fth      (arithmetic: : NEGATE 0 SWAP - ;)
│   └── 04-stars.fth     (loops: : STARS STAR STAR STAR ;)
├── docs/
│   ├── compile.md       (detailed compile-mode design & pseudo-code)
│   ├── eli5-forth.md    (educational overview)
│   ├── inspect-stack-impl.md (ISA reference)
│   └── ...
├── demo.sh              (test harness: demo/repl/test modes)
├── CLAUDE.md            (critical guidance for AI agents: AgentRail protocol, ISA rules)
└── README.md            (empty)
```

---

### 12. Test Patterns & Strategy

**Three testing modes** (`demo.sh`):

1. **Interactive REPL** (`./demo.sh repl`):
   ```bash
   cor24-run --run forth.s --terminal --echo --speed 0
   ```
   Type Forth commands at UART, observe REPL output.

2. **Automated demo** (`./demo.sh demo`):
   Feeds 13 test cases via UART, extracts and displays output.

3. **Regression tests** (`./demo.sh test`):
   - 80+ per-word black-box tests
   - Check arithmetic, stack ops, stack balance, memory access, error handling
   - Uses `sed-rs` for portable text extraction (vs. GNU sed)
   - Golden-output comparison with `reg-rs` framework

**Stack leak testing** (mandatory for each new word):
```bash
cor24-run --run forth.s -u 'DEPTH .\nWORD\nDEPTH .\n' --speed 0 -n 10000000
# DEPTH before and after must match
```

**Test file examples** (from demo.sh):
```bash
check "1 ."           '1 .\n'           '1 ok'
check "2 3 + ."       '2 3 + .\n'       '5 ok'
check "HEX FF ."      'HEX FF . DECIMAL\n' 'FF ok'
check "DUP"           '5 DUP + .\n'     '10 ok'
check_led "1 LED!"    '0 LED!\n1 LED!\n' '01'
```

---

### 13. Assembly Source Files (.fth examples)

The `.fth` files are **plain Forth source** fed directly as UART input:

**00-smoke.fth** (basic interpret):
```forth
1 2 3 .S
2 3 + .
HEX FF . DECIMAL
WORDS
```

**01-colon.fth** (colon definitions):
```forth
: TWO 2 ;
: SQUARE DUP * ;
TWO .
5 SQUARE .
3 CUBE .
```

**03-math.fth** (arithmetic):
```forth
: NEGATE 0 SWAP - ;
: DOUBLE DUP + ;
: TRIPLE DUP DOUBLE + ;
5 NEGATE .
3 DOUBLE .
```

**Loading mechanism**: No special file format. Source is simply piped to UART:
```bash
cor24-run --run forth.s -u "$(cat example.fth)" --speed 0 -n 5000000
```

---

### 14. Key System Variables

Located at lines 2501–2508 in forth.s:

```assembly
var_here_val:     .word 0         ; next free compile address
var_latest_val:   .word 0         ; most recent dictionary entry
var_state_val:    .word 0         ; 0=interpret, nonzero=compile
var_base_val:     .word 10        ; number base (10=decimal, 16=hex)
```

Initialized at startup; updated by FIND, COMMA, STATE changes, etc.

---

### 15. UART I/O Interface

**Hardware registers** (COR24 emulator):
- **Data**: address -65280 (0xFF0100)
- **Status**: address -65279 (0xFF0101)
  - **TX busy**: bit 7 (sign bit when sign-extended)
  - **RX ready**: bit 0

**Polling pattern** (from EMIT):
```assembly
la r2, -65280       ; UART base
lb r0, 1(r2)        ; status (sign-extended)
cls r0, z           ; C = (status < 0) = TX busy
brt emit_poll       ; loop if busy
sb r0, 0(r2)        ; write byte
```

---

### 16. Critical Concepts from CLAUDE.md

**AgentRail Protocol** (mandatory for development):
- Step 1: `agentrail next` (read step prompt)
- Step 2: `agentrail begin` (mark as in-progress)
- Step 3: Work (implement per prompt)
- Step 4: `git commit` (save changes)
- Step 5: `agentrail complete` (record trajectory)

**TDD Rule**: Every new word MUST have a test before implementation.

**Common bugs to avoid**:
- Using `push r2` (data stack) instead of `add r1, -3; sw r2, 0(r1)` (return stack)
- `;` not marked IMMEDIATE → gets compiled instead of ending definition
- Literals compiled incorrectly during compile mode
- No EXIT compiled at end of colon word
- HERE misalignment (must advance in 3-byte units)
- Return stack discipline broken → nested colon calls fail

---

### 17. Git History & Phase Progression

From `git log`:
1. **Phase 1** (fd9f500): Bootstrap, UART I/O, stack primitives
2. **Phase 2** (0fa3e25): Inner interpreter, primitives, threaded tests
3. **Phase 3** (278f4a0): Dictionary headers, FIND, COMMA, compiler
4. **Phase 4** (98743ec): LED!, DOT, NUMBER, interpret-only QUIT
5. **Phase 4b** (cea6c97+): CR, SPACE, HEX, DECIMAL, DEPTH, .S, WORDS
6. **Phase 4c** (787e897): **Compile mode** to INTERPRET + `*` primitive + `.fth` examples

Latest: `33ecd06` (agentrail finalization)

---

### Summary

**tf24a is not a Rust project**—it's a **pure COR24 assembly implementation of a minimal Forth**. The "cor24-run assembler" is the external tool that compiles and executes the `.s` file. The Forth kernel itself is self-contained, hand-coded assembly using:

1. **DTC threaded execution** with IP/RS/DS management
2. **Dictionary-linked word entries** with flags, names, and CFAs
3. **Compile/interpret mode split** via STATE variable
4. **UART-based text interpreter** for interactive and file-fed input
5. **Strict COR24 ISA constraints**: 24-bit cells, 3-byte limits, register-specific load/ALU rules
6. **Test-driven development** with black-box UART output regression tests

All `.s` assembly and `.fth` Forth source files follow the documented syntax and semantics closely.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a complete understanding. Let me compile my findings.

## Comprehensive Exploration of tf24a: Tiny Forth for COR24

I've thoroughly explored the **tf24a** project at `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/tf24a/`. This is **not** a Rust project with cor24-rs dependencies—it's a **clean-room Direct Threaded Code (DTC) Forth implementation written entirely in COR24 assembly language**.

---

### 1. Project Overview

**tf24a** is a minimal, self-extending Forth kernel targeting the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA, implemented as a single assembly file (`forth.s`, 2,630 lines). Key characteristics:

- **Language**: COR24 assembly (not Rust)
- **Size**: Single monolithic `.s` file assembled and run via `cor24-run`
- **Architecture**: Direct Threaded Code (DTC) with a Forth text interpreter
- **Development Stage**: Phases 1-4 complete—bootstrap, threading, dictionary, compile mode, and UART-based REPL

---

### 2. How It Builds & Runs (cor24-run assembler)

The project does **not** use the cor24-rs library as a Rust dependency. Instead:

- **Assembler used**: `cor24-run --run forth.s [options]`
- **Invocation patterns** (from CLAUDE.md):
  ```bash
  cor24-run --run forth.s [opts]              # assemble and execute
  cor24-run --run forth.s --dump --speed 0    # dump machine state
  cor24-run --run forth.s --terminal --echo   # interactive REPL
  cor24-run --run forth.s -u 'input' --speed 0  # feed UART input
  ```
- **Testing**: Uses `reg-rs` regression framework for comparing UART output against golden baselines

---

### 3. Assembler API & COR24 ISA Constraints (from CLAUDE.md)

The assembly strictly follows COR24's **24-bit instruction set limitations**:

**Load destinations** (r0, r1, r2 only—NOT fp, sp):
```assembly
lc r0, imm8        ; load 8-bit constant
lcu r0, imm8       ; load 8-bit unsigned
la r0, imm24       ; load 24-bit address
lw r0, off(base)   ; load word
lb r0, off(base)   ; load byte (sign-extended)
lbu r0, off(base)  ; load byte (zero-extended)
```

**ALU destinations** (r0, r1, r2 only):
```assembly
add r0, r1         ; add register
add r0, imm8       ; add immediate (works for r0,r1,r2,sp)
sub, and, or, xor  ; similar constraints
```

**Unique fp/sp rules**:
- `fp` is **only** usable as a base register for indexing data stack memory
- `sp` cannot receive load results; use `mov fp, sp; push fp; pop r0` to read it
- **Cell size**: 3 bytes (24-bit), so `add r2, 3` advances by one cell
- **Branch offset**: ±127 bytes (signed 8-bit); far jumps use `la r0, label; jmp (r0)`

---

### 4. Assembler Syntax Rules

**COR24 Assembly specifics** (from CLAUDE.md):
- Labels on own line: `label:` (never inline `label: instr`)
- Comments: `;` only (not `#`)
- **Decimals only**: `la r0, -65280` not hex `0xFF0100`
- `.word label` emits 24-bit address (one label per directive)
- `.byte 72, 101, 108` for raw bytes (no string literals)
- No `.align` directive; manually pad with `.byte 0`

---

### 5. Architecture & Register Allocation (Frozen)

From `forth.s` header and CLAUDE.md:

```
r0 = W (work register / scratch)
r1 = RSP (return stack pointer, SRAM ~0x0F0000 growing down)
r2 = IP (instruction pointer for threaded code)
sp = DSP (data stack, hardware push/pop in EBR)
fp = scratch (limited: pop/push/add-as-source only; used as base register for memory ops)
```

**Return stack**: Separate from hardware call stack, grows down from 0x0F0000 in 3-byte cells.

---

### 6. How It Uses the Assembler API (DTC Inner Interpreter)

The core execution model is **Direct Threaded Code**. Every word execution follows this pattern:

**NEXT (inlined at tail of every primitive, 5 bytes)**:
```assembly
lw r0, 0(r2)    ; W = mem[IP] — fetch code address from thread
add r2, 3       ; IP += cell
jmp (r0)        ; execute code at CFA
```

**Two kinds of word entry points**:

1. **Near DOCOL** (for colon words close to do_docol, ~127B range):
   ```assembly
   do_docol:
       add r1, -3
       sw r2, 0(r1)        ; save IP on return stack
       mov r2, r0           ; r2 = CFA (from previous jmp)
       add r2, 3            ; r2 = PFA (skip 3-byte CFA)
       lw r0, 0(r2)
       add r2, 3
       jmp (r0)             ; NEXT
   ```

2. **Far DOCOL** (for distant definitions):
   ```assembly
   do_docol_far:
       add r1, -3
       sw r2, 0(r1)        ; save IP on return stack
       pop r2               ; r2 = CFA (pushed by 6-byte far CFA code)
       add r2, 6            ; r2 = PFA (CFA is 6 bytes for far calls)
       lw r0, 0(r2)
       add r2, 3
       jmp (r0)             ; NEXT
   ```

**Colon word CFA format decision**:
- **Near**: 3 bytes total: `bra do_docol; .byte 0` (if within 127B)
- **Far**: 6 bytes: `push r0; la r0, do_docol_far; jmp (r0)` (CFA saved on data stack)

**EXIT** (restore IP and continue):
```assembly
do_exit:
    lw r2, 0(r1)        ; restore IP from return stack
    add r1, 3
    lw r0, 0(r2)        ; NEXT
    add r2, 3
    jmp (r0)
```

---

### 7. Assembler API Usage in forth.s Source Files

Key implementation primitives with CFA+PFA structure:

**Primitive word entry** (e.g., EMIT):
```assembly
entry_emit:
    .word 0              ; link to previous (0 = chain end)
    .byte 4              ; flags + name length (lower 6 bits = len)
    .byte 69, 77, 73, 84 ; "EMIT"
do_emit:
    pop r0               ; r0 = character from data stack
    add r1, -3
    sw r2, 0(r1)        ; save IP on return stack
    ; ... UART TX polling logic ...
    lw r2, 0(r1)        ; restore IP
    add r1, 3
    lw r0, 0(r2)        ; NEXT
    add r2, 3
    jmp (r0)
```

**Colon word CFA template** (far format):
```assembly
cfa_template:
    .byte 125            ; push r0
    .byte 41             ; la r0 opcode
    .word do_docol_far   ; 3-byte address
    .byte 38             ; jmp (r0)
```

Then the body is a sequence of 24-bit cells pointing to other words:
```assembly
: DOUBLE DUP + ;
double_word:
    push r0
    la r0, do_docol_far
    jmp (r0)
    .word do_dup          ; call DUP primitive
    .word do_plus         ; call + primitive
    .word do_exit         ; return
```

---

### 8. Dictionary Structure

**Header layout** (4 + name_len bytes):
```
Offset  Content
+0      .word link         (3 bytes) — link to previous entry (0 = end)
+3      .byte flags_len    (1 byte) — bit7=IMMEDIATE, bit6=HIDDEN, bits0-5=name_len
+4      .byte c1..cN       (N bytes) — name characters
+4+N    (CFA follows immediately)
```

**Dictionary chain**: All words linked via LATEST pointer, searchable with FIND.

---

### 9. Key Primitives (Assembler-Level)

From `forth.s`, lines 162–485+:

- **EMIT** (entry_emit): Write char to UART (-65280) with TX busy-wait
- **KEY** (entry_key): Read char from UART with RX busy-wait
- **EXIT** (entry_exit): Pop IP from return stack
- **LIT** (entry_lit): Push inline literal from instruction stream
- **BRANCH** (entry_branch): Unconditional branch by signed offset
- **0BRANCH** (entry_zbranch): Branch if TOS is zero
- **+, *, -, AND, OR, XOR, =, <, 0=** (arithmetic/logic primitives)
- **DUP, DROP, SWAP, OVER, >R, R>, R@** (stack manipulators)
- **@, !, C@, C!** (memory load/store)
- **FIND, COMMA, CREATE, :, ;** (compile-time primitives)
- **INTERPRET, WORD, NUMBER** (text interpreter)
- **EXECUTE** (execute word at address)
- **HEX, DECIMAL, BASE** (number base control)
- **.S, DEPTH, WORDS, LED!, .** (debug/I/O)

All primitives follow the same structure: header → machine code with inlined NEXT.

---

### 10. Compile Mode (Phase 4c)

The interpreter (`do_interpret`) switches between two modes:

**Interpret mode** (STATE = 0):
- Words execute immediately
- Numbers push onto data stack

**Compile mode** (STATE ≠ 0, triggered by `:` word):
- Words compile their CFA (except IMMEDIATE words, which execute)
- Numbers compile as `LIT <value>`
- `;` compiles EXIT and returns to interpret mode

**Key compile words**:
- **:**: Calls CREATE (read word name, build header), writes 6-byte far CFA, sets STATE=1
- **;**: Marks IMMEDIATE; compiles EXIT, clears STATE, returns from definition
- **LIT**: Runtime helper; pushes next cell from instruction stream
- **COMMA** (`,`): Append cell at HERE, advance HERE by 3
- **C,**: Append byte

**do_interpret state machine** (from forth.s, line 1834+):
1. Call WORD → get counted string from UART
2. Call FIND → lookup in dictionary
3. If found: execute (STATE=0) or compile (STATE≠0, unless IMMEDIATE)
4. If not found: try NUMBER → push (STATE=0) or compile LIT (STATE≠0)
5. Loop until empty token

---

### 11. Project Structure

```
tf24a/
├── forth.s              (2,630 lines: single monolithic assembly)
├── examples/
│   ├── 00-smoke.fth     (basic interpret: 1 2 3 .S, HEX FF . DECIMAL)
│   ├── 01-colon.fth     (colon definitions: : TWO 2 ;)
│   ├── 02-led.fth       (LED control: : ON 1 LED! ;)
│   ├── 03-math.fth      (arithmetic: : NEGATE 0 SWAP - ;)
│   └── 04-stars.fth     (loops: : STARS STAR STAR STAR ;)
├── docs/
│   ├── compile.md       (detailed compile-mode design & pseudo-code)
│   ├── eli5-forth.md    (educational overview)
│   ├── inspect-stack-impl.md (ISA reference)
│   └── ...
├── demo.sh              (test harness: demo/repl/test modes)
├── CLAUDE.md            (critical guidance for AI agents: AgentRail protocol, ISA rules)
└── README.md            (empty)
```

---

### 12. Test Patterns & Strategy

**Three testing modes** (`demo.sh`):

1. **Interactive REPL** (`./demo.sh repl`):
   ```bash
   cor24-run --run forth.s --terminal --echo --speed 0
   ```
   Type Forth commands at UART, observe REPL output.

2. **Automated demo** (`./demo.sh demo`):
   Feeds 13 test cases via UART, extracts and displays output.

3. **Regression tests** (`./demo.sh test`):
   - 80+ per-word black-box tests
   - Check arithmetic, stack ops, stack balance, memory access, error handling
   - Uses `sed-rs` for portable text extraction (vs. GNU sed)
   - Golden-output comparison with `reg-rs` framework

**Stack leak testing** (mandatory for each new word):
```bash
cor24-run --run forth.s -u 'DEPTH .\nWORD\nDEPTH .\n' --speed 0 -n 10000000
# DEPTH before and after must match
```

**Test file examples** (from demo.sh):
```bash
check "1 ."           '1 .\n'           '1 ok'
check "2 3 + ."       '2 3 + .\n'       '5 ok'
check "HEX FF ."      'HEX FF . DECIMAL\n' 'FF ok'
check "DUP"           '5 DUP + .\n'     '10 ok'
check_led "1 LED!"    '0 LED!\n1 LED!\n' '01'
```

---

### 13. Assembly Source Files (.fth examples)

The `.fth` files are **plain Forth source** fed directly as UART input:

**00-smoke.fth** (basic interpret):
```forth
1 2 3 .S
2 3 + .
HEX FF . DECIMAL
WORDS
```

**01-colon.fth** (colon definitions):
```forth
: TWO 2 ;
: SQUARE DUP * ;
TWO .
5 SQUARE .
3 CUBE .
```

**03-math.fth** (arithmetic):
```forth
: NEGATE 0 SWAP - ;
: DOUBLE DUP + ;
: TRIPLE DUP DOUBLE + ;
5 NEGATE .
3 DOUBLE .
```

**Loading mechanism**: No special file format. Source is simply piped to UART:
```bash
cor24-run --run forth.s -u "$(cat example.fth)" --speed 0 -n 5000000
```

---

### 14. Key System Variables

Located at lines 2501–2508 in forth.s:

```assembly
var_here_val:     .word 0         ; next free compile address
var_latest_val:   .word 0         ; most recent dictionary entry
var_state_val:    .word 0         ; 0=interpret, nonzero=compile
var_base_val:     .word 10        ; number base (10=decimal, 16=hex)
```

Initialized at startup; updated by FIND, COMMA, STATE changes, etc.

---

### 15. UART I/O Interface

**Hardware registers** (COR24 emulator):
- **Data**: address -65280 (0xFF0100)
- **Status**: address -65279 (0xFF0101)
  - **TX busy**: bit 7 (sign bit when sign-extended)
  - **RX ready**: bit 0

**Polling pattern** (from EMIT):
```assembly
la r2, -65280       ; UART base
lb r0, 1(r2)        ; status (sign-extended)
cls r0, z           ; C = (status < 0) = TX busy
brt emit_poll       ; loop if busy
sb r0, 0(r2)        ; write byte
```

---

### 16. Critical Concepts from CLAUDE.md

**AgentRail Protocol** (mandatory for development):
- Step 1: `agentrail next` (read step prompt)
- Step 2: `agentrail begin` (mark as in-progress)
- Step 3: Work (implement per prompt)
- Step 4: `git commit` (save changes)
- Step 5: `agentrail complete` (record trajectory)

**TDD Rule**: Every new word MUST have a test before implementation.

**Common bugs to avoid**:
- Using `push r2` (data stack) instead of `add r1, -3; sw r2, 0(r1)` (return stack)
- `;` not marked IMMEDIATE → gets compiled instead of ending definition
- Literals compiled incorrectly during compile mode
- No EXIT compiled at end of colon word
- HERE misalignment (must advance in 3-byte units)
- Return stack discipline broken → nested colon calls fail

---

### 17. Git History & Phase Progression

From `git log`:
1. **Phase 1** (fd9f500): Bootstrap, UART I/O, stack primitives
2. **Phase 2** (0fa3e25): Inner interpreter, primitives, threaded tests
3. **Phase 3** (278f4a0): Dictionary headers, FIND, COMMA, compiler
4. **Phase 4** (98743ec): LED!, DOT, NUMBER, interpret-only QUIT
5. **Phase 4b** (cea6c97+): CR, SPACE, HEX, DECIMAL, DEPTH, .S, WORDS
6. **Phase 4c** (787e897): **Compile mode** to INTERPRET + `*` primitive + `.fth` examples

Latest: `33ecd06` (agentrail finalization)

---

### Summary

**tf24a is not a Rust project**—it's a **pure COR24 assembly implementation of a minimal Forth**. The "cor24-run assembler" is the external tool that compiles and executes the `.s` file. The Forth kernel itself is self-contained, hand-coded assembly using:

1. **DTC threaded execution** with IP/RS/DS management
2. **Dictionary-linked word entries** with flags, names, and CFAs
3. **Compile/interpret mode split** via STATE variable
4. **UART-based text interpreter** for interactive and file-fed input
5. **Strict COR24 ISA constraints**: 24-bit cells, 3-byte limits, register-specific load/ALU rules
6. **Test-driven development** with black-box UART output regression tests

All `.s` assembly and `.fth` Forth source files follow the documented syntax and semantics closely.

   153→For your first compiler, avoid:
   154→

   162→

   168→
   169→Those features create a disproportionate amount of compiler/runtime complexity.
   170→
   171→Clean-room strategy
   172→Your instinct is good: use existing Pascal systems for ideas and tests, not for porting.
   173→
   174→A good clean-room posture is:
   175→
   176→read language reports and public docs
   177→
   178→study high-level architecture of older Pascal compilers
   179→
   180→do not copy implementation code
   181→
   182→write your own grammar, AST, type system, runtime, and codegen
   183→
   184→use existing Pascal example programs only as black-box test inputs
   185→
   186→That gives you educational value and keeps the implementation genuinely yours.
   187→
   188→Best implementation strategy for your toolchain
   189→Since you already have C and assembler, I would do this:
   190→
   191→Stage A — host-side compiler in C
   192→Run on your current host machine.
   193→
   194→Outputs either:
   195→
   196→textual p-code / bytecode
   197→
   198→or textual assembly for your 24-bit ISA
   199→
   200→This lets you iterate quickly.
   201→
   202→Stage B — target runtime
   203→On your ISA:
   204→
   205→tiny startup
   206→
   207→memory map
   208→
   209→stack/heap model
   210→
   211→runtime support calls
   212→
   213→optional p-code interpreter
   214→
   215→Stage C — native backend
   216→Replace or complement the p-code interpreter with native code generation.
   217→
   218→Stage D — self-hosting subset
   219→Once the compiler is stable enough, rewrite the front end in Pascal itself and compile it with your earlier compiler.
   220→
   221→IR choice: stack machine vs register IR
   222→For Pascal, a stack IR is the easiest starting point.
   223→
   224→Why:
   225→
   226→expressions map naturally
   227→
   228→nested scopes can be handled without solving global register allocation early
   229→
   230→procedure calls are simpler
   231→
   232→p-code history gives you many good precedents
   233→
   234→A tiny IR might look like:
   235→
   236→LDC k load constant
   237→
   238→LD level, offset
   239→
   240→ST level, offset
   241→
   242→ADD SUB MUL DIV
   243→
   244→EQ NE LT LE GT GE
   245→
   246→JMP label
   247→
   248→JZ label
   249→
   250→CALL proc_id
   251→
   252→RET
   253→
   254→ENTER locals_size
   255→
   256→LEAVE
   257→
   258→LDI STI for indirect access
   259→
   260→IDX for array indexing
   261→
   262→FIELD offset

Phase 1: real Pascal block structure
program

declarations

scalar types: integer, boolean, char

arrays with fixed bounds

records

procedures/functions

lexical nesting

local variables

pass-by-value and var parameters

Phase 2: features that force runtime design
strings or packed char arrays

sets

pointers

heap

files

case statements

standard procedures/functions

Phase 3: optional/full language
ISO-ish cleanup

conformance tests

self-hosting subset

For your first compiler, avoid:

packed types

files

variant records

conformant arrays

procedural parameters

goto

real numbers

Those features create a disproportionate amount of compiler/runtime complexity.

Clean-room strategy
Your instinct is good: use existing Pascal systems for ideas and tests, not for porting.

A good clean-room posture is:

read language reports and public docs

study high-level architecture of older Pascal compilers

do not copy implementation code

write your own grammar, AST, type system, runtime, and codegen

use existing Pascal example programs only as black-box test inputs

That gives you educational value and keeps the implementation genuinely yours.

Best implementation strategy for your toolchain
Since you already have C and assembler, I would do this:

Stage A — host-side compiler in C
Run on your current host machine.

Outputs either:

textual p-code / bytecode

or textual assembly for your 24-bit ISA

This lets you iterate quickly.

Stage B — target runtime
On your ISA:

tiny startup

memory map

stack/heap model

runtime support calls

optional p-code interpreter

Stage C — native backend
Replace or complement the p-code interpreter with native code generation.

Stage D — self-hosting subset
Once the compiler is stable enough, rewrite the front end in Pascal itself and compile it with your earlier compiler.

IR choice: stack machine vs register IR
For Pascal, a stack IR is the easiest starting point.

Why:

expressions map naturally

nested scopes can be handled without solving global register allocation early

procedure calls are simpler

p-code history gives you many good precedents

A tiny IR might look like:

LDC k load constant

LD level, offset

ST level, offset

ADD SUB MUL DIV

EQ NE LT LE GT GE

JMP label

JZ label

CALL proc_id

RET

ENTER locals_size

LEAVE

LDI STI for indirect access

IDX for array indexing

FIELD offset

Then later map those to your 24-bit ISA.

The hardest design decisions you need early
These are the places where Pascal compilers get real:

1. Lexical nesting and static links
Pascal’s nested procedures are one of the biggest differences from C.

You need a decision for nonlocal access:

static link per frame

or display

or closure-like environment objects

For a first implementation, use static links. They are simpler and faithful to classic Pascal.

Each frame should contain:

return address

dynamic link

static link

locals

temporaries / spills if needed

2. Data layout on a 24-bit ISA
Decide up front:

size of integer

size of pointer

Open-source implementations worth studying
You asked specifically about liberal licenses.

Good for ideas / architecture
Pascal-P / Pascal-P5 is historically important and directly relevant because it is a portable p-code Pascal family. The Pascal-P5 project is presented as the fifth version of the Zurich Pascal-P compiler and emphasizes ISO 7185 compliance. 

Free Pascal is real, mature, and valuable to study conceptually, but its compiler itself is not BSD/MIT. Its FAQ says applications produced with its RTL are allowed to be proprietary because the RTL uses a modified LGPL exception, but that does not make the compiler source permissively licensed. 

Stanford Pascal is interesting because it still uses p-code plus an interpreter, and the repo says the compiler files are Pascal/P-Code while the interpreter is in C. I did not find an explicit permissive license on the GitHub page I checked, so I would treat it as “study carefully, license unclear until verified.” 

Small permissive educational repos
These are useful for architecture ideas, not for production-grade reuse:

JayPascal — MIT, Pascal compiler in C#. 

XDPC — MIT-0, small educational Pascal compiler with native x86 codegen. Very relevant as a “small compiler that directly emits native code” example, even though it targets DOS/x86. 

Qwerasdzxc/Pascal-Compiler — Apache-2.0, modular educational compiler with interpreter and C code generator. Architecturally useful because it separates lexer/parser/symbolizer/generator/runner. 

One caution: many permissively licensed Pascal repos on GitHub are student or educational compilers, not robust reference implementations. They are good for structure, testing ideas, and scope control, but not for blindly trusting semantics.

What I would personally avoid
For your project, I would avoid:

porting Turbo Pascal ideas too literally

implementing full ISO Pascal before subset stability

designing codegen before you define frame layout and static-scope access

adding real numbers early

building an optimizing compiler first

trying to self-host too soon

Suggested first deliverable
Your first usable milestone should be:

“Compile a small Pascal subset on the host into p-code, run it in a p-code VM on the emulator, and debug it with source-line stepping.”

That gives you:

proof of parsing

proof of semantic analysis

proof of runtime model

proof of debugger architecture

proof of target execution

before native code generation complexity arrives.

A concrete starting subset
I would start with exactly this grammar surface:

program

const

var

scalar integer/boolean

fixed arrays

procedures with parameters

function returning integer

begin/end

assignment

if/then/else

while/do

relational operators

arithmetic operators

writeln builtin

one-dimensional arrays only

That is enough to validate:

scopes

frames

calls

control flow

memory layout

debugger variable display

My recommendation in one sentence
Start with a clean-room Pascal-S/Pascal-P style subset in C, targeting a small p-code VM first, build the debugger metadata and frame model from day one, and only then add a native 24-bit code generator and eventual self-hosting.

If you want, I can turn this into a concrete architecture.md + milestone plan + test matrix + debugger metadata format tailored to your 24-bit ISA.


So I want to split this project into layers.  A low-level p-code interpreter and possibly run-time.  How is memory typically managed in Pascal, malloc/free or gc or other?  Pascal would be the higher-level tooling to compile to the p-code.  Other languaged that compile to p-code would follow.


Thought for 8s
You want the project split into layers, with a low-level p-code machine/runtime first, and then Pascal as one front end targeting that machine, with room for other languages later. That is a good direction.

It gives you:

a stable execution substrate,

a semantic reference model before native codegen,

a reusable toolchain for multiple languages.

The key design question now is the runtime model: stack, heap, globals, code, metadata, and how dynamic memory works.

Short answer on Pascal memory management
Classic Pascal was not generally garbage-collected. The usual model was:

stack allocation for locals, parameters, temporaries

static/global storage for globals

heap allocation for dynamically created structures via new

explicit reclamation, usually via dispose, or sometimes effectively “free everything at program end”

That is still the cleanest model for your first implementation. Standard Pascal and mainstream descendants historically center on explicit heap management rather than GC. Free Pascal’s docs and wiki still document New/Dispose and GetMem/FreeMem style management, and its heap debugging support tracks allocation/deallocation rather than automatic tracing GC. 

So for your first p-code runtime, I would recommend:

no GC initially

explicit heap operations

optional region/arena reset at process end

later, optional reference counting or tracing GC as a runtime extension for other languages

Best layering for your project
I would split the system like this:

Layer 0 — target substrate
Your existing pieces:

assembler

emulator

FPGA hardware

low-level monitor/debug hooks

native calling convention for your 24-bit ISA

This layer should know nothing about Pascal.

Layer 1 — p-code VM / abstract machine
This becomes the real foundation.

It should define:

instruction set

value representation

activation record/frame layout

globals model

heap model

runtime traps

debug metadata hooks

Think of this as your language-neutral machine.

Historically, Pascal p-code systems such as UCSD Pascal used a stack machine model, with a shared stack for expression evaluation and procedure frames. That is exactly the right family of design here. 

Layer 2 — base runtime services
This is not “Pascal runtime” yet. It is the minimal substrate any front end can rely on:

heap allocator

string/array block helpers

bounds-check failure handler

nil-pointer failure handler

divide-by-zero handler

assertion/trap support
