file.content:
I/O hooks

debug symbol and source mapping support

Layer 3 — Pascal-specific runtime
Only put Pascal-specific semantics here:

new, dispose

read, write, writeln

Pascal file model, if you add it later

set operations, if not in the VM

conformant-array helpers, if you ever add them

Pascal string helpers, if you extend beyond packed char arrays

Layer 4 — front ends
Start with:

Pascal compiler → p-code

Later:

tiny BASIC → p-code

structured Forth-like front end → p-code

tiny Lisp → p-code

maybe a C subset → p-code

That is a strong architecture because the VM becomes your shared semantic platform.

How memory is usually organized in a Pascal system
A practical memory map for your p-code machine would be:

+--------------------+ high memory
| debugger scratch   |
| VM bookkeeping     |
+--------------------+
| heap               |  grows upward
|                    |
+--------------------+
| free space         |
+--------------------+
| stack              |  grows downward
| call frames        |
| eval stack         |
+--------------------+
| globals/statics    |
+--------------------+
| constants/literals |
+--------------------+
| p-code / bytecode  |
+--------------------+ low memory
That is a very natural fit for an emulator and FPGA target.

Separate or unified stack?
You have two choices:

Option A — one unified stack
Used for:

procedure frames

expression temporaries

This matches classic p-code machine design well. UCSD p-Machine is described as using one shared stack for both procedure stack frames and instruction operands. 

Pros

simple VM

fewer pointers/registers

historically faithful

Cons

debugger is slightly harder

expression stack corruption can damage frames if your VM is buggy

less convenient for multiple languages with different evaluation models

Option B — call stack + eval stack
Separate:

call stack for frames

eval stack for p-code operands

Pros

cleaner debugger

better invariants

easier to support multiple front ends later

easier to reason about stack depth separately from frame layout

Cons

slightly more VM state

a bit less “classic”

For your project, I would choose separate stacks unless you specifically want historical UCSD-like fidelity. It makes debugging much easier.

Recommended runtime memory model
For a first clean-room system, I would use four storage classes.

1. Globals/statics
Allocated once when program loads.

Use for:

global variables

typed constants that need storage

runtime state

2. Stack
Used for:

parameters

locals

temporaries

return address / dynamic link / static link

This is where Pascal naturally lives for ordinary code.

3. Heap
Used for:

pointers created by new

dynamic records

dynamic nodes for trees/lists

later, strings or managed arrays if you choose

4. Code/metadata space
Used for:

bytecode / p-code

literal pools

source line tables

symbol tables for debugger

What should heap allocation look like?
For Pascal first version: explicit allocation
I recommend these VM/runtime operations:

HEAP_ALLOC size -> ptr

HEAP_FREE ptr

HEAP_ZERO ptr, size

optional HEAP_REALLOC only if another language later wants it

Pascal front end then maps:

new(p) → allocate size of pointed-to type, assign pointer

dispose(p) → free and optionally nil the pointer

maybe mark/release as a nonstandard extension later

That gives you a small, understandable runtime.

Should the heap be malloc/free style?
Yes, for the first version.

Even if the source language says new/dispose, under the hood that is basically a typed wrapper around an allocator API equivalent to malloc/free. Free Pascal’s documentation explicitly distinguishes Pascal-level New/Dispose from lower-level GetMem/FreeMem, which supports this layered interpretation. 

So the layering can be:

VM allocator: raw blocks

Pascal runtime: typed heap ops on top

That is clean.

Should Pascal use GC?
Not initially.

Reasons:

not historically typical for classic Pascal

adds runtime and debugger complexity

muddies semantics while you are still validating the language

not needed to get real Pascal programs working

You can add GC later if another language benefits from it more.

Good heap strategies for your VM
For your hardware and emulator, here are the realistic options.

Option 1 — bump allocator only
Allocate by moving a heap pointer upward.

No free except “reset all”.

Pros

tiny

perfect for bring-up

very easy to debug

Cons

cannot support true dispose

leaks until program end

Good for:

compiler bring-up

tests

short-running tools

Option 2 — free-list allocator
Maintain block headers and a free list.

Pros

simple enough to implement

supports dispose

realistic for Pascal

Cons

fragmentation

coalescing logic

debugger must understand headers

This is the best first “real” allocator.

Option 3 — segregated size classes / slab style
Useful later for managed runtimes or object-heavy languages.

Pros

fast

predictable

Cons

more complexity

overkill initially

Option 4 — tracing GC
Mark-sweep, copying, or generational.

Pros

future-proof for Lisp-like languages

fewer leaks in managed languages

Cons

root scanning complexity

interaction with stack maps

debugger complexity

moving GC complicates raw pointer semantics

Not for version 1.

My recommendation for your first allocator
Implement two modes:

Bring-up mode
bump allocator

no free

reset heap between program runs

Real mode
free-list allocator with headers

block size

free/used bit

maybe next/prev free links

optional coalescing on free

This gives you:

fastest path to first success

smooth transition to a usable Pascal runtime

Pascal-specific semantic model for memory
Pascal usually feels cleaner than C because most ordinary objects are not heap allocated.

Typical pattern:

local records/arrays: stack

globals: static

only pointer-based structures: heap

That means your front end should aggressively prefer stack/static placement and use heap only when the source says so.

Suggested mapping
Variables
local scalar/record/array → frame offset

global scalar/record/array → global segment offset

Parameters
by value → copied into frame or parameter area

var parameter → address passed in

Functions/procedures
frame contains locals and bookkeeping

static link for nested scopes

Pointers
24-bit pointer to heap object or global/static object

nil = 0 is simplest, unless address 0 is valid in your architecture

Suggested p-code machine operations for memory
I would define VM instructions along these lines.

Addressing/storage
LDG offset — load global

STG offset — store global

LDL offset — load local

STL offset — store local

LDA_LOCAL offset — push address of local

LDA_GLOBAL offset — push address of global

LOAD_IND size

STORE_IND size

Frame/nonlocal access
FOLLOW_STATIC depth

LDN depth, offset

STN depth, offset

LDA_NONLOCAL depth, offset

Heap
ALLOC size

FREE

MEMZERO size

MEMCOPY size

Checking
CHK_BOUNDS lo, hi

CHK_NIL

CHK_TAG if you later add tagged objects

TRAP code

Aggregate helpers
INDEX elem_size, low_bound

FIELD offset

This instruction set can support Pascal cleanly and still be reusable for other languages.

Should the runtime be Pascal-specific or generic?
You said other languages may follow. So keep the lower runtime generic.

Generic VM/runtime should own
raw allocation

raw memory movement

stack/frame model

trap handling

debug hooks

primitive I/O abstraction

Pascal front end/runtime should own
static typing rules

new / dispose

var parameter semantics

nested procedure semantics

Pascal built-ins

Pascal standard type semantics

That split lets you later add languages with different memory models.

For example:

Pascal → explicit allocation

Lisp → GC heap

BASIC → dynamic strings + simple reference counting

Forth-like language → manual dictionary arena

Same VM, different front-end/runtime layers.

If other languages will follow, what memory model should the VM expose?
A good neutral model is:

VM object kinds
Not source-language-visible, just runtime categories:

raw block

string block

array block

record/object block

closure/env block

runtime descriptor block

You do not need this all up front, but it is helpful to reserve the concept.

Minimal neutral API
At the VM/runtime boundary:

alloc raw block

free raw block

read/write primitive

copy/fill

query size

optional type tag in header

optional finalizer hook later

That is enough to host both manual-memory and managed-memory languages.

Debugger implications
If memory matters, design the allocator and frames so the debugger can introspect them.

  1332→Concrete recommendation on memory policy
  1333→If I were designing this for your 24-bit platform, I would do this:
  1334→
  1335→VM v1
  1336→separate call stack and eval stack

file.content:
Heap block header suggestion
Each allocated block could have:

struct HeapHeader {
    u24 size_words;
    u24 flags;
    u24 type_id;      // optional
    u24 prev_or_next; // depending on allocator design
}
Useful flags:

allocated/free

Pascal object

array

string

record

internal runtime block

maybe “poisoned/freed”

Then your debugger can show:

live heap blocks

free blocks

leaks on exit

block ownership/type

double free attempts

use-after-free if you poison blocks in debug mode

Frame record suggestion
Each call frame should expose:

return PC

previous frame pointer / dynamic link

static link

procedure id

locals base

eval stack base snapshot

That makes nested Pascal debugging much easier.

A practical staged plan
Phase 1 — define the VM first
Write documents for:

vm-isa.md

vm-memory.md

vm-abi.md

vm-debug.md

Do this before writing much code.

Phase 2 — build a tiny VM with no Pascal yet
Support:

constants

arithmetic

branches

calls

locals/globals

heap alloc/free

traps

line stepping

Then hand-write small p-code tests.

Phase 3 — add Pascal subset front end
Compile a tiny subset to p-code:

integers

booleans

assignment

if

while

procedures

arrays

records

new / dispose

Phase 4 — add debugger
Debugger should show:

p-code instruction stream

current line

frame chain

static chain

locals

globals

heap blocks

Phase 5 — let a second language target the same VM
This will force you to keep the VM generic rather than accidentally baking Pascal into it.

Concrete recommendation on memory policy
If I were designing this for your 24-bit platform, I would do this:

VM v1
separate call stack and eval stack

globals segment

heap with bump allocator in bring-up mode

free-list allocator in normal mode

no GC

explicit traps

pointer is 24-bit

debug headers on every heap block in debug builds

Pascal v1
no GC

new / dispose

stack for locals

globals for statics

heap only for pointers

optional leak report at process exit

runtime bounds checks enabled by default

Later multi-language extension
Add optional alternate heap managers:

manual heap

reference-counted heap

tracing GC heap

and let each front end choose one.

That avoids forcing Pascal into a GC design it does not need, while still leaving room for Lisp-like or scripting languages later.

Bottom line
For your layered architecture, I would treat Pascal as a typed front end over a generic p-code VM, and I would use explicit heap management, not GC, for the first runtime.

That means:

stack for locals and temporaries

globals for static storage

heap for pointer-based dynamic objects

new / dispose at the Pascal level

allocator API underneath that is basically malloc/free in spirit

GC only later, as an optional runtime mode for other languages

That is historically aligned with Pascal, technically simpler, and a better base for a debugger and for additional languages. 

I can turn this into a concrete vm-memory.md and pcode-opcodes.md draft next.


Okay, in order to write tests, I'd like to introduce a p-code-assembler. Does tha make sense?  It would be a subset of Pascal not sharing Pascal idioms, but enough to create Hello World, toggle LEDs, read/write UART.   It would have a simple calling convention, not support typing per-sed, or complex data structures.  The target ISA is integer only, so no floating point.  Does this make sense?

Yes — that makes a lot of sense.

In fact, for your project I think a p-code assembler is one of the best early moves, because it lets you separate three problems that otherwise get tangled together:

VM design

runtime / calling convention design

Pascal front-end correctness

If you skip the assembler, every early failure is ambiguous:

is the VM wrong?

is the runtime wrong?

is the code generator wrong?

is the parser wrong?

is the Pascal semantic analysis wrong?

A p-code assembler gives you a way to test the VM and runtime directly, with tiny hand-written programs.

Restating the architecture
You are proposing a layered system like this:

Layer A — p-code VM
Executes a small instruction set.

Layer B — p-code assembler
Human-writable source language for tests, demos, low-level runtime bring-up.

Layer C — runtime library / monitor bindings
UART, LED, memory, traps, maybe stack/heap helpers.

Layer D — Pascal compiler
Compiles Pascal into p-code.

Layer E — later front ends
Other languages also target the same p-code.

That is a very good architecture.

The p-code assembler becomes for your VM what assembly is for a real ISA:

a precise test tool

a bootstrap tool

a debugger input language

a way to write tiny runtime pieces before Pascal is ready

Why this is a strong idea
1. It gives you direct VM tests
You will want tests like:

push constants, add, print result

branch on zero

call/return

access locals/globals

follow static/dynamic links if you have them

allocate/free heap block

write UART

toggle LED

bounds trap

nil trap

These are much easier to express in a tiny assembly-like language than in Pascal.

2. It lets you bootstrap the runtime before Pascal exists
Before the Pascal compiler works, you still need:

startup

I/O shims

debug/trap handlers

maybe memory helpers

A p-code assembler lets you write these as VM-level programs or modules.

3. It clarifies the calling convention early
You said “simple calling convention.” Good.

The assembler forces you to define:

what a call frame looks like

argument passing

return values

local storage reservation

stack cleanup rules

That discipline helps both the VM and Pascal backend.

4. It gives you reproducible debugger cases
Debugger bring-up is hard if every test comes from a buggy compiler.

With p-asm, you can hand-write:

a three-function call chain

a recursive countdown

a loop with locals

a UART output routine

a deliberate trap

Then verify stepping, breakpoints, stack display, and memory inspection.

My recommendation: make it explicitly not Pascal
I agree with your instinct that it should not share Pascal idioms.

Do not try to make it “tiny Pascal.”

Make it look like a machine-oriented textual form.

That keeps the layers clean:

Pascal is a source language

p-asm is a target-language authoring tool

That distinction is healthy.

What the p-code assembler should be
Think of it as:

untyped or lightly typed

integer-only

explicit control flow

explicit loads/stores

explicit call frames

explicit memory operations

labels

directives for globals, constants, strings, imports/exports

It should feel closer to:

bytecode assembly

threaded-code assembly

simple VM macro assembly

than to Pascal or C.

Good scope for v1
You want enough to write:

Hello World

LED toggle

UART read/write

simple loops

simple function calls

stack and global tests

That suggests a very small feature set.

Source language features
labels

procedures/functions

constants

string literals

integer literals

comments

directives for globals/data

instructions

optional macros later

No need initially for
type declarations

structs

arrays in source syntax

records

source-level pointers

floating point

polymorphism

expression parsing beyond maybe trivial immediate syntax

A good design principle
The assembler should expose the VM’s semantics almost 1:1.

So if the VM has an instruction like:

PUSH 42

LOADG uart_status

JZ loop

CALL putc

RET

then the assembler should map directly to that.

Avoid “convenient” high-level sugar too early.

You want this tool mainly for:

clarity

testing

determinism

not productivity.

One key decision: stack machine vs explicit locals model
Since this is p-code, the natural design is a stack-oriented assembler.

Example style:

proc main
    push 72
    call putc
    push 105
    call putc
    push 10
    call putc
    halt
endproc
But for debugging and maintainability, I recommend you also support named local slots or explicit frame offsets.

For example:

proc blink locals 1
loop:
    push 1
    out led
    call delay
    push 0
    out led
    call delay
    jmp loop
endproc
or, more machine-like:

proc blink 1
loop:
    push 1
    storeg LED_PORT
    call delay
    push 0
    storeg LED_PORT
    call delay
    jmp loop
endproc
That is enough.

Suggested minimum p-code VM model

  1820→Calling convention recommendation
  1821→Since this is for bring-up and testing, make the convention brutally simple.
  1822→
  1823→Suggested convention
  1824→arguments are pushed on the eval stack
  1825→
  1826→call foo
  1827→
  1828→callee uses enter nlocals
  1829→
  1830→arguments become addressable relative to frame pointer
  1831→
  1832→return value is pushed on the eval stack before ret
  1833→
  1834→caller removes args if needed, or callee does — pick one and stick to it
  1835→
  1836→For simplicity, I would choose:
  1837→
  1838→Better v1 convention
  1839→caller pushes args
  1840→
  1841→call foo
  1842→
  1843→callee reads args at known negative offsets from fp
  1844→
  1845→callee leaves one return value on the eval stack, or none for procedures
  1846→
  1847→callee pops its frame only
  1848→
  1849→arguments are cleaned by callee or encoded in ret n
  1850→
  1851→A nice compact form is:
  1852→
  1853→ret 0 for no args
  1854→
  1855→ret 2 meaning pop 2 args and return
  1856→
  1857→or retv 2 meaning return value + pop 2 args
  1858→
  1859→But that may complicate the VM slightly.
  1860→
  1861→A simpler alternative:
  1862→
  1863→keep call frames separate from eval stack
  1864→
  1865→copy args into frame slots on call
  1866→
  1867→That is cleaner but a bit more machinery.
  1868→
  1869→For early work, either is fine. If you expect lots of debugging, a separate frame area is easier to reason about.
  1870→
  1871→For your use case, I would recommend two abstractions for I/O
  1872→Since you want UART and LED access, decide whether p-code talks to:
  1873→
  1874→Option A — VM-level abstract devices

  1876→
  1877→sys PUTC
  1878→
  1879→sys GETC
  1880→
  1881→sys LED_WRITE

  1884→
  1885→portable across host interpreter and real hardware
  1886→
  1887→easy to test
  1888→
  1889→easier debugger integration
  1890→
  1891→Cons
  1892→
  1893→less “metal”
  1894→
  1895→Option B — memory-mapped addresses

  1897→
  1898→push UART_DATA
  1899→
  1900→push 65
  1901→
  1902→storeb
  1903→
  1904→Pros
  1905→
  1906→closer to the hardware

  1909→

  1911→
  1912→less portable
  1913→
  1914→awkward for hosted tests
  1915→
  1916→slightly harder to evolve
  1917→
  1918→Best answer: support both
  1919→Use memory-mapped addresses in the runtime if you want, but also provide small VM/syscall abstractions for common tasks.
  1920→
  1921→For example:
  1922→
  1923→putc implemented as runtime p-asm
  1924→
  1925→inside it, either call sys PUTC on host, or do memory-mapped UART on target
  1926→
  1927→That keeps the assembler small and the runtime portable.
  1928→
  1929→Hello World / LED / UART are perfect early test cases
  1930→These are exactly the right kinds of tests.

  1969→A concrete source-language sketch
  1970→Something like this would work well:
  1971→
  1972→.const UART_DATA   0xFF0100
  1973→.const UART_STATUS 0xFF0101
  1974→.const LED_PORT    0xFF0200

  1976→.proc putc
  1977→    ; arg0 at fp-1
  1978→wait:

  1981→    push 1

  1984→
  1985→    push UART_DATA

  1990→
  1991→.proc puts

  1998→    loadarg 0

  2014→.end

file.content:
For your custom integer-only machine, I would keep the VM itself integer-only too.

Core VM state
pc — p-code instruction pointer

fp — frame pointer

sp — eval stack pointer

gp — globals base, or implicit globals segment

maybe hp — heap pointer if heap exists now

Core data model
one scalar word type: signed/unsigned 24-bit integer value

addresses are also word-sized

byte loads/stores handled explicitly if needed for UART and strings

That is enough for quite a while.

Suggested instruction categories
Stack / constants
push <imm>

dup

drop

swap

over

Arithmetic / logic
add

sub

mul

div

mod

neg

and

or

xor

not

Comparison
eq

ne

lt

le

gt

ge

These can push 0/1.

Control flow
jmp <label>

jz <label>

jnz <label>

call <proc>

ret

halt

trap <code>

Frame/local/global access
enter <nlocals>

leave

loadl <offset>

storel <offset>

loadg <symbol>

storeg <symbol>

addrg <symbol>

addrl <offset>

Indirect memory
load

store

loadb

storeb

Optional runtime
alloc <size>

free

sys <id> or hostcall <id>

You may not need heap yet, but having the slot reserved is useful.

Calling convention recommendation
Since this is for bring-up and testing, make the convention brutally simple.

Suggested convention
arguments are pushed on the eval stack

call foo

callee uses enter nlocals

arguments become addressable relative to frame pointer

return value is pushed on the eval stack before ret

caller removes args if needed, or callee does — pick one and stick to it

For simplicity, I would choose:

Better v1 convention
caller pushes args

call foo

callee reads args at known negative offsets from fp

callee leaves one return value on the eval stack, or none for procedures

callee pops its frame only

arguments are cleaned by callee or encoded in ret n

A nice compact form is:

ret 0 for no args

ret 2 meaning pop 2 args and return

or retv 2 meaning return value + pop 2 args

But that may complicate the VM slightly.

A simpler alternative:

keep call frames separate from eval stack

copy args into frame slots on call

That is cleaner but a bit more machinery.

For early work, either is fine. If you expect lots of debugging, a separate frame area is easier to reason about.

For your use case, I would recommend two abstractions for I/O
Since you want UART and LED access, decide whether p-code talks to:

Option A — VM-level abstract devices
Example:

sys PUTC

sys GETC

sys LED_WRITE

Pros

portable across host interpreter and real hardware

easy to test

easier debugger integration

Cons

less “metal”

Option B — memory-mapped addresses
Example:

push UART_DATA

push 65

storeb

Pros

closer to the hardware

fewer abstractions

Cons

less portable

awkward for hosted tests

slightly harder to evolve

Best answer: support both
Use memory-mapped addresses in the runtime if you want, but also provide small VM/syscall abstractions for common tasks.

For example:

putc implemented as runtime p-asm

inside it, either call sys PUTC on host, or do memory-mapped UART on target

That keeps the assembler small and the runtime portable.

Hello World / LED / UART are perfect early test cases
These are exactly the right kinds of tests.

Hello World tests
Validates:

string/data section handling

loops

loadb/storeb

call/return

UART output

LED toggle tests
Validates:

MMIO writes

loops

delay call structure

integration with emulator/FPGA

UART read/write
Validates:

polling loops

conditional branching

MMIO reads and writes

byte operations

Those will shake out most VM bugs quickly.

A concrete source-language sketch
Something like this would work well:

.const UART_DATA   0xFF0100
.const UART_STATUS 0xFF0101
.const LED_PORT    0xFF0200

.proc putc
    ; arg0 at fp-1
wait:
    push UART_STATUS
    loadb
    push 1
    and
    jz wait

    push UART_DATA
    loadarg 0
    storeb
    ret 1
.end

.proc puts
loop:
    loadarg 0
    loadb
    dup
    jz done
    call putc
    loadarg 0
    push 1
    add
    storearg 0
    jmp loop
done:
    drop
    ret 1
.end

.data msg "Hello, world!\n", 0

.proc main
    push msg
    call puts
    halt
.end
I am not saying this exact syntax is best, only that this level of abstraction is appropriate.

One subtle point: typed vs untyped
You said “not support typing per se.”

That is right for the assembler source language. But the VM still needs operational widths.

Even in an “untyped” p-code assembler, you will still need distinctions like:

word load/store

byte load/store

signed vs unsigned compare, maybe

address vs integer conventions, even if both are same-sized

So the source language can be untyped, but the instruction set should still be explicit enough to avoid ambiguity.

For example:

load = word

loadb = byte

lt = signed compare

ltu = unsigned compare if needed later

This will matter for UART, chars, string traversal, and array/string runtime work.

Should the assembler include macros?
Not initially.

For v1, I would keep it extremely small:

labels

constants

procedures

data/string directives

instructions

Then after the VM is stable, add:

.macro

.include

conditional assembly

symbolic frame slot aliases

Macros are helpful later, but they can hide VM design problems early.

What to test first
I would define a staged test suite.

Stage 1 — pure VM instruction tests
push/add/sub

dup/drop/swap

compare/branch

simple halt

Stage 2 — procedure tests
one call

nested calls

recursion

return value

locals

Stage 3 — memory tests
globals

indirect load/store

byte load/store

string walk

Stage 4 — device tests
UART output

UART polling input

LED on/off

blink loop

Stage 5 — error tests
bad opcode

stack underflow

invalid memory access

explicit trap

These tests should exist before Pascal codegen starts producing real programs.

This also helps your future debugger design
A p-code assembler pairs naturally with a debugger.

You can support debug info at two levels:

Level 1 — instruction/label level
current pc

current proc

label names

stack contents

frame contents

Level 2 — source mapping
p-asm source line to p-code address

later Pascal source line to p-code address

That means your debugger can be useful before Pascal exists.

This is a big win.

One thing I would avoid
I would avoid making the p-code assembler too much like a “portable assembly language for the target ISA.”

It should target the VM, not become a second real ISA project.

Otherwise you risk re-creating a complicated lower-level compiler target and losing the simplicity benefit.

So keep it:

stack-based

compact

procedural

explicit

test-friendly

My recommendation
Yes, introduce a p-code assembler now.

Make it:

machine-like, not Pascal-like

integer-only

stack-oriented

simple calling convention

labels + directives + instructions

enough for:

Hello World

LED toggle

UART I/O

loops

calls

globals

byte/word memory access

And use it for:

VM bring-up

runtime bring-up

debugger bring-up

regression tests

hand-authored demos

That is a very strong foundation for Pascal and later languages.

Recommended next concrete artifacts
Write these first:

pvm.md
VM state

memory layout

stack model

traps

pasm.md
syntax

directives

labels

procedures

calling convention

opcode-table.md
every opcode

operands

stack effect

semantics

trap conditions

tests/
hand-written .pasm test programs

expected stdout / memory / trap results

The best next step is probably to define the actual pasm syntax and the first 20–30 opcodes.

input.content:
# pv24a — Product Requirements Document

## Overview

**pv24a** is a p-code virtual machine (VM) targeting the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA.
It provides a language-neutral execution substrate for compilers (Pascal, Lisp,
BASIC, etc.) that emit stack-based p-code bytecode.

The VM runs on the COR24 emulator (`cor24-run`) and eventually on FPGA hardware.

## Goals

1. **Implement a p-code VM in COR24 assembly** — a stack-based interpreter that
   executes p-code bytecode loaded into memory.
2. **Implement a p-code assembler (pasm) in COR24 assembly** — a tool that
   translates human-readable `.pasm` source into p-code bytecode, used to create
   test programs and demos before any high-level compiler exists.
3. **Validate the VM with hand-written test programs** — Hello World, LED toggle,
   UART I/O, arithmetic, procedure calls, recursion, memory operations.
4. **Provide debugger-friendly metadata** — labels, procedure names, source line
   mappings, frame introspection support.

## Non-Goals (This Project)

- Pascal compiler (separate project, targets this VM)
- Other language front-ends (future projects)
- Native code generation / JIT
- Garbage collection
- Floating point
- Web UI debugger (separate project, like web-tf24a)
- Self-hosting

## Target Platform

- **ISA**: COR24 24-bit RISC (3 GPRs: r0/r1/r2, fp, sp, z, iv, ir)
- **Assembler/Emulator**: `cor24-run` from cor24-rs/cor24-bin
- **Memory**: 1 MB SRAM + 3 KB EBR stack + memory-mapped I/O
- **Cell size**: 3 bytes (24-bit words)
- **I/O**: UART at 0xFF0100/0xFF0101, LED at 0xFF0200

## Deliverables

### D1: P-Code VM (`pvm.s`)
- Stack-based p-code interpreter written in COR24 assembly
- Executes bytecode from a code segment in memory
- Supports: eval stack, call stack (separate), globals, heap (bump allocator)
- Traps for errors: stack overflow/underflow, invalid opcode, division by zero,
  nil pointer, bounds check failure
- ~30 core opcodes (see design.md)
- UART and LED I/O via sys instructions

### D2: P-Code Assembler (`pasm.s`)
- Two-pass assembler written in COR24 assembly
- Reads `.pasm` source text from UART input
- Emits p-code bytecode into a memory buffer
- Supports: labels, procedures, constants, string literals, integer literals,
  comments, directives (.const, .data, .proc/.end, .global)
- Machine-oriented syntax (not Pascal-like)
- Output: bytecode loadable by the VM

### D3: Test Suite (`tests/*.pasm`)
- Hand-written p-code assembly test programs
- Staged: arithmetic → control flow → procedures → memory → I/O → errors
- Run via `cor24-run` with UART input/output verification
- Regression testing pattern (golden output comparison)

### D4: Documentation
- `docs/architecture.md` — layered system architecture
- `docs/design.md` — VM state, opcodes, memory layout, calling convention, pasm syntax
- `docs/prd.md` — this document

## Success Criteria

1. VM executes Hello World (string output via UART)
2. VM executes recursive factorial
3. VM handles procedure calls with locals and arguments
4. VM traps on stack underflow and invalid opcode
5. pasm assembles all test programs correctly
6. LED blink demo runs on emulator
7. All tests pass via `cor24-run` with golden output comparison

## Dependencies

- `cor24-run` CLI (assembler + emulator) from ~/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs/cor24-bin
- COR24 ISA knowledge (instruction encoding, register constraints)
- Pattern reference: ~/github/sw-cli-tools/tf24a (DTC Forth in COR24 assembly)

## Constraints

- COR24 has only 3 GPRs (r0, r1, r2) — register pressure is high
- Branch offsets are ±127 bytes — far jumps need `la r0, label; jmp (r0)`
- No string literals in assembler — use `.byte` sequences
- Labels must be on their own line
- Comments use `;` only
- Integer-only (no floating point in ISA)
- Cell size is 3 bytes — all stack/frame offsets must be multiples of 3

- Pattern reference: ~/github/sw-cli-tools/tf24a (DTC Forth in COR24 assembly)

## Constraints

- COR24 has only 3 GPRs (r0, r1, r2) — register pressure is high
- Branch offsets are ±127 bytes — far jumps need `la r0, label; jmp (r0)`
- No string literals in assembler — use `.byte` sequences
- Labels must be on their own line
- Comments use `;` only
- Integer-only (no floating point in ISA)
- Cell size is 3 bytes — all stack/frame offsets must be multiples of 3

# pv24a — Architecture

## System Layers

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Layer 4: Language Front-Ends       │  (future, separate projects)
│  Pascal compiler → .pasm / bytecode │
│  Lisp, BASIC, etc.                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 3: Language-Specific Runtime  │  (future, separate projects)
│  Pascal: new/dispose, writeln, sets │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 2: Base Runtime Services     │  ← this project (runtime portion)
│  Heap allocator, bounds checking,   │
│  nil/div-zero traps, I/O stubs      │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 1: P-Code VM                 │  ← this project (core)
│  Stack machine interpreter          │
│  Eval stack + call stack + globals  │
│  ~30 opcodes, frame model, traps    │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 0: Target Substrate          │  (existing, external)
│  COR24 ISA, cor24-run assembler,    │
│  emulator, FPGA hardware            │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**This project (pv24a)** implements Layers 1 and 2, plus a p-code assembler
tool for testing.

## Component Map

```
pv24a/
├── pvm.s              P-Code VM interpreter (Layer 1)
├── runtime.s          Base runtime services (Layer 2)
├── pasm.s             P-Code assembler tool
├── tests/
│   ├── t01-arith.pasm     Arithmetic tests
│   ├── t02-stack.pasm     Stack operation tests
│   ├── t03-branch.pasm    Control flow tests
│   ├── t04-proc.pasm      Procedure call tests
│   ├── t05-memory.pasm    Memory access tests
│   ├── t06-uart.pasm      UART I/O tests
│   ├── t07-led.pasm       LED tests
│   ├── t08-recurse.pasm   Recursion tests
│   ├── t09-heap.pasm      Heap allocation tests
│   └── t10-traps.pasm     Error/trap tests
├── examples/
│   ├── hello.pasm         Hello World
│   ├── blink.pasm         LED blink
│   └── echo.pasm          UART echo
├── docs/
│   ├── prd.md
│   ├── architecture.md
│   ├── design.md
│   └── research.txt
└── demo.sh                Test harness script
```

## Memory Map (COR24 address space)

```
0x000000 ┌──────────────────────┐
         │ COR24 boot / entry   │  cor24-run entry point
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ P-Code VM code       │  pvm.s assembled COR24 instructions
         │ (interpreter loop,   │
         │  opcode dispatch,    │
         │  runtime services)   │
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ P-Code bytecode      │  loaded/assembled p-code program
         │ (code segment)       │
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ Constants / strings  │  literal pool
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ Globals segment      │  p-code global variables
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ Call stack            │  frames: ret-pc, dyn-link, static-link,
         │ (grows upward)       │  proc-id, locals
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ Eval stack            │  expression temporaries
         │ (grows upward)       │  (separate from call stack)
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ Free space            │
         ├──────────────────────┤
         │ Heap                  │  dynamic allocation (grows upward)
         │ (bump allocator v1)  │
         ├──────────────────────┤
0x0FFFFF │ End of SRAM           │
         ├──────────────────────┤
0xFEEC00 │ EBR (3KB)             │  COR24 hardware stack (sp)
         │                      │  Used as scratch / COR24-level stack
0xFEF7FF └──────────────────────┘
0xFF0100   UART data register
0xFF0101   UART status register
0xFF0200   LED port
```

## Register Allocation (COR24 → VM)

The COR24 has very few registers. For the VM interpreter:

| COR24 Reg | VM Role         | Notes |
|-----------|-----------------|-------|
| r0        | W (work/scratch)| General scratch, opcode dispatch |
| r1        | VM state ptr    | Points to VM state struct in memory |
| r2        | Scratch #2      | Secondary work register |
| sp        | COR24 stack     | Hardware push/pop (EBR), used sparingly |
| fp        | Memory base     | Used for indexed loads/stores |

VM state (pc, esp, csp, gp, hp, etc.) lives in a memory struct because
COR24 doesn't have enough registers to hold it all.

## Execution Model

### Interpreter Loop (fetch-decode-execute)

```
vm_loop:
    ; fetch opcode byte from p-code[pc]
    ; pc += 1
    ; decode: index into dispatch table
    ; jump to handler
    ; handler executes, modifies VM state
    ; jump back to vm_loop
```

### Dual Stack Architecture

- **Eval stack**: expression temporaries, pushed/popped by arithmetic and
  load/store instructions. Grows upward in SRAM.
- **Call stack**: procedure activation records (frames). Grows upward in
  a separate SRAM region. Each frame contains:
  - Return PC (p-code address)
  - Dynamic link (previous frame pointer)
  - Static link (lexically enclosing frame, for nested scopes)
  - Procedure ID (for debugger)
  - Local variable slots

Separate stacks make debugging much easier and prevent eval stack corruption
from damaging frame data.

### Opcode Encoding

P-code instructions are variable-length byte sequences:

| Format | Bytes | Example |
|--------|-------|---------|
| op     | 1     | `add`, `dup`, `ret` |
| op imm8 | 2   | `push_small 42` |
| op imm24 | 4  | `push 100000`, `jmp label` |
| op u8 u8 | 3  | `loadl 2` (local at offset 2) |

## I/O Model

The VM provides two I/O paths:

1. **sys instructions** — abstract device calls (portable)
   - `sys PUTC` — write byte to output
   - `sys GETC` — read byte from input
   - `sys LED` — write LED state
   - `sys HALT` — stop execution

2. **Memory-mapped I/O** — direct UART/LED access via load/store
   - Available but discouraged for portable p-code programs
   - Runtime library wraps MMIO behind sys calls

## Build & Test

```bash
# Assemble and run VM with a test program
cor24-run --run pvm.s -u "$(cat tests/t01-arith.pasm)" --speed 0 -n 5000000

# Or: assemble pasm source, then run VM with bytecode
# (exact mechanism TBD — may use UART pipe or preloaded memory)

# Interactive mode
cor24-run --run pvm.s --terminal --echo

# Regression test
./demo.sh test
```

## Relationship to Other Projects

| Project | Role | Location |
|---------|------|----------|
| cor24-rs/cor24-bin | COR24 assembler + emulator | ~/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs |
| tf24a | Pattern reference (Forth in COR24 asm) | ~/github/sw-cli-tools/tf24a |
| pv24a | **This project** — P-Code VM + pasm | ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/pv24a |
| (future) | Pascal compiler → p-code | TBD separate project |
| (future) | Web debugger UI (Yew/WASM) | TBD separate project |

input.content:
# pv24a — Design Document

## 1. VM State

The p-code VM maintains the following state in a memory struct:

```
vm_state:
    .word 0     ; pc   — p-code instruction pointer
    .word 0     ; esp  — eval stack pointer (top of eval stack)
    .word 0     ; csp  — call stack pointer (top of call stack)
    .word 0     ; fp   — current frame pointer (call stack)
    .word 0     ; gp   — globals base address
    .word 0     ; hp   — heap pointer (next free)
    .word 0     ; code — base address of p-code segment
    .word 0     ; status — 0=running, 1=halted, 2=trapped
    .word 0     ; trap_code — last trap reason
```

## 2. P-Code Instruction Set

### 2.1 Stack / Constants

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `push` imm24 | 4 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push 24-bit immediate |
| `push_s` imm8 | 2 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push sign-extended 8-bit immediate |
| `dup` | 1 byte | ( a -- a a ) | Duplicate top |
| `drop` | 1 byte | ( a -- ) | Discard top |
| `swap` | 1 byte | ( a b -- b a ) | Swap top two |
| `over` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a b a ) | Copy second to top |

### 2.2 Arithmetic / Logic

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `add` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a+b ) | Add |
| `sub` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a-b ) | Subtract |
| `mul` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a*b ) | Multiply |
| `div` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a/b ) | Divide (trap on b=0) |
| `mod` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a%b ) | Modulo (trap on b=0) |
| `neg` | 1 byte | ( a -- -a ) | Negate |
| `and` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a&b ) | Bitwise AND |
| `or` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a\|b ) | Bitwise OR |
| `xor` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a^b ) | Bitwise XOR |
| `not` | 1 byte | ( a -- ~a ) | Bitwise NOT |
| `shl` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a<<n ) | Shift left |
| `shr` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a>>n ) | Arithmetic shift right |

### 2.3 Comparison

All comparisons push 1 (true) or 0 (false):

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `eq` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Equal |
| `ne` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Not equal |
| `lt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less than (signed) |
| `le` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less or equal (signed) |
| `gt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater than (signed) |
| `ge` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater or equal (signed) |

### 2.4 Control Flow

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `jmp` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( -- ) | Unconditional jump |
| `jz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if zero |
| `jnz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if nonzero |
| `call` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call procedure |
| `ret` nargs8 | 2 bytes | ( [retval] -- [retval] ) | Return, clean nargs |
| `halt` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Stop VM |
| `trap` code8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Trigger trap |

### 2.5 Local / Global / Nonlocal Access

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `enter` nlocals8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Set up frame, reserve N local slots |
| `leave` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Tear down frame |
| `loadl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load local at offset |
| `storel` off8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store local at offset |
| `loadg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load global at offset |
| `storeg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store global at offset |
| `addrl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of local |
| `addrg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of global |
| `loadn` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load nonlocal via static link chain |
| `storen` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store nonlocal via static link chain |

### 2.6 Indirect Memory Access

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `load` | 1 byte | ( addr -- val ) | Load word from address |
| `store` | 1 byte | ( val addr -- ) | Store word to address |
| `loadb` | 1 byte | ( addr -- byte ) | Load byte (zero-extended) |
| `storeb` | 1 byte | ( byte addr -- ) | Store byte |

### 2.7 System / I/O

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `sys` id8 | 2 bytes | (varies) | System call |

System call IDs:

| ID | Name | Stack Effect | Description |
|----|------|-------------|-------------|
| 0 | HALT | ( -- ) | Stop execution |
| 1 | PUTC | ( char -- ) | Write byte to UART |
| 2 | GETC | ( -- char ) | Read byte from UART (blocking) |
| 3 | LED | ( state -- ) | Write LED state |
| 4 | ALLOC | ( size -- ptr ) | Bump-allocate heap block |
| 5 | FREE | ( ptr -- ) | Free heap block (no-op in bump mode) |

### 2.8 Argument Access

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `loada` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load argument by index |
| `storea` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store argument by index |

## 3. Call Frame Layout

When `call` executes, a new frame is pushed onto the call stack:

```
         ┌──────────────────┐  ← new csp (after enter)
         │ local[N-1]       │  offset = +(N-1)*3 from fp
         │ ...              │
         │ local[0]         │  offset = 0 from fp
fp ───→  ├──────────────────┤
         │ procedure ID     │  offset = -3
         │ static link      │  offset = -6  (lexically enclosing frame)
         │ dynamic link     │  offset = -9  (caller's fp)
         │ return PC        │  offset = -12
         ├──────────────────┤
         │ arg[N-1]         │  offset = -15  (from fp)
         │ ...              │
         │ arg[0]           │  offset = -15 - (N-1)*3
         └──────────────────┘
```

- **Caller** pushes arguments onto eval stack, then `call addr`
- **call** instruction: pops args from eval stack into call stack frame header,
  pushes return PC, dynamic link, static link, proc ID
- **enter N**: advances csp by N*3 to reserve local slots, sets fp
- **leave**: restores fp to dynamic link, restores csp
- **ret N**: pushes return value (if any) to eval stack, pops frame, cleans N args

## 4. Calling Convention

Simple and explicit:

1. Caller pushes arguments left-to-right onto eval stack
2. Caller executes `call <proc_addr>`
3. Callee executes `enter <nlocals>` (reserves local slots)
4. Callee accesses args via `loada 0`, `loada 1`, etc.
5. Callee accesses locals via `loadl 0`, `loadl 1`, etc.
6. For functions: callee pushes return value onto eval stack
7. Callee executes `ret <nargs>` (cleans args from frame)
8. Caller finds return value (if any) on eval stack

## 5. P-Code Assembler (pasm) Syntax

### 5.1 Source Format

```
; Comment (semicolon to end of line)

.const UART_DATA  -65280      ; named constant
.const UART_STATUS -65279
.const LED_PORT   -65024

.global counter 1             ; 1 word of global storage
.global buffer 32             ; 32 words

.data msg 72, 101, 108, 108, 111, 0   ; "Hello\0" as bytes

.proc main 0                 ; procedure, 0 locals
    push msg                  ; push address of msg
    call puts
    halt
.end

.proc puts 1                 ; 1 local (loop pointer)
    loada 0                   ; arg0 = string address
    storel 0                  ; local0 = current ptr
loop:
    loadl 0
    loadb                     ; load byte at ptr
    dup
    jz done                   ; if zero, done
    sys 1                     ; PUTC
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0                  ; ptr++
    jmp loop
done:
    drop                      ; drop the zero
    ret 1                     ; return, clean 1 arg
.end
```

### 5.2 Directives

| Directive | Syntax | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| `.const` | `.const NAME value` | Define named constant |
| `.global` | `.global NAME nwords` | Reserve global storage |
| `.data` | `.data NAME byte, byte, ...` | Define byte data (string/array) |
| `.proc` | `.proc NAME nlocals` | Begin procedure (nlocals = local slot count) |
| `.end` | `.end` | End procedure |

### 5.3 Labels

Labels appear on their own line, ending with `:`:

```
loop:
    ; instructions...
    jmp loop
```

Labels are local to the enclosing `.proc` / `.end` block.

### 5.4 Integer Literals

- Decimal: `42`, `-1`, `0`
- Hex: `0xFF` (if supported) or decimal equivalents
- Named constants: `UART_DATA` (substituted by assembler)

### 5.5 Instructions

One instruction per line. Operand follows mnemonic separated by whitespace:

```
push 42
loadl 0
jz done
call puts
sys 1
ret 0
```

## 6. Opcode Encoding Table

Opcodes are assigned sequential byte values:

| Byte | Mnemonic | Operand |
|------|----------|---------|
| 0x00 | halt | — |
| 0x01 | push | imm24 |
| 0x02 | push_s | imm8 |
| 0x03 | dup | — |
| 0x04 | drop | — |
| 0x05 | swap | — |
| 0x06 | over | — |
| 0x10 | add | — |
| 0x11 | sub | — |
| 0x12 | mul | — |
| 0x13 | div | — |
| 0x14 | mod | — |
| 0x15 | neg | — |
| 0x16 | and | — |
| 0x17 | or | — |
| 0x18 | xor | — |
| 0x19 | not | — |
| 0x1A | shl | — |
| 0x1B | shr | — |
| 0x20 | eq | — |
| 0x21 | ne | — |
| 0x22 | lt | — |
| 0x23 | le | — |
| 0x24 | gt | — |
| 0x25 | ge | — |
| 0x30 | jmp | addr24 |
| 0x31 | jz | addr24 |
| 0x32 | jnz | addr24 |
| 0x33 | call | addr24 |
| 0x34 | ret | nargs8 |
| 0x35 | trap | code8 |
| 0x40 | enter | nlocals8 |
| 0x41 | leave | — |
| 0x42 | loadl | off8 |
| 0x43 | storel | off8 |
| 0x44 | loadg | off24 |
| 0x45 | storeg | off24 |
| 0x46 | addrl | off8 |
| 0x47 | addrg | off24 |
| 0x48 | loada | idx8 |
| 0x49 | storea | idx8 |
| 0x4A | loadn | depth8 off8 |
| 0x4B | storen | depth8 off8 |
| 0x50 | load | — |
| 0x51 | store | — |
| 0x52 | loadb | — |
| 0x53 | storeb | — |
| 0x60 | sys | id8 |

Reserved ranges 0x70–0xFF for future opcodes (heap, debug, extended ops).

## 7. Trap Codes

| Code | Name | Trigger |
|------|------|---------|
| 0 | USER_TRAP | `trap 0` instruction |
| 1 | DIV_ZERO | Division/modulo by zero |
| 2 | STACK_OVERFLOW | Eval or call stack exceeds limit |
| 3 | STACK_UNDERFLOW | Eval stack pop when empty |
| 4 | INVALID_OPCODE | Unknown opcode byte |
| 5 | INVALID_ADDRESS | Memory access out of bounds |
| 6 | NIL_POINTER | Dereference of address 0 |
| 7 | BOUNDS_CHECK | Array index out of bounds |

On trap: VM sets status=2, trap_code=N, halts. Diagnostic message to UART.

## 8. Memory Segment Layout

At VM initialization:

1. **Code segment** starts at a fixed base address (after VM code)
2. **Constants/data** follows code (emitted by pasm)
3. **Globals** follow data (allocated per `.global` directives)
4. **Call stack** starts after globals, grows upward
5. **Eval stack** starts after call stack region, grows upward
6. **Heap** starts after eval stack region, grows upward
7. Segment base addresses stored in VM state struct

Sizes are configurable at VM init time. Default allocations:
- Call stack: 4 KB
- Eval stack: 2 KB
- Heap: remaining SRAM up to ~900 KB

## 9. Implementation Strategy

### Phase 1: Minimal VM
- Opcode dispatch loop
- push, dup, drop, add, sub, halt
- UART output via sys PUTC
- Hardcoded test bytecode (no pasm yet)

### Phase 2: Control Flow + Procedures
- jmp, jz, jnz
- call, ret, enter, leave
- loadl, storel, loada, storea
- Frame management

### Phase 3: Full Instruction Set
- All arithmetic/logic/comparison opcodes
- Globals (loadg, storeg)
- Indirect memory (load, store, loadb, storeb)
- Nonlocal access (loadn, storen)
- All sys calls

### Phase 4: P-Code Assembler
- Lexer for pasm syntax (UART input)
- Two-pass assembly (collect labels, emit bytes)
- Directive handling (.const, .global, .data, .proc/.end)
- Output bytecode to memory buffer

### Phase 5: Integration & Test Suite
- pasm assembles test programs
- VM executes assembled bytecode
- Regression test harness (demo.sh)
- Full test coverage per staged plan

## 10. COR24 Assembly Patterns

Reference patterns from tf24a for common idioms:

### Dispatch Table
```asm
; Index into jump table by opcode byte
; r0 = opcode
la r2, dispatch_table
; multiply r0 by 3 (entry size = .word = 3 bytes)
; ... load target address, jmp
```

### UART Output
```asm
; r0 = character to send
la r2, -65280       ; UART base
tx_wait:
    lb r0, 1(r2)   ; status (sign-extended)
    cls r0, z       ; C = (status < 0) = TX busy
    brt tx_wait
    sb r0, 0(r2)   ; write byte
```

### Memory Struct Access
```asm
; Load vm_state.pc into r0
la fp, vm_state
lw r0, 0(fp)       ; pc at offset 0
```

toolUseResult.content:
# pv24a — Design Document

## 1. VM State

The p-code VM maintains the following state in a memory struct:

```
vm_state:
    .word 0     ; pc   — p-code instruction pointer
    .word 0     ; esp  — eval stack pointer (top of eval stack)
    .word 0     ; csp  — call stack pointer (top of call stack)
    .word 0     ; fp   — current frame pointer (call stack)
    .word 0     ; gp   — globals base address
    .word 0     ; hp   — heap pointer (next free)
    .word 0     ; code — base address of p-code segment
    .word 0     ; status — 0=running, 1=halted, 2=trapped
    .word 0     ; trap_code — last trap reason
```

## 2. P-Code Instruction Set

### 2.1 Stack / Constants

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `push` imm24 | 4 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push 24-bit immediate |
| `push_s` imm8 | 2 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push sign-extended 8-bit immediate |
| `dup` | 1 byte | ( a -- a a ) | Duplicate top |
| `drop` | 1 byte | ( a -- ) | Discard top |
| `swap` | 1 byte | ( a b -- b a ) | Swap top two |
| `over` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a b a ) | Copy second to top |

### 2.2 Arithmetic / Logic

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `add` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a+b ) | Add |
| `sub` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a-b ) | Subtract |
| `mul` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a*b ) | Multiply |
| `div` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a/b ) | Divide (trap on b=0) |
| `mod` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a%b ) | Modulo (trap on b=0) |
| `neg` | 1 byte | ( a -- -a ) | Negate |
| `and` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a&b ) | Bitwise AND |
| `or` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a\|b ) | Bitwise OR |
| `xor` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a^b ) | Bitwise XOR |
| `not` | 1 byte | ( a -- ~a ) | Bitwise NOT |
| `shl` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a<<n ) | Shift left |
| `shr` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a>>n ) | Arithmetic shift right |

### 2.3 Comparison

All comparisons push 1 (true) or 0 (false):

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `eq` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Equal |
| `ne` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Not equal |
| `lt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less than (signed) |
| `le` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less or equal (signed) |
| `gt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater than (signed) |
| `ge` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater or equal (signed) |

### 2.4 Control Flow

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `jmp` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( -- ) | Unconditional jump |
| `jz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if zero |
| `jnz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if nonzero |
| `call` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call procedure |
| `ret` nargs8 | 2 bytes | ( [retval] -- [retval] ) | Return, clean nargs |
| `halt` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Stop VM |
| `trap` code8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Trigger trap |

### 2.5 Local / Global / Nonlocal Access

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `enter` nlocals8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Set up frame, reserve N local slots |
| `leave` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Tear down frame |
| `loadl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load local at offset |
| `storel` off8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store local at offset |
| `loadg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load global at offset |
| `storeg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store global at offset |
| `addrl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of local |
| `addrg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of global |
| `loadn` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load nonlocal via static link chain |
| `storen` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store nonlocal via static link chain |

### 2.6 Indirect Memory Access

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `load` | 1 byte | ( addr -- val ) | Load word from address |
| `store` | 1 byte | ( val addr -- ) | Store word to address |
| `loadb` | 1 byte | ( addr -- byte ) | Load byte (zero-extended) |
| `storeb` | 1 byte | ( byte addr -- ) | Store byte |

### 2.7 System / I/O

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `sys` id8 | 2 bytes | (varies) | System call |

System call IDs:

| ID | Name | Stack Effect | Description |
|----|------|-------------|-------------|
| 0 | HALT | ( -- ) | Stop execution |
| 1 | PUTC | ( char -- ) | Write byte to UART |
| 2 | GETC | ( -- char ) | Read byte from UART (blocking) |
| 3 | LED | ( state -- ) | Write LED state |
| 4 | ALLOC | ( size -- ptr ) | Bump-allocate heap block |
| 5 | FREE | ( ptr -- ) | Free heap block (no-op in bump mode) |

### 2.8 Argument Access

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `loada` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load argument by index |
| `storea` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store argument by index |

## 3. Call Frame Layout

When `call` executes, a new frame is pushed onto the call stack:

```
         ┌──────────────────┐  ← new csp (after enter)
         │ local[N-1]       │  offset = +(N-1)*3 from fp
         │ ...              │
         │ local[0]         │  offset = 0 from fp
fp ───→  ├──────────────────┤
         │ procedure ID     │  offset = -3
         │ static link      │  offset = -6  (lexically enclosing frame)
         │ dynamic link     │  offset = -9  (caller's fp)
         │ return PC        │  offset = -12
         ├──────────────────┤
         │ arg[N-1]         │  offset = -15  (from fp)
         │ ...              │
         │ arg[0]           │  offset = -15 - (N-1)*3
         └──────────────────┘
```

- **Caller** pushes arguments onto eval stack, then `call addr`
- **call** instruction: pops args from eval stack into call stack frame header,
  pushes return PC, dynamic link, static link, proc ID
- **enter N**: advances csp by N*3 to reserve local slots, sets fp
- **leave**: restores fp to dynamic link, restores csp
- **ret N**: pushes return value (if any) to eval stack, pops frame, cleans N args

## 4. Calling Convention

Simple and explicit:

1. Caller pushes arguments left-to-right onto eval stack
2. Caller executes `call <proc_addr>`
3. Callee executes `enter <nlocals>` (reserves local slots)
4. Callee accesses args via `loada 0`, `loada 1`, etc.
5. Callee accesses locals via `loadl 0`, `loadl 1`, etc.
6. For functions: callee pushes return value onto eval stack
7. Callee executes `ret <nargs>` (cleans args from frame)
8. Caller finds return value (if any) on eval stack

## 5. P-Code Assembler (pasm) Syntax

### 5.1 Source Format

```
; Comment (semicolon to end of line)

.const UART_DATA  -65280      ; named constant
.const UART_STATUS -65279
.const LED_PORT   -65024

.global counter 1             ; 1 word of global storage
.global buffer 32             ; 32 words

.data msg 72, 101, 108, 108, 111, 0   ; "Hello\0" as bytes

.proc main 0                 ; procedure, 0 locals
    push msg                  ; push address of msg
    call puts
    halt
.end

.proc puts 1                 ; 1 local (loop pointer)
    loada 0                   ; arg0 = string address
    storel 0                  ; local0 = current ptr
loop:
    loadl 0
    loadb                     ; load byte at ptr
    dup
    jz done                   ; if zero, done
    sys 1                     ; PUTC
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0                  ; ptr++
    jmp loop
done:
    drop                      ; drop the zero
    ret 1                     ; return, clean 1 arg
.end
```

### 5.2 Directives

| Directive | Syntax | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| `.const` | `.const NAME value` | Define named constant |
| `.global` | `.global NAME nwords` | Reserve global storage |
| `.data` | `.data NAME byte, byte, ...` | Define byte data (string/array) |
| `.proc` | `.proc NAME nlocals` | Begin procedure (nlocals = local slot count) |
| `.end` | `.end` | End procedure |

### 5.3 Labels

Labels appear on their own line, ending with `:`:

```
loop:
    ; instructions...
    jmp loop
```

Labels are local to the enclosing `.proc` / `.end` block.

### 5.4 Integer Literals

- Decimal: `42`, `-1`, `0`
- Hex: `0xFF` (if supported) or decimal equivalents
- Named constants: `UART_DATA` (substituted by assembler)

### 5.5 Instructions

One instruction per line. Operand follows mnemonic separated by whitespace:

```
push 42
loadl 0
jz done
call puts
sys 1
ret 0
```

## 6. Opcode Encoding Table

Opcodes are assigned sequential byte values:

| Byte | Mnemonic | Operand |
|------|----------|---------|
| 0x00 | halt | — |
| 0x01 | push | imm24 |
| 0x02 | push_s | imm8 |
| 0x03 | dup | — |
| 0x04 | drop | — |
| 0x05 | swap | — |
| 0x06 | over | — |
| 0x10 | add | — |
| 0x11 | sub | — |
| 0x12 | mul | — |
| 0x13 | div | — |
| 0x14 | mod | — |
| 0x15 | neg | — |
| 0x16 | and | — |
| 0x17 | or | — |
| 0x18 | xor | — |
| 0x19 | not | — |
| 0x1A | shl | — |
| 0x1B | shr | — |
| 0x20 | eq | — |
| 0x21 | ne | — |
| 0x22 | lt | — |
| 0x23 | le | — |
| 0x24 | gt | — |
| 0x25 | ge | — |
| 0x30 | jmp | addr24 |
| 0x31 | jz | addr24 |
| 0x32 | jnz | addr24 |
| 0x33 | call | addr24 |
| 0x34 | ret | nargs8 |
| 0x35 | trap | code8 |
| 0x40 | enter | nlocals8 |
| 0x41 | leave | — |
| 0x42 | loadl | off8 |
| 0x43 | storel | off8 |
| 0x44 | loadg | off24 |
| 0x45 | storeg | off24 |
| 0x46 | addrl | off8 |
| 0x47 | addrg | off24 |
| 0x48 | loada | idx8 |
| 0x49 | storea | idx8 |
| 0x4A | loadn | depth8 off8 |
| 0x4B | storen | depth8 off8 |
| 0x50 | load | — |
| 0x51 | store | — |
| 0x52 | loadb | — |
| 0x53 | storeb | — |
| 0x60 | sys | id8 |

Reserved ranges 0x70–0xFF for future opcodes (heap, debug, extended ops).

## 7. Trap Codes

| Code | Name | Trigger |
|------|------|---------|
| 0 | USER_TRAP | `trap 0` instruction |
| 1 | DIV_ZERO | Division/modulo by zero |
| 2 | STACK_OVERFLOW | Eval or call stack exceeds limit |
| 3 | STACK_UNDERFLOW | Eval stack pop when empty |
| 4 | INVALID_OPCODE | Unknown opcode byte |
| 5 | INVALID_ADDRESS | Memory access out of bounds |
| 6 | NIL_POINTER | Dereference of address 0 |
| 7 | BOUNDS_CHECK | Array index out of bounds |

On trap: VM sets status=2, trap_code=N, halts. Diagnostic message to UART.

## 8. Memory Segment Layout

At VM initialization:

1. **Code segment** starts at a fixed base address (after VM code)
2. **Constants/data** follows code (emitted by pasm)
3. **Globals** follow data (allocated per `.global` directives)
4. **Call stack** starts after globals, grows upward
5. **Eval stack** starts after call stack region, grows upward
6. **Heap** starts after eval stack region, grows upward
7. Segment base addresses stored in VM state struct

Sizes are configurable at VM init time. Default allocations:
- Call stack: 4 KB
- Eval stack: 2 KB
- Heap: remaining SRAM up to ~900 KB

## 9. Implementation Strategy

### Phase 1: Minimal VM
- Opcode dispatch loop
- push, dup, drop, add, sub, halt
- UART output via sys PUTC
- Hardcoded test bytecode (no pasm yet)

### Phase 2: Control Flow + Procedures
- jmp, jz, jnz
- call, ret, enter, leave
- loadl, storel, loada, storea
- Frame management

### Phase 3: Full Instruction Set
- All arithmetic/logic/comparison opcodes
- Globals (loadg, storeg)
- Indirect memory (load, store, loadb, storeb)
- Nonlocal access (loadn, storen)
- All sys calls

### Phase 4: P-Code Assembler
- Lexer for pasm syntax (UART input)
- Two-pass assembly (collect labels, emit bytes)
- Directive handling (.const, .global, .data, .proc/.end)
- Output bytecode to memory buffer

### Phase 5: Integration & Test Suite
- pasm assembles test programs
- VM executes assembled bytecode
- Regression test harness (demo.sh)
- Full test coverage per staged plan

## 10. COR24 Assembly Patterns

Reference patterns from tf24a for common idioms:

### Dispatch Table
```asm
; Index into jump table by opcode byte
; r0 = opcode
la r2, dispatch_table
; multiply r0 by 3 (entry size = .word = 3 bytes)
; ... load target address, jmp
```

### UART Output
```asm
; r0 = character to send
la r2, -65280       ; UART base
tx_wait:
    lb r0, 1(r2)   ; status (sign-extended)
    cls r0, z       ; C = (status < 0) = TX busy
    brt tx_wait
    sb r0, 0(r2)   ; write byte
```

### Memory Struct Access
```asm
; Load vm_state.pc into r0
la fp, vm_state
lw r0, 0(fp)       ; pc at offset 0
```

input.command:
agentrail complete \
  --summary "Project initialized with docs/prd.md, docs/architecture.md, docs/design.md, and agentrail saga. Research from docs/research.txt distilled into concrete VM design with ~35 opcodes, dual-stack architecture, pasm assembler syntax, calling convention, and memory layout." \
  --next-slug vm-scaffold \
  --next-prompt "Create pvm.s scaffold: COR24 entry point, VM state struct (pc, esp, csp, fp, gp, hp, code, status, trap_code), memory segment initialization (code/globals/call-stack/eval-stack/heap base addresses and limits), and the main fetch-decode-execute loop skeleton (fetch opcode byte from code[pc], pc++, dispatch via jump table to opcode handlers — stub all handlers as halt-with-unknown-opcode for now). Include UART output helper (tx_wait polling loop at 0xFF0100/0xFF0101) for diagnostic printing. Test: cor24-run --run pvm.s --speed 0 should assemble clean and halt immediately. Reference tf24a/forth.s for COR24 idioms (label-on-own-line, .byte/.word, register constraints, UART polling pattern)." \
  --next-role production \
  --next-task-type cor24-asm \
  --planned "vm-stack-ops: Implement push/push_s/dup/drop/swap/over opcode handlers and sys PUTC (opcode 0x60 with id=1). Test by embedding hardcoded bytecode: push_s 72, sys 1, push_s 10, sys 1, halt — should print H-newline to UART." \
  --planned "vm-arithmetic: Implement add/sub/mul/div/mod/neg/and/or/xor/not/shl/shr and eq/ne/lt/le/gt/ge opcode handlers. Test with hardcoded bytecode: push 7, push 6, mul, sys PUTC should output ASCII '*' (42)." \
  --planned "vm-control-flow: Implement jmp/jz/jnz opcode handlers. Test with hardcoded countdown loop: push 5, loop: dup, jz done, push_s 1, sub, jmp loop, done: halt." \
  --planned "vm-frames: Implement call/ret/enter/leave and loadl/storel/loada/storea opcode handlers. Build frame management: push/pop activation records on call stack, argument passing from eval stack to frame. Test with hardcoded bytecode for a factorial function." \
  --planned "vm-memory: Implement loadg/storeg/addrg/addrl and indirect load/store/loadb/storeb. Implement globals segment. Test with hardcoded bytecode: global counter, store/load/print." \
  --planned "vm-nonlocal: Implement loadn/storen for static link chain traversal (needed for Pascal nested procedures). Test with hardcoded 2-level nested procedure accessing outer local." \
  --planned "vm-syscalls: Implement remaining sys calls: HALT(0), GETC(2), LED(3), ALLOC(4), FREE(5). Bump allocator for heap. Test: LED toggle, UART echo, heap alloc/write/read." \
  --planned "vm-traps: Implement trap handling for all trap codes (div-zero, stack overflow/underflow, invalid opcode, invalid address, nil pointer). Print diagnostic to UART on trap. Test each trap condition." \
  --planned "pasm-lexer: Create pasm.s — p-code assembler in COR24 assembly. Start with lexer: read pasm source from UART, tokenize into token types (mnemonic, number, label, directive, newline, comment, eof). Use a token buffer in memory. Test by feeding simple pasm source and dumping token stream." \
  --planned "pasm-parser: Implement pasm parser: two-pass assembly. Pass 1: collect labels and procedure addresses, count globals/data sizes. Pass 2: emit bytecode. Handle .const/.global/.data/.proc/.end directives. Test by assembling a minimal program (push 42, halt) and verifying bytecode bytes." \
  --planned "pasm-integration: Connect pasm output to VM input. Assemble pasm source into bytecode buffer, then execute. Test with hello.pasm (full puts procedure, data string, main calling puts). First end-to-end test." \
  --planned "test-suite: Create full test suite: tests/t01-arith.pasm through t10-traps.pasm. Create demo.sh test harness with golden output comparison (following tf24a pattern). Create examples/hello.pasm, examples/blink.pasm, examples/echo.pasm." \
  --planned "hardening: Fix bugs found by test suite. Add stack depth checking. Validate memory bounds. Clean up diagnostic output. Ensure all tests pass. Final documentation update."
